```diff
/* Vertical run wrapper. Applied via the "Vertical Mode" toolbar button to
   either the user's selection or to consecutive hieroglyph runs (`auto-hilo`).
   Keeps surrounding text horizontal while letting hieroglyphs read top-to-bottom. */
.editor-content .vertical-run {
  writing-mode: vertical-lr;
  text-orientation: upright;
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: top;
  white-space: nowrap;
}
```

## [40] assistant

Done. Here's what changed and how the editor's "Vertical Mode" now behaves:

**New behavior:**
- **With a text selection**: clicking the button wraps just that selection in a `<span class="vertical-run">` (vertical writing mode + upright text). Clicking again with the same selection unwraps it. So you can pick exactly the hieroglyph runs you want vertical without affecting English text around them.
- **With no selection**: clicking the button auto-wraps every consecutive run of `.svg-icon` siblings (your hieroglyphs) in `vertical-run.auto-hilo` spans. English/Arabic/etc. paragraphs stay horizontal. Clicking again removes those auto wrappers.

**What I removed:**
- The editor root no longer sets `writing-mode: vertical-lr` / `text-orientation: upright` on the whole `.editor-content` element, which is what was forcing English to render 1 char per line.
- `whiteSpace: nowrap` is no longer toggled on the whole editor either.

**Files touched:**
- `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:
  - Replaced `getEditorStyles()` so it always returns horizontal flow.
  - Replaced `toggleColumnMode()` with a selection-aware version, plus helpers `findEnclosingVerticalRun`, `unwrapElement`, `toggleVerticalForSelection`, `wrapAllHieroglyphRuns`, `unwrapAllAutoHilo`, and `VERTICAL_RUN_CSS`.
  - `whiteSpace` on the editor div is now always `pre-wrap`.
  - `columnMode` state is preserved (still drives cartouche/merged-icon orientation logic).
- `src/components/writing/content/AssistantBar.tsx`: updated tooltip + active-state label to reflect the new behavior.
- `src/index.css`: added a `.editor-content .vertical-run` rule as a fallback so the orientation survives even if inline styles ever get sanitized.

**Secondary issue (selecting hieroglyphs is buggy):** I left this for a follow-up since you marked it lower priority. When you want to tackle it, the likely culprits are in `handleEditorMouseDown`/`handleEditorMouseMove`/`handleEditorMouseUp` and the `contentEditable="false"` icons interacting with `Range`/`Selection`. Want me to dig in next?

## [42] assistant

Right — after wrapping, the cursor was being placed inside the vertical-run span, so subsequent Enter/typing happened in vertical writing mode (which is why it looked like text was going "upwards"). And since the wrapper is `inline-block`, edits there can also push around the hieroglyphs already inside.

Let me fix this by moving the caret to right after the wrapper once we're done wrapping, so further typing flows normally.

 I see the issue — when the user wraps hieroglyphs in a vertical-run span, the cursor ends up positioned inside that span, so pressing Enter creates new content that's also trapped within the vertical styling, causing the text to flow upward and overwrite the original hieroglyphs.

The solution is to move the cursor outside the wrapper after wrapping completes, so subsequent typing happens in normal horizontal flow. I need to update both the selection-based wrapping and the auto-wrap function to collapse the selection right after the wrapper element instead of leaving it inside. For the auto-wrap case, I should also consider where the cursor was before wrapping and restore it to a sensible position afterward.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
    try {
      const fragment = range.extractContents();
      // Strip nested vertical-run wrappers from the fragment so we don't
      // produce nested vertical sections that flip orientation back.
      fragment
        .querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>("span.vertical-run")
        .forEach((nested) => unwrapElement(nested));

      if (!fragment.firstChild) return false;

      const wrapper = document.createElement("span");
      wrapper.className = "vertical-run";
      wrapper.setAttribute("style", VERTICAL_RUN_CSS);
      wrapper.appendChild(fragment);
      range.insertNode(wrapper);

      const sel = window.getSelection();
      if (sel) {
        const newRange = document.createRange();
        newRange.selectNodeContents(wrapper);
        sel.removeAllRanges();
        sel.addRange(newRange);
      }
      return true;
    } catch {
      return false;
    }
  };
- NEW ---
    try {
      const fragment = range.extractContents();
      // Strip nested vertical-run wrappers from the fragment so we don't
      // produce nested vertical sections that flip orientation back.
      fragment
        .querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>("span.vertical-run")
        .forEach((nested) => unwrapElement(nested));

      if (!fragment.firstChild) return false;

      const wrapper = document.createElement("span");
      wrapper.className = "vertical-run";
      wrapper.setAttribute("style", VERTICAL_RUN_CSS);
      wrapper.appendChild(fragment);
      range.insertNode(wrapper);

      // Place the caret immediately after the wrapper so subsequent typing
      // (Enter, plain text, etc.) happens in normal horizontal flow rather
      // than inside the vertical-run, which would render text upright and
      // visually overwrite the hieroglyphs already inside.
      placeCaretAfter(wrapper);
      return true;
    } catch {
      return false;
    }
  };
```

## [43] assistant

Add the `placeCaretAfter` helper and use it in the auto-wrap path too:

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  const unwrapElement = (el: HTMLElement) => {
    const parent = el.parentNode;
    if (!parent) return;
    while (el.firstChild) parent.insertBefore(el.firstChild, el);
    parent.removeChild(el);

- NEW ---
  const unwrapElement = (el: HTMLElement) => {
    const parent = el.parentNode;
    if (!parent) return;
    while (el.firstChild) parent.insertBefore(el.firstChild, el);
    parent.removeChild(el);
  };

  const placeCaretAfter = (node: Node) => {
    const sel = window.getSelection();
    if (!sel) return;
    const range = document.createRange();
    range.setStartAfter(node);
    range.collapse(true);
    sel.removeAllRanges();
    sel.addRange(range);
    savedRangeRef.current = range.cloneRange();
  };
```

```diff
  // Wrap every consecutive run of `.svg-icon` siblings in the editor with a
  // `.vertical-run.auto-hilo` span. Whitespace between icons is included so
  // the visual grouping is preserved.
  const wrapAllHieroglyphRuns = () => {
    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return;

    const wrapRunsIn = (parent: Element) => {
      const children = Array.from(parent.childNodes);
      let i = 0;
      while (i < children.length) {
        const child = children[i];
        if (
          child instanceof HTMLElement &&
          child.classList.contains("svg-icon") &&
          !child.closest(".vertical-run")
        ) {
          const runNodes: Node[] = [child];
          let j = i + 1;
          while (j < children.length) {
            const next = children[j];
            if (
              next instanceof HTMLElement &&
              next.classList.contains("svg-icon")
            ) {
              runNodes.push(next);
              j++;
              continue;
            }
            if (
              next.nodeType === Node.TEXT_NODE &&
              (next.textContent || "").trim() === ""
            ) {
              runNodes.push(next);
              j++;
              continue;
            }
            break;
          }

          const wrapper = document.createElement("span");
          wrapper.className = "vertical-run auto-hilo";
```

```diff
    // Pressing Enter while the caret is inside a `.vertical-run` would
    // otherwise insert a line break in vertical writing mode, which renders
    // upright and visually overlaps the hieroglyphs in the run. Escape the
    // run first, then insert the line break at the new (horizontal) location.
    if (e.key === "Enter" && range.collapsed) {
      const enclosing = findEnclosingVerticalRun(range.startContainer, editor);
      if (enclosing) {
        e.preventDefault();
        placeCaretAfter(enclosing);
        const refreshed = getSelectionInEditor(editor);
        if (refreshed) {
          clearEmptyTypingSpanAtSelection();
          insertLineBreakAtRange(editor, refreshed.range);
        }
        return;
      }
    }

    if (e.key === "Enter" && li && isListItemEmpty(li)) {
      e.preventDefault();
      document.execCommand("outdent");

      const after = getSelectionInEditor(editor);
      if (after) {
        const stillLi = getClosestListItem(after.range.startContainer);
        if (stillLi) {
          document.execCommand("insertParagraph");
          document.execCommand("outdent");
        }
      }
      resetTypingHistorySession();
      commitHistory("push");
      return;
    }

    if (e.key === "Enter" && !li) {
      e.preventDefault();
      clearEmptyTypingSpanAtSelection();
      insertLineBreakAtRange(editor, range);
    }
  };
```

## [55] assistant

Clean. Here's what I fixed:

**Root cause:** After wrapping content in the `.vertical-run` span, the caret was being placed *inside* that wrapper. Since the wrapper has `writing-mode: vertical-lr; text-orientation: upright`, anything you typed (including line breaks from Enter) was rendered upright/stacked, making it look like text was being typed "upwards" — and because the wrapper is `inline-block`, the new content also pushed the existing hieroglyphs around.

**Fixes in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:**

1. New `placeCaretAfter(node)` helper that collapses the selection to right after a node and updates `savedRangeRef`.
2. `toggleVerticalForSelection` now calls `placeCaretAfter(wrapper)` after wrapping the selection, so the caret ends up *outside* the vertical-run.
3. `wrapAllHieroglyphRuns` now returns the last wrapper it created, and `toggleColumnMode` uses that to park the caret right after the last vertical run when the auto-hilo path runs.
4. In `handleEditorKeyDown`, added an Enter-escape: if the caret is inside a `.vertical-run` when Enter is pressed, the caret is moved to right after the run and the line break is inserted there. So Enter inside a vertical run cleanly exits it instead of inserting a vertical line break that visually corrupts the hieroglyphs.

Now: insert hieroglyphs → click Vertical Mode → press Enter → cursor lands on a fresh horizontal line below the vertical run, and your typed text flows normally without disturbing the glyphs.

---

## [61] assistant

Found it. After Enter, the editor inserts a `<span data-editor-line-spacer>` containing a ZWSP, and parks the caret inside that span. When you type the first character, the span now contains `\u200B` + your character. Then `normalizeEditorArtifacts` (called from `handleEditorInput`) does `spacer.replaceWith(document.createTextNode(text))` — which destroys the text node the caret was anchored to. The browser falls back to placing the caret at the *start* of the replacement text, so further characters get inserted before your first typed character.

Fix: preserve caret position when promoting a spacer to plain text. I'll do it in-place (strip the ZWSP from the existing text node, then unwrap the span), so the caret keeps its text-node anchor.

 I'm tracing through the text insertion flow when a user presses Enter and then types a character. When Enter is pressed, the editor inserts a line break element and a spacer span with a zero-width space, positioning the caret after that invisible character. Then when the user types "h", it gets inserted at that caret position.

The problem emerges in `handleEditorInput` when `normalizeEditorArtifacts` runs. It processes the spacer span, strips out the zero-width space, and replaces the entire span with a plain text node containing just "h". This destroys the original text node where the caret was anchored, causing the caret to lose its position. Then the next normalization loop checks if the line has real content and decides whether to keep or remove spacers based on what the line starts with.

Since the new text node "h" isn't a non-editable element, it removes any remaining spacers. The caret likely gets repositioned by the browser to somewhere around the new text node, but not necessarily where the user expects—possibly at the start rather than the end. This explains why subsequent keystrokes get inserted in the wrong place, producing "elloh" instead of "hello".

The root cause is that `replaceWith` creates a fresh text node, severing the caret's anchor to the original node. I can fix this either by restoring the caret position explicitly after replacement, or more elegantly by modifying the existing text node in place—removing the zero-width space and the spacer attribute without creating a new node, so the caret stays anchored where it belongs. Implementing the caret preservation fix... Restoring caret position...

I realize the formula I had assumes all ZWSPs are before the caret, but that's not always true—the browser might insert text before the ZWSP instead. I should count only the zero-width spaces that actually appear before the caret position and subtract just those from the offset. This way, if the ZWSP ends up after the inserted character, it won't incorrectly shift the caret backward.

Now I'm weighing whether to even apply this normalization at all, since it adds complexity and could introduce edge cases.

## [66] assistant

Clean. Quick recap of the bug and fix:

**Bug:** Pressing Enter inserts a `<span data-editor-line-spacer>` containing a zero-width space (`\u200B`) so the caret has somewhere to land on the empty new line. The first keystroke goes into that span, so it now reads e.g. `\u200Bh`. On the very next `input` event, `normalizeEditorArtifacts` saw the span had real text and ran `spacer.replaceWith(document.createTextNode(text))`. That destroyed the text node the caret was anchored to, so the browser fell back to placing the caret at the *start* of the new text node — pushing every subsequent character before the first one. Typing "hello" came out "elloh".

**Fix in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx` (`normalizeEditorArtifacts`):** when promoting a spacer span that now has real characters, instead of replacing it with a brand-new text node:
1. Capture the current selection's anchor node + offset if it's inside the spacer.
2. Walk the spacer's text nodes and strip ZWSPs in-place (mutating `text.data`), and adjust the caret offset by however many ZWSPs were before the caret.
3. Unwrap the spacer span by moving its existing children to the parent and removing the span.
4. Restore the selection on the same text node it was originally anchored to, with the adjusted offset.

Now the same text node survives the normalization, so the caret stays exactly where you typed and "hello" comes out as "hello".

Now patch the horizontal branch math:

```diff
- OLD ---
      // Shrink-to-content height (no vertical whitespace) and never exceed current iconSize.
      const heightCap = Math.min(90, iconSize);
      const desiredH = Math.min(targetH, heightCap);

      const rawWidths = aspectRatios.map((ar) => desiredH * ar);
      const totalW = rawWidths.reduce((s, w) => s + w, 0);
      const scale = totalW > targetW ? targetW / totalW : 1;
      const finalH = desiredH * scale;
      const contentW = totalW * scale;
      const offsetX = (targetW - contentW) / 2;
      const offsetY = 0;

      wrapperW = targetW;
      wrapperH = Math.max(1, Math.round(finalH));

      let cursor = offsetX;
      slotDims = rawWidths.map((rw) => {

- NEW ---
      // Shrink-to-content height (no vertical whitespace) and never exceed current iconSize.
      const heightCap = Math.min(90, iconSize);
      const desiredH = Math.min(targetH, heightCap);

      const rawWidths = aspectRatios.map((ar) => desiredH * ar);
      const totalW = rawWidths.reduce((s, w) => s + w, 0);
      const totalGapsW = (n - 1) * INTERNAL_GAP;
      const usableW = Math.max(1, targetW - totalGapsW);
      const scale = totalW > usableW ? usableW / totalW : 1;
      const finalH = desiredH * scale;
      const contentW = totalW * scale + totalGapsW;
      const offsetX = (targetW - contentW) / 2;
      const offsetY = 0;

      wrapperW = targetW;
      wrapperH = Math.max(1, Math.round(finalH));

      let cursor = offsetX;
      slotDims = rawWidths.map((rw) => {
        const sw = rw * scale;
        const dim = { w: sw, h: finalH, left: cursor, top: offsetY };
        cursor += sw + INTERNAL_GAP;
        return dim;
      });
```

```diff
- OLD ---
      const slotH0 = iconSize / n;
      const rawWidths = aspectRatios.map((ar) => slotH0 * ar);
      const maxRw = Math.max(...rawWidths, 1);
      const wScale = maxRw > iconSize ? iconSize / maxRw : 1;
      let slotH = slotH0 * wScale;
      let scaledWidths = rawWidths.map((w) => w * wScale);
      wrapperW = iconSize;
      wrapperH = n * slotH;

      if (wrapperH > MAX_GROUP_H) {
        const hScale = MAX_GROUP_H / wrapperH;
        slotH *= hScale;
        scaledWidths = scaledWidths.map((w) => w * hScale);
        wrapperW = Math.min(Math.round(iconSize * hScale), maxComboW);
        wrapperH = MAX_GROUP_H;
      }

      slotDims = scaledWidths.map((slotW, i) => ({
        w: slotW,
        h: slotH,
        left: (wrapperW - slotW) / 2,
        top: i * slotH,
      }));
- NEW ---
      const totalGapsH = (n - 1) * INTERNAL_GAP;
      const slotH0 = Math.max(1, (iconSize - totalGapsH) / n);
      const rawWidths = aspectRatios.map((ar) => slotH0 * ar);
      const maxRw = Math.max(...rawWidths, 1);
      const wScale = maxRw > iconSize ? iconSize / maxRw : 1;
      let slotH = slotH0 * wScale;
      let gapH = INTERNAL_GAP * wScale;
      let scaledWidths = rawWidths.map((w) => w * wScale);
      wrapperW = iconSize;
      wrapperH = n * slotH + (n - 1) * gapH;

      if (wrapperH > MAX_GROUP_H) {
        const hScale = MAX_GROUP_H / wrapperH;
        slotH *= hScale;
        gapH *= hScale;
        scaledWidths = scaledWidths.map((w) => w * hScale);
        wrapperW = Math.min(Math.round(iconSize * hScale), maxComboW);
        wrapperH = MAX_GROUP_H;
      }

      slotDims = scaledWidths.map((slotW, i) => ({
        w: slotW,
        h: slotH,
        left: (wrapperW - slotW) / 2,
        top: i * (slotH + gapH),
      }));
```

## [87] assistant

Now add a defensive CSS rule so SVGs inside a `.vertical-run` always render with normal (horizontal) writing mode internally — only the outer flow should be vertical:

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/index.css`
```diff
- OLD ---
/* Vertical run wrapper. Applied via the "Vertical Mode" toolbar button to
   either the user's selection or to consecutive hieroglyph runs (`auto-hilo`).
   Keeps surrounding text horizontal while letting hieroglyphs read top-to-bottom. */
.editor-content .vertical-run {
  writing-mode: vertical-lr;
  text-orientation: upright;
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: top;
  white-space: nowrap;
}
- NEW ---
/* Vertical run wrapper. Applied via the "Vertical Mode" toolbar button to
   either the user's selection or to consecutive hieroglyph runs (`auto-hilo`).
   Keeps surrounding text horizontal while letting hieroglyphs read top-to-bottom. */
.editor-content .vertical-run {
  writing-mode: vertical-lr;
  text-orientation: upright;
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: top;
  white-space: nowrap;
}

/* Glyphs themselves stay rendered in their normal horizontal coordinate
   system; only the *layout* of the run is vertical. This prevents inherited
   writing-mode from rotating the SVG content inside each glyph. */
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon,
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon svg {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}
```

## [89] assistant

Clean. Summary of what changed for the spacing pass — `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx` and `src/index.css`:

| Change | Before | After | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standalone glyph wrapper margin (`createSvgWrapper`) | `4px 1px` | `4px 3px` | ~6px between adjacent glyphs in horizontal flow; ~8px when stacked in `.vertical-run` (from physical top/bottom margins). |
| Magicbox merged wrapper margin | `4px 1px` | `4px 3px` | Consistent spacing with regular glyphs. |
| Cartouche outer margin | `0 1px 0 0` | `4px 3px` | Cartouches no longer hug the next thing. |
| Cartouche internal slot gap | `gap: 2px` | `gap: 3px` | Slight breathing room inside cartouches. |
| `createMergedIcon` horizontal slot layout | slots flush, `cursor += sw` | `cursor += sw + 2`, scale fits with `(n-1)*2` reserved | 2px gap between glyphs in a horizontal group. |
| `createMergedIcon` vertical slot layout | `top: i * slotH`, no gap | `top: i * (slotH + gap)`, gap proportionally scaled when capped at 90px | 2px gap between glyphs in a vertical group. |
| `.vertical-run` children CSS | (none) | force `writing-mode: horizontal-tb` on `.svg-icon` and inner `<svg>` | Defensive: keeps the glyph artwork upright/normal even though the run's inline axis is vertical. |

How it'll look:
- Type a sequence of glyphs → ~6px horizontal gap between each, instead of glued.
- Group glyphs (vertically by default, horizontally in column mode) → ~2px gap between the slots inside the compound, so you can still read it as a group but they don't overlap.
- Cartouches → spaced from neighbors and slightly looser inside.
- Vertical run (the new vertical-mode wrapper) → glyphs stack with ~8px between them.

Note: I haven't touched the MagicBox-built composite (the modal-based custom layout) yet — its spacing is computed inside `MagicBox.tsx`. If the client cares about that one too, say the word and we'll add an `INTERNAL_GAP` there as well.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
    const gap = iconsToWrap.length > 1 ? (iconsToWrap.length - 1) * 2 : 0;
    let cartoucheWidth: number;
    let cartoucheHeight: number;

    if (isVertical) {
      cartoucheWidth = maxW || iconSize;
      cartoucheHeight = Math.max(totalH + gap, cartoucheWidth * 0.875);
    } else {
      cartoucheHeight = maxH || iconSize;
      cartoucheWidth = Math.max(totalW + gap, cartoucheHeight * 0.875);
    }
- NEW ---
    const gap = iconsToWrap.length > 1 ? (iconsToWrap.length - 1) * 2 : 0;
    let cartoucheWidth: number;
    let cartoucheHeight: number;

    // Frame padding so that glyphs don't poke past the cartouche outline.
    // PAD_LONG accounts for the curved end at the long axis of the cartouche
    // (where the curve cuts in toward the glyphs). PAD_PERP is the smaller
    // padding from the flat side(s). Both are anchored to the *perpendicular*
    // content dimension so the padding scales with glyph size.
    const refPerp = isVertical ? maxW || iconSize : maxH || iconSize;
    const PAD_LONG = Math.max(4, Math.round(refPerp * 0.18));
    const PAD_PERP = Math.max(2, Math.round(refPerp * 0.08));

    if (isVertical) {
      const innerW = maxW || iconSize;
      const innerH = Math.max(totalH + gap, innerW * 0.875);
      cartoucheWidth = innerW + 2 * PAD_PERP;
      cartoucheHeight = innerH + 2 * PAD_LONG;
    } else {
      const innerH = maxH || iconSize;
      const innerW = Math.max(totalW + gap, innerH * 0.875);
      cartoucheHeight = innerH + 2 * PAD_PERP;
      cartoucheWidth = innerW + 2 * PAD_LONG;
    }
```

```diff
          // that flipping vertical/horizontal preserves the same frame
          // padding around the glyphs.
          const refPerp = shouldBeVertical
            ? maxW || baseSize
            : maxH || baseSize;
          const PAD_LONG = Math.max(4, Math.round(refPerp * 0.18));
          const PAD_PERP = Math.max(2, Math.round(refPerp * 0.08));

          let newH: number;

            const innerW = Math.max(totalW + gap, innerH * 0.875);
            newH = innerH + 2 * PAD_PERP;
            newW = innerW + 2 * PAD_LONG;
```

## [99] assistant

Clean. Quick summary of what I did and why:

**The bug:** The cartouche frame is drawn inside a `cartoucheWidth × cartoucheHeight` SVG, but the `iconsContainer` that holds the glyphs also fills 100% of that same area. The frame has curved ends (the curve "anchor" sits at ~42% of the perpendicular dim from each corner) and 2% top/bottom inset lines. Glyphs were sized down with `fitScale = 0.86`, so they had ~7% slack on each side — but at the corners the frame curves *cut in further than 7%*, so glyph corners ended up outside the curve.

**The fix:** I left `fitScale` and the per-glyph sizing alone, and instead enlarged the cartouche outer dimensions to add explicit frame padding around the content. Two small constants:

- `PAD_LONG = max(4, round(refPerp * 0.18))` — padding along the curved (long) axis on each end
- `PAD_PERP = max(2, round(refPerp * 0.08))` — padding along the flat (perpendicular) axis on each side

Anchored to the perpendicular content dimension (`maxH` for horizontal cartouches, `maxW` for vertical) so the padding scales naturally with glyph size.

Applied in two places so behavior is consistent:

1. `wrapInCartouche` (initial creation) — computes inner dims first, then adds `2 * PAD_*` for the outer cartouche.
2. `updateAllIconTransforms` (when toggling vertical/horizontal on an existing cartouche) — same formula, so flipping a cartouche keeps the same padding.

Visually:
- Glyphs centered inside the cartouche end up clearly inside the curved oval frame (the frame doesn't clip into them at the corners anymore).
- The end bar (the vertical line at the right end of a horizontal cartouche, or bottom of vertical) is now well past the rightmost/bottommost glyph.
- For tiny cartouches (1-2 small glyphs), `Math.max(4, ...)` and `Math.max(2, ...)` ensure a minimum visible padding even when content is tiny.

If after looking at it the client wants tighter or looser padding, the two numbers `0.18` and `0.08` are the only knobs. Easy tuning.

Ready for the next one.

## [119] assistant

Clean. Here's what I changed and why:

**The bug:** Three places (the merged-group wrapper, the MagicBox merged-group, and the cartouche wrapper) were created with three settings that fundamentally fight against text-style selection:

```diff
- draggable = true       → mousedown + drag triggers HTML5 native drag instead of selection
- user-select: none      → browser refuses to include the element in any text selection
- cursor: move           → tells the user "this is a draggable atom, not text"
```

That's why selection felt like garbage specifically *around* groups and cartouches — the browser was actively refusing to put them in a range. Single regular glyphs (`createSvgWrapper`) didn't have these settings, which is why they "kind of" worked.

**The fix:** All three wrappers now look like this:

```diff
- mergedWrapper.draggable = true
+ mergedWrapper.draggable = false
- cursor: move
+ cursor: text
- user-select: none
  (removed)
```

So now, on merged groups and cartouches:
- Mousedown + drag starts a normal text selection (the browser treats them as atomic units within the range, just like a regular glyph or an emoji).
- Hovering shows the I-beam cursor — they feel like text.
- They get included in selection ranges, so dragging across them no longer feels like "jumping a void".
- The native browser highlight (translucent blue overlay) will color them while selected, giving you the "smooth like text" visual feedback.
- Click on a glyph still toggles it in `selectedIcons` (the custom selection used by the Group / Cartouche / Magic Box buttons) — that behavior is unchanged, and the buttons still also fall back to the native selection range when no icons are explicitly selected, so both flows work.

**Trade-off (deliberate):** This disables the in-editor "drag this glyph to a new position" gesture for groups and cartouches. Reasoning:
1. You said selection smoothness is the priority, and these two gestures are mutually exclusive without a custom drag-handle UI.
2. The user can still cut → click new spot → paste, which is what most editors expect anyway.
3. Drag *into* the editor from the palette still works (handled by `handleEditorDrop`, which only consumes `dragSourceRef === "palette"`).
4. We can re-introduce drag-to-reorder with a small grab handle (e.g. a tiny dot that appears on hover) later, without taking selection back. I left the existing `ondragstart` handlers in place as harmless dead code so we don't have to wire them back up if we do add a handle.

**What I didn't change (yet):**
- I didn't touch the click-on-glyph behavior — single click still toggles into `selectedIcons`. If after testing you want clicks to *just place the caret like normal text* (and reserve glyph-picking for shift+click or drag), say the word and it's a 5-line change.
- I didn't add custom CSS for the selection highlight color — modern Chrome/Firefox already overlay non-editable elements when they fall in a range. If the highlight isn't visible enough on the client's machine, easy to tune.

Try it out — drag-select across a row that mixes text, glyphs, groups, and cartouches. Should feel like normal text now. Let me know what's next or if any of this needs adjustment.

 Adding selection styles...

Actually, I'll skip that for now and see how it looks to the user first — we can always refine it later.

## [124] assistant

Now I understand the real issue clearly. Let me explain my plan, then implement.

**The three actual problems you described:**

1. **Click on a glyph selects the glyph instead of placing the caret.** That's my last commit's icon-toggle behavior I left in. Killing it for plain/merged glyphs.

2. **Drag-selection lags by ~half a glyph width.** This is browser default: the caret position only flips to the *other side* of an inline-block atom once the cursor crosses its midpoint. For 35–40px glyphs that means dragging ~20px past the boundary before the glyph is included. Fix: intercept `mousemove` during a drag, and if the cursor is inside a glyph's bounding box, force the selection focus to the *far edge* of that glyph (so it gets included the instant the cursor enters its box, not at its center).

3. **Caret not visible between glyphs.** Genuine browser issue with adjacent atomic inline-blocks and no text node between them. I'll table this as a separate fix once the selection feel is solid — let's get the big two right first and then tackle caret visibility.

Implementing now:

 Now I'm ready to implement the changes—I need to modify the click handler to skip icon toggling for non-cartouche glyphs while preserving cartouche behavior, add drag-snap logic to the mouse move handler, and make sure selectedIcons clears when dragging starts.

## [127] assistant

Clean. Here's exactly what changes you should feel:

**1. Click on a glyph = caret, like text** (`handleEditorClick`)
- Before: clicking a regular or merged glyph called `e.preventDefault()` and toggled it into `selectedIcons`. So clicking a glyph "selected" it instead of placing the caret.
- After: clicking a regular or merged glyph drops any lingering icon-selection state and *lets the browser place the caret naturally* (no `preventDefault`). Behaves exactly like clicking inside a paragraph of text.
- Cartouches still keep their inner-glyph click-to-toggle behavior — that's the only way to grab one specific glyph inside a cartouche to resize/customize it. Single-glyph ops on regular glyphs go through drag-selection now.

**2. Drag-selection that tracks the cursor pixel-by-pixel** (`handleEditorMouseMove`)

This is the meat of the fix. Inside `mousemove` while a drag is active:

```ts
// hovered glyph element (or its merged/cartouche outer atom)
const atom =
  (hover.closest(".svg-icon.merged") as HTMLElement | null) ||
  (hover.closest(".svg-icon.cartouche-wrapper") as HTMLElement | null) ||
  hover;

// Decide which side of the atom the anchor sits on, then snap the focus
// to the OPPOSITE side so the atom is included in the selection.
const anchorIsBeforeAtom = /* document-order check */;
const focusOffset = anchorIsBeforeAtom ? atomIdx + 1 : atomIdx;

sel.setBaseAndExtent(
  anchorNode, anchorOffset,
  parentOfAtom, focusOffset,
);
```

What that means in practice:
- You drag from after the last glyph leftward.
- The instant your cursor enters a glyph's bounding box (1px in is enough), the focus snaps to *before* that glyph and it becomes part of the selection.
- Drag the cursor 1px more out of the glyph to the left — now you're between glyphs, native browser handling places focus there (still includes everything to the right).
- Cursor enters the next glyph's box → focus snaps to *before* that one too. And so on.
- Same logic in reverse for left-to-right drags.

So pixel of cursor movement = pixel of selection change, even across 40px-wide glyphs. No more "drag way past it before it selects".

**3. Drag clears stale icon-selection at start**
- The first time `mousemove` exceeds the 4px drag threshold, we now also call `clearSelectedIcons()`. So the Group / Cartouche buttons (which prefer `selectedIcons` over the native range) won't operate on stale picks left over from a previous click — the new range is the source of truth.

**Caret-not-visible-between-glyphs:** Still on the list, separate fix. The browser-level cause is two adjacent atomic inline-blocks with no text node between them — the caret has no anchor text to render against. Two ways I can fix it:
- Add zero-width-space text nodes between consecutive glyphs at insert/normalize time (cleanest, has DOM bloat implications for copy/paste).
- Render a CSS-driven caret indicator at the editor div's caret position (more visual hackery, but no DOM changes).

I'll wait for your feedback on the selection feel first, and then pick the right caret-visibility approach. Try a drag and see if it now matches your expectation.

## [129] assistant

I get it exactly. The problem is that my snap *only* fires when the cursor is over a glyph. The instant the cursor is in the *gap* between glyphs, my handler returns early and the browser falls back to native behavior — and the browser uses midpoint-of-the-gap logic to decide which side glyph3 belongs to. So as you drift through the gap, the focus flips back to "after glyph3" → glyph3 deselects → only re-selects when you start touching glyph2.

Fix: stop reasoning about "what's under the cursor" and instead reason about **where cursor.x is relative to each glyph's edge**, every mousemove. Concrete rule:

- **Dragging leftward (anchor on the right):** include every glyph whose **right edge is past the cursor** (i.e. `cursor.x <= glyph.right`). Focus snaps to *before* the leftmost such glyph.
- **Dragging rightward (anchor on the left):** include every glyph whose **left edge is past the cursor** (i.e. `cursor.x >= glyph.left`). Focus snaps to *after* the rightmost such glyph.

This means the moment your cursor crosses a glyph's outer edge (right edge when going left, left edge when going right), that glyph is included — and crucially the gap *between* glyphs no longer flips state, because the relevant glyph's far edge is still beyond the cursor.

| At anchor (after `3`) | no glyph satisfies `cursor ≤ glyph.right`, native takes over | empty |
| Inside `3`'s box | `3.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓, `2.right < cursor.x`. Best = `3`. Focus = before `3` | `[3]` |
| Gap between `2` and `3` | `3.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓ (3.right is way right of cursor), `2.right < cursor.x`. Best = `3` still. Focus = before `3` | `[3]` ← **was dropping out before, now stays selected** |
| Inside `2`'s box | `3.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓, `2.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓, `1.right < cursor.x`. Best (leftmost) = `2`. Focus = before `2` | `[2, 3]` |
| Gap between `1` and `2` | `3.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓, `2.right ≥ cursor.x` ✓, `1.right < cursor.x`. Best = `2`. Focus = before `2` | `[2, 3]` ← **was missing `2` before, now stays selected** |
| Inside `1`'s box | All three pass. Best = `1`. Focus = before `1` | `[1, 2, 3]` |

The key difference: before, my snap only fired when cursor was *over* a glyph, so the gap was left to the browser's midpoint logic. Now the rule depends on the cursor's x relative to each glyph's *outer edge* (right edge for leftward drag, left edge for rightward), so once you're past a glyph's far edge, that glyph stays selected regardless of where you are in subsequent gaps.

Other useful properties of this rewrite:

- **Symmetric for rightward drag** — drag from before `1` rightward and you get the same edge-accurate inclusion as you cross each glyph's left edge.
- **Multi-line falls back to native.** The filter requires both anchor and cursor to be vertically inside a glyph's bounding band; if you drag to a different line, native browser selection takes over (which handles vertical movement well).
- **Non-glyph drags are unchanged.** If the cursor's line has no glyphs, the snap returns early and native selection works as normal text.
- **Direction-aware via cursor-vs-anchor.x** rather than via document-position comparison, so it handles cases like the anchor being inside a text node correctly.
- **Top-level glyphs only.** I excluded nested glyphs inside merged groups / cartouches via `!el.parentElement?.closest(".svg-icon")` so the snap treats those as single atoms (focus snaps to the outer wrapper, not to inner sub-glyphs).

Try the same `1 2 3` drag again. The selection should now track your cursor pixel-by-pixel — `3` stays highlighted as you cross into the gap, `2` joins as soon as cursor enters its box, and they both stay highlighted while you drift through the next gap toward `1`.

## [149] assistant

Two fixes landed in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:

**1. Drag-selection lag/gap bug** — the snap was silently no-op'ing when `start.x` happened to be at-or-left-of a glyph's right edge (e.g. browser snapped anchor to *before* glyph 3 when you mousedowned slightly inside it). I replaced the screen-position anchor check (`r.right <= anchorX + 1`) with a document-order check via `compareDocumentPosition` (`isGlyphAfterAnchor`). Now: dragging leftward from after glyph 3 always considers glyph 3 a candidate as long as `cursor.x ≤ glyph3.right`, so the moment your cursor enters the gap between 2 and 3, focus snaps to before glyph 3 — no more "drop out in the gap." Direction is still derived from `cursor.x` vs `mousedown.x`, but membership relative to the anchor is now unambiguous.

**2. Caret not showing / placed in the wrong gap** — when you click in the margin space between two atomic glyphs, the browser's hit-test for `contentEditable=false` inline-blocks frequently resolves to a position one or two slots off, and sometimes fails to show a caret at all. Now `handleEditorMouseDown` takes over for any non-glyph click:

- `e.preventDefault()` to stop the browser from overwriting us
- `editor.focus()` so the caret is actually visible
- `placeCaretAtPoint(x, y)` which uses `caretPositionFromPoint` / `caretRangeFromPoint`, lifts the resolved position out of any glyph atom (picks the side closer to click x), and falls back to the nearest top-level glyph edge in the click's y-band for clicks in empty trailing space

Net effect: clicking between 1 and 2 now anchors between 1 and 2; clicking past the last glyph anchors right after it; the caret is visible because we explicitly focus the editor.

Clicks on a glyph still pass through to native handling (no `preventDefault`), and cartouche inner-glyph clicks keep their toggle behavior. Drags still flow through the new mousemove snap, which now reads the anchor we placed in mousedown.

I switched to using document order comparison instead, so now it correctly identifies whether a glyph is before or after the anchor point regardless of drag direction.

For the caret visibility issue, I added direct caret placement when clicking empty space—using the browser's position-from-point APIs to find where the cursor should go, extracting it from any nested atoms, and falling back to the nearest top-level glyph edge if needed. This handles both the missing caret between glyphs and the misplaced caret problems by computing the position ourselves rather than trusting the browser's hit-testing on inline elements.

```diff
  // Place a collapsed caret at the given screen point inside the editor.
  //
  // We can't trust the browser's `caretPositionFromPoint` /
  // `caretRangeFromPoint` for clicks in the margin gap between two atomic
  // inline-block glyphs: when the click misses every text node, the

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return false;

    const topLevelGlyphs = Array.from(
      editor.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(".svg-icon"),
    ).filter((el) => !el.parentElement?.closest(".svg-icon"));

    // Glyphs whose vertical band the click is on. Use a small slop so
```

## [153] assistant

Caret placement is now glyph-aware in `placeCaretAtPoint`:

- Collect top-level `.svg-icon` atoms whose vertical band contains the click y, sort left→right.
- Walk them: if `clientX < glyph.left` → caret goes *before* that glyph (the gap to its left). If `clientX ≤ glyph.right` → caret goes on the closer half of that glyph. Past the last glyph on the line → caret goes after it.
- Resolve to `(parent, idx)` and set the range there.
- Only fall back to `caretPositionFromPoint`/`caretRangeFromPoint` when the line has no glyphs at all (plain-text lines), where the browser's resolver is reliable.

Net effect: clicking between glyph 1 and glyph 2 now lands exactly between glyph 1 and glyph 2 — no more "stuck on the trailing spacer" behavior.

Drag-selection is untouched; the new caret placement just sets the anchor more reliably, which the existing mousemove snap reads from. tsc clean, eslint clean (only the pre-existing `initializeHistory` warning).

Right — the issue is the early return for `target.closest(".svg-icon")`. When you click anywhere on a glyph (even an edge close to the gap), the mousedown handler bails to "let the browser handle it," and the browser's atom-midpoint caret rule takes over (and is finicky/invisible).

The fix: always run our own glyph-aware caret placement on mousedown, including for clicks that land *on* a glyph — `placeCaretAtPoint` already handles "click inside glyph" by picking the closer side based on the midpoint. The only exception is cartouche atoms, which need their inner-glyph toggle workflow preserved.

 I see the issue now—when a click lands in the visual space between two glyphs but actually hits one of them because its rendered area extends that far, the click handler detects it's on a glyph and returns early, preventing the caret from being placed where the user intended.

The solution is to stop returning early for glyph clicks and instead always use the glyph-aware hit-test logic in `placeCaretAtPoint`, which already knows how to position the caret at the correct side based on the midpoint. However, I need to preserve the special behavior for cartouche inner-glyph clicks, which should still toggle selection for the resize workflow. So the logic should be: if it's a cartouche atom, return early to let the click handler manage the toggle; otherwise, always call `placeCaretAtPoint` with `preventDefault()` to take over caret placement for regular glyphs, merged glyphs, and non-glyph areas.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  const handleEditorMouseDown = (e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    pointerDownPointRef.current = { x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY };
    isPointerDraggingRef.current = false;
    suppressEditorClickRef.current = false;

    const target = e.target as HTMLElement;
    if (target.closest(".svg-icon")) {
      // Click on a glyph atom: leave caret placement to the browser (it
      // will pick the side of the atom closer to the click), and don't
      // clear icon-selection state — the click handler decides.
      return;
    }

    // Click in plain editor space (gap between glyphs, end of line, blank
    // line, etc.). Take over caret placement so it lands exactly where the
    // user clicked, and ensure the editor is focused so the caret is
    // visible. preventDefault stops the browser from overwriting our
    // position with its own (often-wrong) hit-test result.
    e.preventDefault();
    editorRef.current?.focus({ preventScroll: true });
    placeCaretAtPoint(e.clientX, e.clientY);

    clearSelectedIcons();
  };
- NEW ---
  const handleEditorMouseDown = (e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    pointerDownPointRef.current = { x: e.clientX, y: e.clientY };
    isPointerDraggingRef.current = false;
    suppressEditorClickRef.current = false;

    const target = e.target as HTMLElement;
    const atom = target.closest(".svg-icon") as HTMLElement | null;

    // Cartouche atoms keep the browser's default mousedown handling and
    // their click-to-toggle workflow (inner-glyph selection for resize /
    // customization). For everything else — plain text, gaps between
    // glyphs, and regular/merged glyph atoms — we take over caret
    // placement so a click anywhere near a glyph anchors at the closer
    // edge of that glyph (or in the gap between two glyphs), instead of
    // surfacing the browser's atom-midpoint hit-test which is unreliable
    // and invisible at our glyph sizes.
    if (atom && atom.dataset.cartouche === "true") {
      return;
    }

    e.preventDefault();
    editorRef.current?.focus({ preventScroll: true });
    placeCaretAtPoint(e.clientX, e.clientY);

    if (!atom) {
      clearSelectedIcons();
    }
  };
```

---

```diff
      const lastWrapper = wrapAllHieroglyphRuns();
      if (lastWrapper) placeCaretAfter(lastWrapper);
    } else {
      unwrapAllAutoHilo();
    }
    relayoutAllMergedIcons();
    setColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    commitHistory("push");
    editor?.focus();
```

```diff
    if (horizontal) {
      // Side-by-side: box width/height = dimensions of the *biggest*
      // selected icon, scaled to fit.
      const measureIconWH = (el: HTMLElement) => {
        const r = el.getBoundingClientRect();
        let w = r.width || parseFloat(el.style.width) || 0;

        const svg = el.querySelector("svg") as SVGSVGElement | null;
        if ((w <= 0 || h <= 0) && svg) {

          h = parseFloat(svg.style.height) || baseSize;
        }
        if (w <= 0) w = baseSize;
        if (h <= 0) h = baseSize;
        return { w, h };
      };

      const extentM = icons.map((icon) => {
        const { w, h } = measureIconWH(icon as HTMLElement);
        return Math.max(w, h);
      });
      const maxExtent = Math.max(...extentM);
      const refIdx = extentM.findIndex((m) => m === maxExtent);
      const chosen = measureIconWH(icons[refIdx] as HTMLElement);
      const targetW = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, chosen.w)));
      const targetH = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, chosen.h)));
      columnMergeTargetW = targetW;
      columnMergeTargetH = targetH;

      const aspectRatios = readAspectRatios();
- NEW ---
    if (horizontal) {
      // Side-by-side: anchor the target box to baseSize × baseSize. We
      // intentionally don't measure the source icons' bounding rects —
      // for relayouts, the sources are already slot-sized clones, and
      // reading their rects produces a shrunken box. baseSize keeps the
      // group's horizontal dimensions stable across orientation flips.
      const targetW = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      const targetH = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      columnMergeTargetW = targetW;
      columnMergeTargetH = targetH;

      const aspectRatios = readAspectRatios();
```

## [171] assistant

Clean. Here's what changed in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:

**1. Layout math extracted** — `computeMergedLayout(icons, horizontal, baseSize)` now owns all the geometry (slot positions, wrapper dims, gap math). `createMergedIcon` and the new `relayoutMergedIcon` both use it.

**2. Orientation is now context-driven, not state-driven** — `createMergedIcon` decides horizontal vs. vertical from the local context of where it'll be inserted (`closest('.vertical-run')` on the first source icon), not from the global `columnMode`. New helper `shouldMergedGroupBeHorizontal(node)` encodes the rule "inside a vertical-run → horizontal layout."

**3. Horizontal target box anchored to `baseSize`** — previously it was measured from the source icons' bounding rects, which broke for relayouts because the sources are already slot-shrunk clones. `baseSize × baseSize` keeps dimensions stable across flips.

**4. `relayoutMergedIcon(wrapper, horizontal)`** — re-flows an existing merged group in place: recomputes via `computeMergedLayout`, then updates wrapper width/height/dataset, each slot's left/top/width/height, and each inner svg's dims. Skips `data-magicbox="true"` groups since their slot positions are user-set.

**5. `relayoutAllMergedIcons()`** — walks every `.svg-icon.merged` in the editor, computes its desired orientation from current context, and only relayouts if it differs from `dataset.layout` (no-op when nothing needs flipping).

**6. Wired into both toggle paths** — called after `toggleVerticalForSelection` and after the auto-wrap/unwrap branches of `toggleColumnMode`. So the moment you toggle vertical mode (whether on a selection or globally), every merged group inside the affected scope flips to be perpendicular to the new line direction.

Try the user flow:
- Type "1 2 3 4 5".
- Select 2-3, Group → "1 23 4 5" (2 stacked over 3).
- Select all + Vertical Mode → text turns vertical, and the "23" group now reads 2 next to 3 (horizontal).
- Vertical Mode again to toggle off → text horizontal, group flips back to 2 over 3.

Cartouches still follow the old `columnMode`-based flip in `updateAllIconTransforms` — let me know if you want them context-aware too (same idea, different geometry helper).

Four separate issues. Let me address each.

**Issue 1 — text selection broken**: My `handleEditorMouseDown` is calling `e.preventDefault()` *unconditionally* for any non-cartouche click. That kills the browser's native drag-selection on plain text. I need to only override when the click's line actually contains glyphs (where the browser's hit-test is unreliable).

**Issue 2 — selection lost after toggle**: `toggleVerticalForSelection` collapses the caret after wrapping/unwrapping. Should restore a range spanning the affected contents so re-clicking the button toggles back.

**Issue 3 — bottom-up drag fails in vertical**: My drag-snap logic is X-based (`extendingRight = cursorX >= start.x`, filter by cursorY overlap). In a `.vertical-run` glyphs are stacked vertically — all at similar X — so the X-based direction is degenerate and the cursorY filter only matches one glyph at a time. Need a Y-based mode when the anchor is inside a vertical-run.

**Issue 4 — stale active state**: The button-state effect listens for `click` and `keyup`. The vertical-toggle button click fires outside the editable area (filter bails) and the toggle doesn't generate keyup. Adding a `selectionchange` listener will catch the selection change the toggle produces.

Let me make all four fixes.

 I'm identifying a couple of issues with the text selection behavior. The `preventDefault()` call in the mouse down handler is blocking native text selection on plain text, and when toggling the vertical formatting, the selection gets lost because the DOM manipulation doesn't preserve the user's original selection range.

For vertical content, the drag-selection logic needs to account for both directions since it currently only handles horizontal overlap checks. Looking at the first issue more closely, the `preventDefault()` stops the browser from starting its native selection tracking, so when dragging over plain text without any glyphs, my custom selection logic returns early and nothing gets selected.

The real fix is to only prevent default when we're actually in a glyph-aware context. If the click lands on a line with glyphs, I'll use the custom caret placement and preventDefault. For plain-text-only lines, I should let the browser handle selection naturally. I could refactor `placeCaretAtPoint` to signal whether it did glyph-aware placement, then only preventDefault in those cases.

But there's a timing issue: if I set the selection without preventDefault, the browser's default hit-test will override it. So I need preventDefault whenever I'm calling `placeCaretAtPoint`, which means I also need to handle drag-selection myself for plain text.

The cleaner approach is to check upfront whether the click's y-band contains any glyphs. If it does, take over with preventDefault and custom caret placement. If it's pure plain text, let the browser do everything natively.

Building the detection logic...

Now for the vertical drag issue: the current logic uses X-coordinates to determine drag direction, but for vertically-stacked glyphs, I need to check Y-coordinates instead.

For vertical content, all glyphs share similar X positions but have different Y ranges, so I should detect the writing mode and switch to Y-based direction logic—checking if the cursor is moving down the document rather than right.

On the selection preservation issue, I'm replacing the `placeCaretAfter` call with code that creates a new range spanning the wrapper's contents and applies it to the selection, though I need to be careful since the original placement was meant to prevent typing inside the vertical-run.

Still writing the toggle logic... the same checks as the selection-change handler, so state updates whenever selection shifts. This approach is additive and won't break existing click/keyup behavior.

Second, after toggling vertical mode, I'll restore the selection to span the modified content instead of collapsing it.   // Wrap the current selection in a `.vertical-run`. If the selection is

```diff
  const toggleVerticalForSelection = (range: Range): boolean => {
    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return false;

      range.startContainer,
      editor,
    );
    const enclosingEnd = findEnclosingVerticalRun(range.endContainer, editor);

      wrapper.className = "vertical-run";
```

```diff
  // Check formatting state on keyup, click, and selectionchange.

  // whenever the underlying selection range changes, including those
  // programmatic re-sets.

      const sel = window.getSelection();
      if (!sel || sel.rangeCount === 0) return;
      const anchorNode = sel.anchorNode;
      if (!anchorNode) return;

      const anchorEl =
        anchorNode.nodeType === Node.ELEMENT_NODE
          ? (anchorNode as Element)
          : anchorNode.parentElement;
      const editorElement = anchorEl?.closest('[contenteditable="true"]');
      if (!editorElement) return;

        setIsBold(document.queryCommandState("bold"));
        setIsItalic(document.queryCommandState("italic"));
```

## [192] assistant

All clean. Summary:

**1. Plain-text selection restored** (`handleEditorMouseDown`)
The takeover (`preventDefault` + `placeCaretAtPoint`) now only fires when the click is on a glyph atom *or* the click's y-band actually contains glyphs. For plain-text-only lines the browser handles focus, caret placement, and drag-selection natively — exactly as it did originally.

**2. Selection preserved across Vertical Mode toggle** (`toggleVerticalForSelection`)
- Wrap path: after `range.insertNode(wrapper)`, the new selection spans the wrapper's contents (`selectContentsOf(wrapper)`).
- Unwrap path: after `unwrapElement(target)`, the selection spans from before the target's old `firstChild` through after its old `lastChild` (`selectRangeOfSiblings`).
- Both update `savedRangeRef` so the saved range matches what the user sees.
- Result: re-clicking Vertical Mode immediately reverts the previous toggle without re-selecting.

**3. Bottom-up drag in vertical content** (`handleEditorMouseMove`)
Made the drag-snap orientation-aware: detects whether the selection anchor is inside a `.vertical-run`. When vertical:
- Direction along Y (`extendingForward = cursorY >= start.y`).
- Glyph "in line" filter uses cursorX overlap (perpendicular axis).
- Snap edges are top/bottom of glyphs instead of left/right.

So dragging up *or* down through a stack of vertical glyphs now snaps the selection focus to the appropriate horizontal edge of each glyph as it's crossed — same pixel-accurate behavior as the horizontal case, just rotated.

**4. Live button state** (`AssistantBar`)
Added a `selectionchange` document listener that calls a refresh helper directly (no editor-target gate, since selection changes triggered programmatically by `toggleColumnMode` originate outside the editor element). The helper still validates that the selection's anchor is inside a contentEditable area before reading state. Click/keyup keep their editor-target filter.

Net effect: clicking Vertical Mode flips the active state immediately (the toggle re-sets the selection inside/outside the new `.vertical-run`, fires `selectionchange`, refresh runs, `isInVerticalRun` updates).

Try the flow:
- Drag-select plain text → works.
- Select hieroglyphs → click Vertical Mode → selection stays on the now-vertical content, button is active → click again → reverts back, selection still spans the same content, button deactivates.
- Drag-select vertical glyphs from any starting point in any direction.

## [199] assistant

Clean — 0 errors, 0 warnings on the file.

What I changed in `AssistantBar`:

- Lifted `refreshFromSelection` out of the `useEffect` and into a top-level `useCallback`. Same logic, now reusable.
- Added `handleVerticalModeClick`: calls `toggleColumnMode`, then schedules `refreshFromSelection` on the next animation frame.
- Vertical Mode button now uses `handleVerticalModeClick`.

Why this fixes it: `selectionchange` is debounced in Chrome and may not fire until after the click handler has fully returned and the DOM has settled. Within the synchronous toggle path we do multiple `removeAllRanges`/`addRange` plus a `focus()` plus `commitHistory`, and Chrome batches all those into one async `selectionchange` that lands later. By calling `requestAnimationFrame(refreshFromSelection)` right after the toggle, we guarantee the state pulls from the post-toggle selection before the next paint — so the button updates immediately with no extra click required.

Bold/Italic/etc. continue to update through the same listeners (no behavior change there).

Add CSS to make the spacer / selection-boundary placeholders invisible and skip the selection highlight on them:

Adding the CSS styles...

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/index.css`
```diff
- OLD ---
/* Glyphs themselves stay rendered in their normal horizontal coordinate
   system; only the *layout* of the run is vertical. This prevents inherited
   writing-mode from rotating the SVG content inside each glyph. */
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon,
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon svg {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}
- NEW ---
/* Glyphs themselves stay rendered in their normal horizontal coordinate
   system; only the *layout* of the run is vertical. This prevents inherited
   writing-mode from rotating the SVG content inside each glyph. */
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon,
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon svg {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}

/* Caret-anchor placeholders. These spans hold a ZWSP that lets the caret
   land before a leading icon / on an empty line. They have to remain
   selectable by the Selection API (the caret needs to be able to live in
   them) but should be visually invisible at all times — including when
   the user does Ctrl+A or drags a selection across them. Without these
   rules they render as a thin selectable strip beside the content
   (especially obvious when an entire vertical run is selected, where the
   strip's height matches the run's). */
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"],
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 0;
  max-width: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
  font-size: 0;
  line-height: 0;
  color: transparent;
  vertical-align: top;
}

.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"] *::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] *::selection {
  background-color: transparent;
  color: transparent;
}
```

## [208] assistant

Clean. What I added in `src/index.css`:

```css
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"],
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 0;
  max-width: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
  font-size: 0;
  line-height: 0;
  color: transparent;
  vertical-align: top;
}

.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"] *::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] *::selection {
  background-color: transparent;
  color: transparent;
}
```

Two helper spans hold ZWSPs as caret anchors:

- `[data-selection-boundary]` — placed before a leading icon so the caret has somewhere to land in front of it.
- `[data-editor-line-spacer]` — empty-line placeholder and start-of-line anchor when a line begins with a non-editable atom (icon/img).

They have to stay in the DOM (deleting them breaks "click before the first glyph to place caret" and "type on an empty line"), but their default rendering shows a thin selectable strip — most visible on Ctrl+A and especially next to a `.vertical-run`, where the strip's height matches the run.

The new rules:
- Force them to render as zero-width, zero-line-height, transparent containers regardless of their inline styles. They still exist in the DOM and remain valid selection endpoints (programmatic ranges still work, typing still routes through them via `normalizeEditorArtifacts`).
- The `::selection` overrides paint their selection highlight transparent — so even when included in a Ctrl+A range, no visible strip is drawn.

Caret anchoring, typing-on-empty-line, click-before-leading-icon all keep working because we didn't touch `user-select` or `pointer-events` (the leading boundary's existing `pointer-events: none` is preserved by inline style).

Try Ctrl+A now — the trailing/beside-the-glyphs strip should be gone, and the glyphs themselves still get the normal selection highlight.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/index.css`
```diff
- OLD ---
/* Glyphs themselves stay rendered in their normal horizontal coordinate
   system; only the *layout* of the run is vertical. This prevents inherited
   writing-mode from rotating the SVG content inside each glyph. */
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon,
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon svg {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}

/* Caret-anchor placeholders. These spans hold a ZWSP that lets the caret
   land before a leading icon / on an empty line. They have to remain
   selectable by the Selection API (the caret needs to be able to live in
   them) but should be visually invisible at all times — including when
   the user does Ctrl+A or drags a selection across them. Without these
   rules they render as a thin selectable strip beside the content
   (especially obvious when an entire vertical run is selected, where the
   strip's height matches the run's). */
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"],
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 0;
  max-width: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
  font-size: 0;
  line-height: 0;
  color: transparent;
  vertical-align: top;
}

.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-editor-line-spacer="true"] *::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"]::selection,
.editor-content [data-selection-boundary="true"] *::selection {
  background-color: transparent;
  color: transparent;
}
- NEW ---
/* Glyphs themselves stay rendered in their normal horizontal coordinate
   system; only the *layout* of the run is vertical. This prevents inherited
   writing-mode from rotating the SVG content inside each glyph. */
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon,
.editor-content .vertical-run .svg-icon svg {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}
```

```diff
- OLD ---
    if ((e.ctrlKey || e.metaKey) && !e.altKey) {
      const key = e.key.toLowerCase();
      if (key === "z") {
        e.preventDefault();
        if (e.shiftKey) {
          performRedo();

- NEW ---
    if ((e.ctrlKey || e.metaKey) && !e.altKey) {
      const key = e.key.toLowerCase();
      if (key === "z") {
        e.preventDefault();
        if (e.shiftKey) {
          performRedo();
        } else {
          performUndo();
        }
        return;
      }

      if (key === "y") {
        e.preventDefault();
        performRedo();
        return;
      }

      if (key === "a") {
        // Native Ctrl+A in this editor occasionally fails to highlight the
        // very first child — most often the first glyph — because the
        // leading `data-selection-boundary` ZWSP span anchors the range's
        // start *inside* its text node, and the browser's atom-highlight
        // pass starts after that anchor. Take over and set the range
        // explicitly to span editor:0 → editor:childNodes.length so every
        // top-level child (including the first glyph) is in the range.
        e.preventDefault();
        const sel = window.getSelection();
        if (sel) {
          const range = document.createRange();
          range.selectNodeContents(editor);
          sel.removeAllRanges();
          sel.addRange(range);
          savedRangeRef.current = range.cloneRange();
        }
        return;
      }
    }
```

```diff
    icons.forEach((icon, i) => {
      const { w, h, left, top } = slotDims[i];

      const svgSpan = document.createElement("span");
      svgSpan.style.cssText = `
          position: absolute;
          left: ${left}px;
          top: ${top}px;
          width: ${w}px;
          height: ${h}px;
          overflow: hidden;
          `;

      const svgClone = icon.cloneNode(true) as HTMLElement;
      svgClone.style.cssText = `
          position: absolute;
          top: 0;
          left: 0;
          width: ${w}px;
          height: ${h}px;
          `;
      const inner = svgClone.querySelector("svg") as SVGElement | null;
      if (inner) {
        inner.removeAttribute("width");
        inner.removeAttribute("height");
        (inner.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).width = `${w}px`;
        (inner.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).height = `${h}px`;
        inner.setAttribute("preserveAspectRatio", "xMidYMid meet");
      }

      svgSpan.appendChild(svgClone);
      mergedWrapper.appendChild(svgSpan);
    });
- NEW ---
    icons.forEach((icon, i) => {
      const { w, h, left, top } = slotDims[i];

      const svgSpan = document.createElement("span");
      svgSpan.style.cssText = `

          left: ${left}px;
          top: ${top}px;
          width: ${w}px;
          height: ${h}px;
          overflow: hidden;
          `;

      const svgClone = icon.cloneNode(true) as HTMLElement;
      fitCloneIntoSlot(svgClone, w, h);

      svgSpan.appendChild(svgClone);
      mergedWrapper.appendChild(svgSpan);
    });
```

```diff
  // True iff the merged group at `node` should lay out horizontally —
  // i.e. its line is running vertically (it's inside a .vertical-run).
  const shouldMergedGroupBeHorizontal = (node: Node | null): boolean => {
    if (!node) return false;

  // True iff the merged group at `node` should lay out horizontally —

  const shouldMergedGroupBeHorizontal = (node: Node | null): boolean => {
    if (!node) return false;
    const el =
      node.nodeType === Node.ELEMENT_NODE
        ? (node as Element)
        : node.parentElement;
    return !!el?.closest(".vertical-run");
  };

  // Fit a cloned glyph into a merged-group slot of size (slotW × slotH).
  //
  // For a regular glyph the inner SVG is inherently scalable (uses
  // viewBox + preserveAspectRatio), so we just resize the wrapper and
  // the SVG element directly.
  //
  // Cartouches are different: their wrapper contains a frame SVG plus a
  // separate `.cartouche-icons-container` whose inner glyph SVGs are
  // absolutely sized in pixels. Resizing only the outer wrapper makes
  // the inner glyphs poke out of the frame (they don't shrink with the
  // wrapper). We instead keep the cartouche's natural dimensions and
  // apply `transform: scale()` to the whole clone so the frame and its
  // inner glyphs scale together, then center the scaled box inside the
  // slot.
  const fitCloneIntoSlot = (
    clone: HTMLElement,
    slotW: number,
    slotH: number,
  ) => {
    const isCartouche = clone.dataset.cartouche === "true";
    if (isCartouche) {
      const origW = Number(clone.dataset.origWidth) || slotW;
      const origH = Number(clone.dataset.origHeight) || slotH;
      const safeO

_... [truncated 1036 chars] ..._
```

```diff
      slot.style.height = `${dim.h}px`;
```

## [232] assistant

Clean. Summary:

**New `fitCloneIntoSlot(clone, slotW, slotH)` helper** in `createMergedIcon`'s region:

- **Regular glyph** (existing behavior): the inner SVG uses `viewBox` + `preserveAspectRatio`, so directly resizing the wrapper and the SVG element scales the artwork correctly. No regression.
- **Cartouche** (`data-cartouche="true"`): the wrapper contains a frame SVG plus a `.cartouche-icons-container` div with absolutely-pixel-sized inner glyph SVGs. Resizing only the outer wrapper makes the inner glyphs ignore the resize and poke out of the frame. The new path:
  - Reads the cartouche's stored natural dims from `dataset.origWidth/origHeight`.
  - Computes `scale = min(slotW / origW, slotH / origH)` — uniform fit.
  - Keeps the layout box at its original `origW × origH` and applies `transform: scale(scale)` with `transform-origin: top left`. Frame + inner glyphs scale together as a unit.
  - Centers the scaled visual inside the slot via `left: (slotW - scaledW) / 2`, `top: (slotH - scaledH) / 2`.

**Used in two places**:
1. `createMergedIcon` — when first building the merged group's slots.
2. `relayoutMergedIcon` — when an existing merged group flips orientation (e.g. user toggles Vertical Mode and a cartouche-containing group needs to re-flow).

Net effect for the user's flow:
- Wrap glyphs in a cartouche → padding intact (existing fix).
- Group that cartouche with another glyph → cartouche scales uniformly into its slot; padding inside the cartouche is preserved relative to its frame; nothing pokes out.
- Toggle that whole group's orientation (vertical/horizontal text) → cartouche re-fits its new slot the same way.

tsc + eslint clean.

## [243] assistant

 I'm setting up a scaling system where icons are rendered at 8x their display size for better quality, so a 39x39 icon becomes a 312x312 canvas, and composite icons like merged cartouches at 78x78 scale up to 624x624—which takes about 1.5MB of raw RGBA data. Each rendered PNG gets stored as a data URL string in the cache, with a 624x624 image's data URL being roughly 50 kilobytes.

When a user selects multiple icons and changes their size, the system iterates through each one, updates the DOM, and re-rasterizes the entire PNG by creating a scaled canvas, serializing the inner SVG, converting it to a blob with an object URL, loading it as an image asynchronously, drawing it onto the canvas, and finally converting the canvas to a PNG data URL—which is a synchronous, CPU-intensive operation that blocks the UI. The real problem is that rapid size changes create a cascade of async image loads and canvas operations that can fire out of order, stale PNGs overwrite fresh ones in the cache, old canvases pile up in memory without cleanup, and the synchronous PNG encoding becomes a bottleneck for documents with many icons.

The cache itself is unbounded and never cleared—when icons are removed or recreated with new IDs, their old PNG entries remain in memory indefinitely. I'm seeing that cachePngForSvgIcon gets called from multiple places: the useEffect cleanup checks if an icon is already cached before regenerating, but handleIconSizeChange bypasses that check entirely and forces a re-rasterization every single time. Looking at the call sites, it's also triggered during drag operations, merged icon creation, relayout, and cartouche wrapping, so there are many paths that can pile up simultaneous canvas operations.

The real bottleneck is size changes—when the user spams the +/- button, every icon gets re-rasterized synchronously (SVG serialization and canvas toDataURL are CPU-heavy), and with 50+ icons each generating 100k-500k pixels of canvas data, this creates a massive queue. I need to find where the cache is actually consumed to understand if we can defer caching until it's actually needed, or if we should throttle the size change handler, implement lazy caching on demand, or add an LRU eviction policy to keep memory bounded.

## [281] assistant

Now I have a clear picture. Let me start implementing the fixes.

 I need to handle the drag clone case conservatively by focusing on the size-change scenario first, then applying the same pattern elsewhere. The key insight is that the queue needs to be keyed by icon ID so that duplicate scheduling requests for the same icon get merged together. I'll keep the existing `cachePngForSvgIcon` function as-is and introduce a new `scheduleCachePng` function alongside it to manage this scheduling logic.

Now I'm thinking through the trade-offs: the drag clone appears immediately on screen with finalized DOM, and since `cachePngForSvgIcon` is async anyway (Image.onload), the PNG cache will populate after the initial render whether I schedule it or call it eagerly. The real difference is that scheduling delays the start, but the visual result is the same — SVG initially, then PNG when ready. For copy operations, if the cache isn't ready yet, the fallback to SVG data URI works fine since Word supports both formats. This trade-off is acceptable, so I'll use the scheduler everywhere except in specific cases where there's a strong reason not to, like immediate copy paths. I'm also planning to lower the COPY_SCALE from 8 to 4, then add the `scheduleCachePng` helper and systematically replace the call sites.

Actually, the core issue is simpler: I need to handle size changes properly. If I just remove the `cachePngForSvgIcon` call from `handleIconSizeChange`, the cache will still contain PNGs at the old size, so copies will show the wrong dimensions. Instead, I should schedule a re-cache after size changes so the correct PNGs eventually populate, and if the user copies before that finishes, they'll either get the old PNG or fall back to SVG — both acceptable outcomes.

But now I'm realizing the real performance problem: when a user clicks the size buttons multiple times rapidly (say 5 clicks in a second with 50 icons each), there are 250 in-flight Image objects loading in parallel. Each one completes, calls `toDataURL` on a 720×720 canvas, and caches the result. The synchronous `toDataURL` operations alone could take 50–100ms each, totaling 25 seconds of main-thread blocking work — that's the actual explosion.

On top of that, memory usage spikes: 250 canvases sitting in memory, each holding 2MB of raw pixel data at COPY_SCALE=8, adds up to 500MB. Even at COPY_SCALE=4 it's still 130MB. The real solution is to serialize the work — process only a few canvases at a time using idle scheduling instead of letting them all fire at once.

Writing the fix now...

## [282] assistant

Now let me implement the fixes. I'll start by reducing `COPY_SCALE` and adding an idle-time PNG cache scheduler.

 I'm pinpointing the performance issue: when selecting all icons and adjusting size, the handler loops through every SVG element and synchronously caches each one as a PNG, allocating large canvases for each conversion that's consuming memory and blocking the UI.

For 50 icons, this creates roughly 100MB of temporary memory usage and several seconds of blocking work as each canvas encodes to a data URL, plus rapid clicks queue up stale operations that keep firing. The cache itself never clears, so old PNG strings accumulate with each merge or relayout.

My approach is to reduce the canvas scale factor from 8 to 4 for faster encoding, then replace the synchronous caching with an idle-time scheduler that processes icons in small batches to avoid blocking the main thread.

Implementing the cache pruning now...

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  const COPY_SCALE = 8;
  const COPY_DPI = 96 * COPY_SCALE;
- NEW ---
  // Oversampling factor when rasterizing SVGs to PNG for the copy clipboard.
  // 4x is enough for ~Retina sharpness and keeps each canvas small enough
  // that batch re-caching (e.g. on a global icon-size change) doesn't blow
  // up RAM or stall on toDataURL. Bumping this back to 8 multiplies canvas
  // memory and PNG-encoding time by ~4x per icon.
  const COPY_SCALE = 4;
  const COPY_DPI = 96 * COPY_SCALE;
```

Now add the idle scheduler:

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  const pngCacheRef = useRef<Map<string, string>>(new Map());
- NEW ---
  const pngCacheRef = useRef<Map<string, string>>(new Map());
  // Deferred / chunked PNG-cache scheduler.
  //
  // `cachePngForSvgIcon` allocates a canvas at COPY_SCALE×, serializes the
  // SVG, fires an async <img> decode, then synchronously encodes a PNG via
  // `canvas.toDataURL`. Calling that for *every* icon in handlers like
  // `handleIconSizeChange` (which iterates the whole document) was the cause
  // of the RAM spike + UI freeze on "select all + change size": many large
  // canvases live in memory at once and many toDataURL calls block the main
  // thread back-to-back.
  //
  // Instead, we queue icons to be re-rasterized and process the queue in
  // small chunks during browser idle time. The Map is keyed by data-id so
  // repeated requests for the same icon coalesce. Flushing also prunes
  // cache entries for icons that no longer exist in the editor (merge /
  // relayout assigns new ids, leaving the old entries dangling forever).
  const pngCacheQueueRef = useRef<Map<string, HTMLElement>>(new Map());
  const pngCacheScheduledRef = useRef<number | null>(null);
```
