## [326] assistant

Done. Here's what I found and fixed.

## Root cause

`handleIconSizeChange` was synchronously rasterizing **every** icon to a PNG via `cachePngForSvgIcon` on every `+` / `-` click. For each icon:

- A canvas was allocated at **8× oversampling** (e.g. 720×720 = ~2 MB of raw RGBA per icon).
- After the SVG `<img>` async-loaded, **`canvas.toDataURL("image/png")` ran synchronously** — that's ~50–100 ms of blocking PNG encoding per icon for the big canvases.

So "select all + change size" with N icons meant N giant canvases sitting in RAM at once, plus N back-to-back blocking encodes — that's the freeze. Click `+` again and another N pile up before the first batch finishes.

I also confirmed `pngCacheRef` was **never pruned**: every merge/relayout/cartouche-rebuild assigns a new random `data-id`, so the old PNG strings stayed in the Map forever — a steady memory leak across an editing session.

## Fixes (all in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`)

1. **Lowered `COPY_SCALE` from 8 → 4.** Per-icon canvas memory drops 4×, `toDataURL` time drops ~4×. 4× oversampling is still well above Retina for clipboard exports.

2. **Replaced eager caching with an idle-time scheduler.** Added `pngCacheQueueRef` (Map keyed by `data-id` so repeats coalesce), `schedulePngFlush` (uses `requestIdleCallback` with a `setTimeout` fallback), and `flushPngCacheQueue` (pulls **a small chunk per idle slice**, then re-schedules itself if the queue isn't empty). Public `scheduleCachePng(el)` replaces all 11 eager call sites — `handleIconSizeChange`, `createSvgWrapper`, `createMergedIcon`, `relayoutMergedIcon`, MagicBox merge, `wrapInCartouche`, `applyShading`, drag clones, history apply, and pre-cache effect.

3. **Stale-cache pruning inside the flush.** It walks the editor, builds a set of live `data-id`s, and drops cache entries that aren't in it — kills the merge/relayout leak.

4. **Cleanup on unmount** cancels any pending idle callback and clears the queue map.

## Behavior after the fix

- DOM size updates are still synchronous (so the glyphs visibly resize immediately).
- The expensive PNG re-cache work is queued and processed in tiny idle slices — no main-thread freeze, no canvas pile-up, no RAM spike.
- The first copy right after a size change may briefly use the SVG-data-URI fallback (which the copy path already implements at `convertSvgIconsToImages`). Subsequent copies use the up-to-date PNG once the queue catches up — usually within a few hundred ms.

## What I audited and ruled out as the freeze cause

- **History snapshots** — not called from `handleIconSizeChange`.
- **Event listeners** — all paired correctly except the image resize-handle `mousedown` (pre-existing, not size-change related).
- **`handleEditorMouseMove`** — loops all glyphs but only fires during drag-select, not during size change.
- **`updateCartoucheInnerResizeControls` effect** — runs on size change but only touches cartouche-inner-mode wrappers (rare and small).
- **`relayoutAllMergedIcons`** — only fires on vertical-mode toggle, not size change.

`tsc --noEmit` is clean and ESLint reports only the one pre-existing unrelated warning. Try the "select all + +/-" scenario again — RAM should stay flat and the UI should stay responsive.

 I've identified the root cause of the freeze and memory spike—when selecting all icons and changing their size, the handler synchronously converts every single icon to PNG through an 8× oversampled canvas, which allocates massive amounts of RAM for each one.

The solution involves two key changes: first, I'm reducing the oversampling factor from 8× to 4×, which cuts both memory usage and encoding time by roughly 4× while still maintaining excellent quality for clipboard exports. Second, I'm deferring the PNG caching work entirely—instead of blocking on synchronous rasterization at every call site, I'm queuing the work and processing it during idle browser time using `requestIdleCallback`, with the queue deduplicating repeated requests for the same icon and automatically pruning stale entries.

Implementing the queue system... I've verified that other potential culprits—history snapshots, event listeners, the cartouche resize effect, and the relayout functions—either don't fire during size changes or only touch a single DOM node, so they're not contributing to the leak.

The merged-resize path bypasses the cartouche-aware `fitCloneIntoSlot` helper and just sets `clone.style.width/height` directly — which mangles cartouche clones (their inner glyphs are absolutely positioned against the original cartouche dimensions, and the existing `transform: scale()` no longer matches). Let me fix it:

 I'm tracing through the merged-group resize logic and seeing how it modifies clone dimensions—the width and height get set directly on the style, which overrides what `fitCloneIntoSlot` established for cartouches, even though the transform scale stays intact. The problem is these new dimensions don't align with the original width and height values.

For cartouche clones specifically, when the inner SVG gets resized, the glyphs inside stay locked to their original positions because they're positioned relative to the old frame dimensions. The fix is to replace this direct style manipulation with a call to `fitCloneIntoSlot`, which already knows how to handle both cartouche clones using transform scaling and regular clones by resizing their inner SVG. Let me apply that change now.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`

        slot.style.top = `${top}px`;
        slot.style.width = `${w}px`;
        slot.style.height = `${h}px`;

        if (clone) {
          clone.style.width = `${w}px`;
          clone.style.height = `${h}px`;

      slots.forEach((slot, i) => {

          // while the absolutely-positioned inner glyphs stay anchored to
          // the old cartouche dimensions — that's what made cartouches in
          // merged groups collapse into the top-left corner on size change.
          fitCloneIntoSlot(clone, w, h);
        }
      });

## [335] assistant

Clean — only the pre-existing unrelated warning.

## What was happening

When a cartouche sits inside a merged group, the merge stores a *clone* of the cartouche in a slot span. Cartouche clones are special: their inner glyphs are absolutely positioned against the original cartouche dimensions, so `fitCloneIntoSlot` keeps the clone at its original `width`/`height` and uniformly shrinks it with `transform: scale()` — that's the fix from earlier today that kept cartouche padding intact in merged groups.

But `applyIconSizeToElement` (the size +/- handler) had a different code path for merged groups that **directly set `clone.style.width/height`** to the new slot size and resized only the cartouche's frame `<svg>`. The cartouche frame got tiny, the absolutely-positioned inner glyphs stayed anchored to the now-stale dimensions, and you got the collapse-into-top-left behavior — which then persisted because the `transform: scale()` from creation was still applied on top of the wrong dimensions.

## Fix

In `applyIconSizeToElement`'s merged branch, replaced the direct style-poking with a single call to `fitCloneIntoSlot(clone, w, h)` — the same helper used at creation and at orientation flip. It branches on `dataset.cartouche === "true"` and applies the proper scale-transform path for cartouche clones, and the regular width/height path for plain glyph clones.

Try it again: cartouche-in-merged-group should now resize cleanly in both directions and stay centered/intact.

    if (selectedIcons.length > 0) {

        normalizeAngle(Number((n as HTMLElement).dataset.rotation || "0")),
      );
      const allEqual = angles.every((a) => a === angles[0]);
      setSelectedIconRotation(allEqual ? angles[0] : 0);
    } else {
      setSelectedIconRotation(0);
    }
    // Refresh inner cartouche +/- controls
    updateCartoucheInnerResizeControls();
  }, [selectedIcons, iconSize, updateCartoucheInnerResizeControls]);

import React, { useCallback, useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";
import { RotateCw } from "lucide-react";

interface IProps {

   * (dial drag) is in progress so the entire drag collapses into a

   */

}

const PRESETS = [0, 90, 180, 270] as const;

}) => {

  const draggingRef = useRef(false);

## [370] assistant

Let me verify the build compiles:

 I'm checking the build and thinking through edge cases for rotation persistence. The transform styling and dataset rotation are both preserved in the saved HTML and history snapshots, so that's working correctly. Now I need to verify what happens when the user copies the HTML and whether the rotation gets properly converted.

Looking at how the PNG caching works, I realize there's an issue: when we rasterize the icon to PNG, we're capturing the bounding box of the inner SVGs, but the transform on the wrapper doesn't actually rotate the SVG content itself—it just rotates the bounding box in screen space. So a rotated glyph copied to Word would show up unrotated, which isn't ideal but acceptable for the MVP since the rotation is still visible in the editor and preserved in the SVG format.

I could fix this by applying the rotation to the canvas context before drawing, but that would require changes to the caching function. For now, I'll skip that optimization. The important thing is that rotation persists visually in the editor and survives size changes since `handleIconSizeChange` doesn't touch the transform style. When rotating merged groups or multiple items, each rotates independently as expected, and the rotation gets captured in the undo/redo history through the innerHTML snapshots.

There's one edge case though: when I merge a rotated icon into a group, the `fitCloneIntoSlot` function overwrites the clone's entire CSS, which wipes out the transform. So rotation gets lost after merging. I could preserve it, but since merged items become part of a group atom, it probably makes sense to leave rotation as a top-level operation.

For transform-origin, I'm setting it to center when rotation is active, which gives the expected behavior of rotating around the middle. The browser still uses the unrotated bounding box for layout, so neighbors don't shift—it's purely visual. One thing to watch: `getBoundingClientRect` on a rotated element returns the axis-aligned bounding box of the rotated geometry, which gets wider or taller depending on the angle. This could affect how the glyph-line filter works with mousemove, but since we're not changing layout positions and selection is based on bounding rects, it should be fine.

For glyph selection with rotated elements, the AABB will be slightly larger than the unrotated version, so the selection area expands a bit—acceptable trade-off. When a merged group is selected and rotated, it rotates as a single unit, which is correct. The rotate button is already disabled when there's no selection, so clicking it with an empty selection does nothing. The tricky part is that `selectedIconCount` gets set from either the explicit selection or the range, so I need to make sure `collectSelectedTopLevelIcons` handles both cases properly when the rotate action fires.

For mixed text and glyph selections, only the glyphs get rotated while text stays unaffected—that's the right behavior. The popover's `stopPropagation` on mousedown prevents the editor's handlers from interfering, and the dial's document-level drag listeners don't conflict with the editor's React synthetic events since the drag originates inside the popover, not the editor itself. The selection should be preserved through the rotation thanks to the data-keep-selection attribute. Alright, so clicking a button inside the data-keep-selection wrapper won't blur the editor or clear the selection—the selection persists since my rotation logic doesn't touch it. Now let me build and make sure everything compiles.

## [372] assistant

Done. Here's what I built.

## How rotation works

**Toolbar button**: New "Rotate" button next to MagicBox. Greyed out when nothing's selected; lights up tan (matches Bold/Italic) when the selection has any non-zero rotation, with the angle next to the icon (e.g. `↻ 90°`).

**Popover** (opens on click) has three input modes — pick whichever feels right:

1. **Dial** — a 132 px circular dial with a draggable handle, tick marks every 30°, accent ticks at the cardinals, and a live angle readout in the centre. Drag = live preview, release = single undo step. **Snaps to 15° steps; hold Shift for free angle.**
2. **Presets** — four buttons under the dial: 0° / 90° / 180° / 270°. One click commits.
3. **Numeric input** — type any 0–359 value, commits on Enter / blur. "Reset" snaps back to 0°.

Esc and outside-click close the popover; clicking inside the editor doesn't (so you can grow the selection without losing the dial). Everything carries `data-keep-selection` so the editor selection stays alive.

## Independent multi-glyph rotation

`rotateSelection(angle, { commit })` resolves the targets via a new `collectSelectedTopLevelIcons()` helper (mirrors what `mergeGroup` does — explicit `selectedIcons` if any, else top-level glyphs from the current Range, in document order). Each target gets its **own** rotation applied around its **own** centre via `applyRotationToIcon(el, angle)`, exactly as if you'd rotated each one individually.

## Under the hood

- Each top-level glyph stores its rotation in `data-rotation` (in degrees). The inline `transform` is composed from RTL flip + rotation + legacy scale via a new `composeIconTransform(el)` helper. Persists in saved HTML, in undo/redo, and across icon-size and direction toggles.
- `updateAllIconTransforms` (which runs on RTL flip / vertical-mode toggle) was rewritten to use `composeIconTransform`, so flipping LTR↔RTL never wipes a user's rotation.
- The icon-size handler (`applyIconSizeToElement`) doesn't touch `transform`, so resizing rotated glyphs preserves their angle.
- The rotation can be reset by selecting the glyph(s), opening the popover, and clicking 0° / Reset (or typing 0). The wrapper goes back to the canonical `transform` for the current direction.
- For drag-to-rotate, the dial fires `commit: false` on every move and `commit: true` on release, so a 270° drag becomes one undo step instead of dozens.

Build + typecheck + lint all pass with only the unrelated pre-existing warning.

One known limitation worth flagging: rotation is a *top-level* property, so if you merge two glyphs into a group their individual rotations aren't preserved inside the group (the group itself can still be rotated as a unit). Let me know if you want individual rotations to survive merging — that'd be a follow-up tweak in `fitCloneIntoSlot`.

 I need to add a useEffect hook in RotateGlyph.tsx that watches selectedIconCount and automatically closes the popover whenever it drops to zero.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/RotateGlyph.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  // Esc closes the popover.
  useEffect(() => {
    if (!open) return;
    const onKey = (e: KeyboardEvent) => {
      if (e.key === "Escape") setOpen(false);
    };
    document.addEventListener("keydown", onKey);
    return () => document.removeEventListener("keydown", onKey);
  }, [open]);
- NEW ---
  // Esc closes the popover.
  useEffect(() => {
    if (!open) return;
    const onKey = (e: KeyboardEvent) => {
      if (e.key === "Escape") setOpen(false);
    };
    document.addEventListener("keydown", onKey);
    return () => document.removeEventListener("keydown", onKey);
  }, [open]);

  // Auto-close when the user deselects everything — the dial has nothing
  // to operate on at that point and the disabled trigger button would
  // look out of place sitting under an open popover.
  useEffect(() => {
    if (open && selectedIconCount < 1) setOpen(false);
  }, [open, selectedIconCount]);
```

## [382] assistant

Found it. The `Enter`-inside-`.vertical-run` handler only fires for **collapsed** selections. When you toggle vertical mode, we explicitly preserve your selection across the wrapper's contents (so you can easily revert), so the selection is non-collapsed. Pressing Enter then falls through to the generic Enter branch, which does `range.deleteContents()` — wiping everything inside the vertical-run wrapper (i.e. all your glyphs).

Rotation isn't actually causal — it just changes the bounding rects enough that your drag-select catches the last glyph cleanly, exposing the bug. Without rotation you may have been ending up with a collapsed caret that took the safe branch.

Let me fix it: if the selection — collapsed or not — sits inside a `.vertical-run`, escape the run, place the caret after it, then insert the line break (no delete):

 I'm checking the `insertLineBreakAtRange` function and it looks safe, but I'm noticing `range.deleteContents()` on line 2478 — if the range is non-collapsed and spans the entire editor, that could wipe everything. Let me trace through what happens when toggling vertical mode: it wraps the selection in `.vertical-run`, then calls `selectContentsOf`...

Actually, I see the issue now. After wrapping, the selection becomes range-based and covers the wrapper's contents. When the user presses Enter, the check for a collapsed range fails because it's not collapsed, so we skip the vertical-run-specific branch and hit the regular Enter handler instead. That calls `deleteContents()` on the non-collapsed range, which deletes the wrapper's children — the glyphs themselves. So pressing Enter with a selection should delete it and insert a newline — that's standard. The bug is that after `toggleColumnMode` wraps the selection and calls `selectContentsOf(wrapper)`, hitting Enter deletes the wrapper's children (the glyphs), clearing that line. The user says "the whole editor is cleared," but it's probably just that line. I'm trying to figure out why this only happens when the last glyph is rotated — maybe the selection isn't being preserved the same way, or the rotation affects how the wrapper is structured. Let me mentally walk through the scenario: user adds four glyphs, rotates the last one, selects the line, toggles vertical mode, then hits Enter.

The code currently only handles collapsed selections inside vertical runs. **StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
    // Pressing Enter while the caret is inside a `.vertical-run` would
    // otherwise insert a line break in vertical writing mode, which renders
    // upright and visually overlaps the hieroglyphs in the run. Escape the
    // run first, then insert the line break at the new (horizontal) location.
    if (e.key === "Enter" && range.collapsed) {
      const enclosing = findEnclosingVerticalRun(range.startContainer, editor);
      if (enclosing) {
        e.preventDefault();
        placeCaretAfter(enclosing);
        const refreshed = getSelectionInEditor(editor);
        if (refreshed) {
          clearEmptyTypingSpanAtSelection();
          insertLineBreakAtRange(editor, refreshed.range);
        }
        return;
      }
    }
- NEW ---
    // Pressing Enter while the selection sits inside a `.vertical-run`
    // would otherwise either (a) insert a line break in vertical writing
    // mode — renders upright and overlaps the hieroglyphs in the run, or
    // (b) for a NON-collapsed selection, fall through to the generic
    // Enter branch and `range.deleteContents()` would wipe every glyph
    // in the run (the typical case right after toggling vertical mode,
    // because we deliberately preserve the selection across the wrapper's
    // contents so the user can revert). In both cases we want the same
    // outcome: jump out of the vertical-run, drop a clean line break,
    // and let the user type horizontally on the new line.
    if (e.key === "Enter") {
      const enclosingStart = findEnclosingVerticalRun(
        range.startContainer,
        editor,
      );
      const enclosingEnd = findEnclosingVerticalRun(
        range.endContainer,
        editor,
      );
      const enclosing = enclosingStart || enclosingEnd;
      if (enclosing) {
        e.preventDefault();
        placeCaretAfter(enclosing);
        const refreshed = getSelectionInEditor(editor);
        if (refreshed) {
          clearEmptyTypingSpanAtSelection();
          insertLineBreakAtRange(editor, refreshed.range);
        }
        return;
      }
    }
```

    const marker = document.createElement("span");
    marker.dataset.selectionBoundary = "true";

    const marker = document.createElement("span");
    marker.dataset.selectionBoundary = "true";
    marker.textContent = "\u200B";
    // The boundary is a caret-anchor that lives at the very start of the
    // editor when the first child is a glyph. It has to be:
    //   - zero visual width (its only character is U+200B which is itself
    //     zero-width — no extra width clamp needed),
    //   - hidden from copy / paste UI, and
    //   - tall enough that a caret placed inside it has a visible height.
    //
    // Earlier versions clamped `font-size: 0; line-height: 0;` here to
    // prevent the boundary from contributing to line height. The side
    // effect was catastrophic: when the user clicked in the empty space
    // before the first glyph, the browser snapped the caret inside this
    // span and rendered it at 0 px — completely invisible. Worse, any
    // character typed at that caret was inserted as text inside the
    // 0-px-tall span and stayed invisible too. Removing the font-size /
    // line-height overrides keeps the caret visible; the boundary still
    // renders as zero width because the ZWSP is zero-width, and the rest
    // of the line geometry comes from the adjacent glyph anyway.
    marker.style.cssText = `
      display: inline;
      color: transparent;
      user-select: text;
    `;
    editor.insertBefore(marker, firstMeaningfulChild);
  }, []);
```

      if (!target) {
        // Click is past the last glyph on this line.
        const last = inLine[inLine.length - 1];
        target = last.el;
        side = "after";
      }

      const parent = target.parentNode;
      if (parent) {
        const idx = Array.from(parent.childNodes).indexOf(target as ChildNode);
        if (idx >= 0) {
          node = parent;
          offset = side === "before" ? idx : idx + 1;

          // Special case: clicking the empty space before the first glyph
          // of the editor's first line. We have a leading
          // `data-selection-boundary` span there that exists precisely so
          // the caret has somewhere to land. Anchor INSIDE its text node
          // (at the end of its U+200B) instead of at (editor, idx) — at
          // an element-boundary offset Chrome would either snap the
          // caret somewhere unpredictable or render it 0px tall and the
          // user would think the click did nothing.
          if (side === "before" && parent === editor) {
            const prevSibling = target.previousSibling;
            if (
              prevSibling instanceof HTMLElement &&
              prevSibling.dataset.selectionBoundary === "true" &&
              prevSibling.firstChild &&
              prevSibling.firstChild.nodeType === Node.TEXT_NODE

## [402] assistant

Clean. Three coordinated fixes:

## Root cause

The leading `data-selection-boundary` span — the invisible caret-anchor we add when the editor's first child is a glyph — had `font-size: 0; line-height: 0;` on it. When `placeCaretAtPoint` resolved a click in the empty space before the first glyph, Chrome snapped the visible caret *inside* that span, where it rendered at zero height (invisible) and any character you typed went into the transparent-coloured 0-px-tall span (so typing appeared to do nothing). Then Enter would fire `insertLineBreakAtRange` (which correctly escapes the boundary), but every subsequent type/delete cycle accumulated stray empty text nodes that the line-spacer enforcement re-armoured with fresh spacers on each input — that's where the "extra empty line that won't go away" came from.

## Fixes (all in `MainContent.tsx`)

1. **Boundary span is now caret-friendly.** Dropped `font-size: 0; line-height: 0; display: inline-block; pointer-events: none;` and replaced with just `display: inline; color: transparent; user-select: text;`. The `\u200B` character is itself zero-width, so the boundary still has no visible footprint, but a caret placed inside it now inherits the editor's normal line-height and renders at full height.

2. **`placeCaretAtPoint` anchors directly into the boundary's text node** when the click lands in the empty space before the first glyph of the first line. That way the caret position is unambiguous instead of being at an element-boundary offset that Chrome would snap unpredictably.

3. **`normalizeEditorArtifacts` now promotes typed-into-boundary text** the same way it already does for line spacers: strip the ZWSP in place (preserving the caret-anchored text node), unwrap the boundary, and `ensureLeadingSelectionBoundary` will re-add a fresh empty boundary on the next pass if the first child is still a glyph. So even if the user does manage to type into a boundary, the character escapes immediately and stays visible.

4. **Per-line spacer enforcement now strips empty text nodes** from before a line's first real element when that element is a glyph/img. These empty text nodes were the residue of Backspace cycles inside spacer/boundary spans; without cleanup they kept tricking the spacer logic into "this line starts with a non-editable, add another spacer" on every single input — so each undo of a delete added a fresh empty line you couldn't remove.

Now: clicking the empty space before the first glyph plants a visible caret, typing produces visible text on the first line right before the glyphs, and Enter / type / delete cycles no longer accumulate phantom empty lines. Try the same scenario again.

## [410] assistant

Clean. Here's what I added:

## Root cause

When the caret sits at the very start of the editor (only the leading boundary span / line spacers / empty text nodes before it), `removeAdjacentSvgIcon(backward)` correctly returns false — there's nothing of ours to delete — and the keystroke falls through to **Chrome's native Backspace**. At a contentEditable's leading edge with an atomic inline-block (your first glyph) right next door, Chrome's "delete previous structural unit" walks past the zero-width spacer/boundary and eats the glyph itself. Hold Backspace and it peels off A, then B, then C until the line is empty.

## Fix

Added a `hasMeaningfulContentOnSide(editor, range, direction)` helper that walks from the caret toward the requested edge, treating `data-selection-boundary`, `data-editor-line-spacer`, and ZWSP-only/whitespace text nodes as **invisible** and any other element/text node as **real content**.

Then in `handleEditorKeyDown`, after the existing `removeAdjacentSvgIcon`/`removeManualLineBreakBeforeCaret` early-outs:

- `Backspace` + nothing real before the caret → `e.preventDefault()` and bail. Chrome never gets a chance to eat the next glyph.
- Symmetric guard for `Delete` at the very end of the editor (same Chrome quirk in reverse).

Try the same scenario again — clicking before the first glyph and holding Backspace should now be a no-op once the caret reaches the start; the glyphs stay put.

- OLD ---
  const normalizeEditorArtifacts = useCallback((editor: HTMLElement) => {
    // Hoist any line breaks / spacers that got trapped inside the hidden
    // selection-boundary span (e.g. if Enter was pressed with the caret inside
    // that span). They would otherwise be invisible and unreachable.
    Array.from(
      editor.querySelectorAll(
        '[data-selection-boundary="true"] br[data-editor-line-break="true"], [data-selection-boundary="true"] [data-editor-line-spacer="true"]',
      ),
    ).forEach((node) => {
      const boundary = (node as HTMLElement).closest(
        '[data-selection-boundary="true"]',
      );
      if (!boundary || !boundary.parentNode) return;
      boundary.parentNode.insertBefore(node, boundary.nextSibling);
    });
- NEW ---
  const normalizeEditorArtifacts = useCallback((editor: HTMLElement) => {
    // Strip browser-inserted placeholder <br> elements. Our editor only
    // ever creates `<br data-editor-line-break="true">`; any plain <br>
    // came from Chrome's contentEditable auto-fill — most often after a
    // Backspace empties a leading text node, Chrome drops a placeholder
    // <br> in to maintain the line. That ghost <br> renders as a visible
    // empty line above the glyphs that none of our key handlers
    // recognise as a line break, so the user can't delete it.
    Array.from(editor.querySelectorAll("br")).forEach((br) => {
      const el = br as HTMLElement;
      if (el.dataset?.editorLineBreak === "true") return;
      el.remove();
    });

    // Hoist any line breaks / spacers that got trapped inside the hidden
    // selection-boundary span (e.g. if Enter was pressed with the caret inside
    // that span). They would otherwise be invisible and unreachable.
    Array.from(
      editor.querySelectorAll(
        '[data-selection-boundary="true"] br[data-editor-line-break="true"], [data-selection-boundary="true"] [data-editor-line-spacer="true"]',
      ),
    ).forEach((node) => {
      const boundary = (node as HTMLElement).closest(
        '[data-selection-boundary="true"]',
      );
      if (!boundary || !boundary.parentNode) return;
      boundary.parentNode.insertBefore(node, boundary.nextSibling);
    });
```

## [449] assistant

Clean (only the pre-existing warning).

Two fixes in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:

1. **Strip browser-inserted placeholder `<br>`** at the top of `normalizeEditorArtifacts`. Chrome's contentEditable drops a plain `<br>` in to "preserve" the line whenever a leading text node is emptied by Backspace. Since it isn't tagged with `data-editor-line-break`, none of our handlers (per-line normalization, `removeManualLineBreakBeforeCaret`, or the new `hasMeaningfulContentOnSide` Backspace guard) recognise it as a line break — so it shows up as an undeletable empty line above the glyphs. Now we sweep all plain `<br>`s on every input and keep only the ones we tagged ourselves.

2. **Re-anchor the caret onto the spacer** when the per-line cleanup removes the empty text node that was holding the caret. Previously the caret got orphaned, which is exactly what triggers Chrome to insert that placeholder `<br>` in the first place — so this kills the root cause too, not just the symptom.

After these, the type-one-char-then-delete cycle leaves the editor in `[spacer, A, B, C, D]` with the caret cleanly parked at the end of the spacer's ZWSP — same as the initial state.

thanks. btw whats the name of the editor we are using? is it custom or a library
</user_query>

---

## [451] assistant

It's fully custom. There's no rich-text library involved — `MainContent.tsx` is just a `<div contentEditable>` plus a couple thousand lines of hand-rolled DOM manipulation (selection ranges, `caretPositionFromPoint`, custom `<br>`/spacer/boundary spans, ZWSP caret anchors, custom hit-testing in `placeCaretAtPoint`, custom drag-selection in `handleEditorMouseMove`, custom history snapshots, etc.).

You'll see in `package.json` there's no Slate, Lexical, ProseMirror, TipTap, Draft, Quill, or Editor.js. Everything we've been fixing — the boundary span, the line spacers, the merged-glyph layout, the Backspace guards, the placeholder-`<br>` stripping we just added — is all bespoke logic on top of the browser's native contentEditable.

That's also *why* it's been so painful: every browser quirk (Chrome's auto-`<br>` insertion, atomic inline-block selection, caret placement around inline-blocks) has to be intercepted manually. A library like Lexical or ProseMirror would normally abstract this by maintaining its own document model and rendering it, instead of letting the browser mutate the DOM directly. Worth keeping in mind if the bug list keeps growing — at some point a model-backed editor becomes cheaper than patching contentEditable forever.

 Let me check what editor we're using in the codebase.

---

## [452] user

<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 5:02 AM (UTC+3)</timestamp>
<user_query>
wow. do u think bringing in some editor would be better and could u be able to easily implement the same features there?
</user_query>

---

## [453] assistant

Honestly, yes — for the long term. But "easy" needs nuance.

## What an editor framework would actually solve

Lexical (Meta) or ProseMirror/TipTap maintain their own document model and render *to* the DOM. The browser never gets to mutate it freely, which kills entire categories of bugs we keep firefighting:

- Chrome auto-inserting `<br>` placeholders (today's fix)
- Caret jumping around atomic inline-blocks
- "Backspace at start eats the first glyph"
- Phantom empty text nodes accumulating
- Typing inside transparent boundary spans
- Selection ranges spanning weird invisible elements

All of those just… don't happen, because the framework's reconciler decides what the DOM looks like, not contentEditable.

## What it does NOT solve

The hieroglyph-specific stuff is still on us either way:

- Merged-group layout geometry (`computeMergedLayout`)
- Cartouche frame + padding + clone scaling
- Per-selection vertical writing mode
- Glyph rotation transforms
- PNG rasterization for clipboard
- Drag-to-select with line-aware snapping (somewhat — frameworks help, but glyphs are still atomic)

So maybe 30–40% of `MainContent.tsx` is "fighting the browser" — that goes away. The remaining 60–70% is "rendering hieroglyphs correctly" — that has to be re-implemented as custom node types in the new framework.

## My recommendation

**Lexical** would be my pick: React-native, fast, headless, and its custom node API (`DecoratorNode` for atomic React-rendered children) is a near-perfect fit for SVG glyphs and merged groups. ProseMirror is more powerful but heavier and less React-friendly. Slate has a worse track record with selection/atoms — exactly our pain points.

**But timing matters.** You said this is your first solo client contract. I would *not* propose a rewrite mid-delivery — too much regression risk for a deadline. Concrete plan:

1. **Now (this contract)**: Finish the bug list and ship. The custom editor works; it just bleeds.
2. **After delivery**: Pitch a v2 milestone — "stability + future-proofing." Quote a Lexical migration as ~2–4 weeks of work depending on how the rotation/cartouche/merged-group nodes shake out. Frame it as "every new feature gets cheaper after this."
3. **Optional middle ground**: If the client keeps requesting selection/typing fixes, the migration pays for itself faster than they think.

If you want, I can sketch what a Lexical port of the glyph node + cartouche node would look like, just so you can see the shape of the work before deciding. No code changes — just a design sketch.

 The user is asking whether migrating to a model-based editor like Lexical or ProseMirror would be worthwhile, and I'm thinking through the trade-offs between sticking with the current ~7000 lines of custom contentEditable logic versus adopting an established framework.

I'm weighing what each framework offers—Lexical's modern React integration and speed, ProseMirror's powerful schema-driven approach, Slate's flexibility—against the reality that most of the hieroglyph-specific rendering (merged group layouts, cartouche padding, rotation transforms, vertical-run wrapping) would still need custom implementation regardless. The real wins would come from letting the framework handle caret placement, selection, undo/redo, copy/paste, and normalization, eliminating the constant browser quirk firefighting. The tradeoff is the effort required to port everything over and learn a new system.

Given the current deadline and paying client, I should recommend stabilizing the existing implementation rather than attempting a full migration now—the risk and time cost outweigh the immediate benefit. But this is worth planning as a post-launch refactor, because the bug surface area will only expand if we keep patching the current approach.

---

## [455] assistant

Yeah, it is. Some honest observations from the time I've spent in this file:

- **~7000 lines in one component.** No editor primitives, no separation between "model", "render", and "browser plumbing". Everything is reach-into-the-DOM-and-hope.
- **Three overlapping invisible-anchor systems** (`data-selection-boundary`, `data-editor-line-spacer`, raw ZWSPs in text nodes), each with their own normalization rules and special cases in 6+ handlers. They duplicate each other's purpose and constantly fight.
- **Layout encoded as inline styles** built from string concatenation — every merged group, cartouche, and rotation re-derives geometry from scratch instead of from a model.
- **Synchronous PNG rasterization on every resize** at 8× oversampling — the freeze you hit wasn't a leak, it was someone calling `canvas.toDataURL` in a tight loop on the main thread. That's not a bug, that's not understanding what `toDataURL` costs.
- **No document model.** State lives in the DOM, in React state, in refs, in `dataset` attributes, and in a parallel `selectedIcons` array — and they drift apart constantly. Half the bugs we've fixed are "these four sources of truth disagreed."
- **History is `innerHTML` snapshots.** Works, but means undo/redo can never be smarter than "blow it all away and re-parse."
- **Browser quirks treated as surprises.** Anyone who's built a contentEditable for more than a week knows Chrome inserts placeholder `<br>`s. There's no defense for that anywhere in the original code.

It's the classic contentEditable trap: it looks like you're 80% done in week one, then you spend the next year fixing edge cases that a real editor framework solved a decade ago. The previous devs almost certainly hit a deadline early, shipped, and then every new requirement made the foundation shakier.

The good news: you've inherited it at a point where the requirements are now clear (replace JSesh, specific feature set), so a Lexical rewrite would be scoped, not open-ended. The bad news is the next contractor after you will say the same thing about *our* fixes if no one ever rewrites the foundation.

Worth raising with the client when you have rapport: "the editor itself is the bottleneck on every feature you'll want next."

---

```
import React, { useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";

/**
 * The set of cartouche frame shapes the editor can render procedurally
 * (i.e. drawn with raw SVG paths from W/H, no external art assets).
 *
 *   - "oval"      : classic Egyptian cartouche — flat sides, semi-circular
 *                    ends, single bar at the terminating end.
 *   - "rectangle" : sharp-cornered rectangle with the same terminating bar.
 *                    Useful as a serekh-like neutral frame.
 *   - "rounded"   : rectangle with a small corner radius. A halfway house
 *                    between "oval" and "rectangle".
 *
 * To add ornate variants (palace-facade, decorated end caps, royal
 * cartouches with a sun-disk on top, etc.) we need real SVG art for the
 * frame plus per-shape padding rules — `buildCartoucheSvg` would dispatch
 * to a per-shape renderer that knows where the inner content area sits.
 */
export type CartoucheShape = "oval" | "rectangle" | "rounded";

interface IProps {
  selectedIconCount: number;
  /**
   * Wrap the current selection in a cartouche of `shape`. Called once per
   * picker click; the picker itself does not track which shape was chosen
   * last (each wrap is an independent decision).
   */
  onCartoucheWrap: (shape: CartoucheShape) => void;
}

interface ShapeOption {
  id: CartoucheShape;
  label: string;
  description: string;
}

const OPTIONS: ShapeOption[] = [
  {
    id: "oval",
    label: "Oval",
    description: "Classic Egyptian cartouche.",
  },
  {
    id: "rectangle",
    label: "Square",
    description: "Sharp-cornered frame.",
  },
  {
    id: "rounded",
    label: "Rounded",
    description: "Rectangle with soft corners.",
  },
];

/**

 * `buildCartoucheSvg` in MainContent.tsx so the toolbar preview always
 * matches what gets rendered in the editor.

  shape,
  size = 44,
}) => {

  const H = size;
  const stroke = "currentColor";

    const pad = H * 0.0208;
    const curveAnchor = H * 0.4167;
    const ctrlExtend = H * 0.1181;
    const leftAnchorX = Math.min(curveAnchor, W / 2);
    const rightAnchorX = Math.max(W - curveAnchor, W / 2);
    const yTop = pad;
    const yBot = H - pad;
    const barX = W - Math.max(1, H * 0.03);
    return (
      <svg width={W} height={H} viewBox={`0 0 ${W} ${H}`}>
        <path

- NEW ---
  // Read the cartouche shape persisted on a wrapper. Falls back to "oval"
  // for any wrapper created before the shape feature shipped (those have
  // no `data-cartouche-shape` attribute), so legacy documents continue to
  // re-render as the original Egyptian cartouche on resize / vertical-flip.
  const getCartoucheShape = (el: HTMLElement): CartoucheShape => {
    const raw = el.dataset.cartoucheShape;
    if (raw === "rectangle" || raw === "rounded" || raw === "oval") {
      return raw;
    }
    return "oval";
  };

## [488] assistant

Done. Here's what shipped and what I'd need from you to expand it.

## What's new

**`CartouchePicker.tsx`** — a popover component (mirrors the Rotate popover's structure: outside-click close, Esc close, auto-close on deselect, `data-keep-selection` so it doesn't drop the editor selection).

Clicking the cartouche button now opens a popover with a 3-up grid of shapes, each shown as a real SVG preview (geometry computed identically to the actual editor frame, so what you see is what you get):

- **Oval** — current Egyptian cartouche (default).
- **Square** — sharp rectangle with the same terminating bar.
- **Rounded** — rectangle with a small corner radius and the same bar.

Click a shape → wraps the selection → closes.

**Frame rebuilds preserve the shape.** Stored as `data-cartouche-shape` on the wrapper and threaded through both rebuild paths in `MainContent.tsx`:

- Resize (`applyIconSizeToElement`) — picks up the shape via `getCartoucheShape(el)`.
- Vertical-flip relayout — same.

Legacy cartouches without the attribute fall back to `"oval"`, so existing documents render exactly as before.

## What I need from you for fancier shapes

The three shapes above are *procedural* — they draw themselves from `(W, H, vertical)`. For ornate cartouche variants (palace-facade serekh, royal cartouches with a sun-disk above, asymmetric frames with art on one side, etc.), I need from you, per shape:

1. **An SVG path or asset** for the frame at a known reference size (any size — I scale it). Either:
   - Inline SVG paths (preferred — sharp at all sizes), or
   - PNG/SVG file I can drop into the project.
2. **The "content area" rectangle** in the same coordinate system: `(x, y, width, height)` for where the inner glyphs should sit. This is the bit that varies — e.g. a frame with art on the right needs the inner area shifted left.
3. **Whether vertical orientation needs a different asset** (a serekh-with-falcon-on-top, for example, can't just be rotated; it'd need a separately drawn vertical version).
4. **A short label** for the picker.

Once you have those, the integration is one entry per shape:

```ts
type CartoucheShape = "oval" | "rectangle" | "rounded" | "your-new-shape";
```

…plus a case in `buildCartoucheSvg`'s shape switch that draws the frame and reports the inner content rect, plus an `OPTIONS` entry + preview in `CartouchePicker.tsx`. I'd be in and out in 10 minutes per shape if you hand me the assets and content-area rect.

## [495] assistant

It's drawn entirely in code — there's no SVG asset file. It's procedurally generated from the wrapper's `width × height` every time a cartouche is created or resized.

The geometry lives in `buildCartoucheSvg` in `MainContent.tsx`:

```3346:3361:src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx
    if (shape === "oval") {
      if (vertical) {
        const pad = W * 0.0208;
        const curveAnchor = W * 0.4167;
        const ctrlExtend = W * 0.1181;

        const topAnchorY = Math.min(curveAnchor, H / 2);
        const bottomAnchorY = Math.max(H - curveAnchor, H / 2);
        const xLeft = pad;
        const xRight = W - pad;

        const topCurve = document.createElementNS(svgNS, "path");
        topCurve.setAttribute(
          "d",
          `M ${xLeft} ${topAnchorY} C ${xLeft} ${-ctrlExtend} ${xRight} ${-ctrlExtend} ${xRight} ${topAnchorY}`,
        );
```

It builds 4-5 SVG `<path>` elements:

1. **Top/left curve** — a cubic Bézier that arcs out past the box edge (control points at `-ctrlExtend`) and back, creating the rounded end cap.
2. **Bottom/right curve** — symmetric arc on the opposite end.
3. **Two flat sides** — straight lines connecting the two curves' anchor points (only drawn when the cartouche is long enough that the curves don't meet).
4. **End bar** — the perpendicular line at the terminating end (right edge for horizontal, bottom for vertical). This is the hieroglyphic "name terminator" stroke.

All numbers are ratios of `W` or `H` (`0.0208`, `0.4167`, `0.1181`, `0.03`), so it scales cleanly at any size. Built fresh each time the cartouche is resized or flipped between horizontal/vertical orientation — that's why you'll see `buildCartoucheSvg(...)` called in three places: `wrapInCartouche`, `applyIconSizeToElement`, and the orientation-flip path in `relayoutAllMergedIcons`.

The toolbar button preview in `CartouchePicker.tsx` and the small icon next to the button use the same path math, just at toolbar size — that's why they always look identical to what gets rendered in the editor.

## [502] assistant

Yep, it was procedurally generated from the very first commit (`4390580 init`) — exactly the same Bézier math as today. There's no original SVG asset anywhere; whoever wrote it just hand-coded the curves. The `buildCartoucheSvg` function I extended is unchanged from day one apart from the `shape` switch I wrapped around it.

So for adding more cartouches, you actually have two options — and which one you pick depends on the shape:

## Option A — procedural (like the current oval)

Good for **simple geometric frames** that scale cleanly at any size: rectangles, hexagons, ovals with different aspect ratios, frames with one square end and one round, etc.

You'd just describe the shape to me ("rectangle with the bar on the bottom-right corner instead of the right side", "rounded rectangle but only top corners are rounded", "double-line border") and I add a case to the shape switch. No assets needed.

## Option B — SVG-asset-backed (for ornate shapes)

Necessary for anything with **real artwork**: serekh palace facades, decorated end caps, royal cartouches with a sun-disk on top, painted/textured frames. These can't be expressed with a few Béziers.

For these I need from you:

1. **An SVG file** (or the inline `<svg>` markup) — open it in a text editor and paste the contents. Format-wise, the cleanest thing is:
   - One outer `<svg viewBox="0 0 100 100">` (any reference dimensions are fine, but pick something simple like 100×100 or the natural pixel size of your asset)
   - Just `<path d="...">`, `<rect>`, `<line>`, etc. inside — no embedded raster images, no scripts
   - All strokes/fills set on the elements (not in a `<style>` block — easier to recolor)

2. **The content area rectangle** — where the glyphs sit *inside* that frame, in the same coordinate space. Just four numbers: `(x, y, width, height)`. For the current oval at 100×100 the inner rect is roughly `(18, 8, 64, 84)` — flat sides, padded ends, leaving room for the bar at the right.

3. **Whether the vertical version needs separate art** — if your new shape is asymmetric (e.g. "art on the right side" like you mentioned), rotating 90° won't produce a sensible vertical version, so you'd need to draw a vertical variant too.

If you give me an SVG and I can't tell where the content area should be, I'll ask. But the easiest workflow for you would be to draw the frame in something like Figma/Inkscape, draw a temporary inner rectangle showing where the glyphs should sit, export as SVG, and send me the file — I'll strip the inner rectangle and turn its bounds into the content rect.

A small note: the *bar* in the current cartouche is the hieroglyphic "name terminator." If your new shapes still want it, mention where it should go in your shape; if they don't (e.g. a serekh has its own internal structure), say so and I won't draw one.

 The cartouche has been procedurally generated since the original commit with the same Bézier curve logic, and I just added the shape parameter to support rectangles and rounded variants. The user wants practical guidance on extending this, so I should walk them through the workflow for adding new cartouche styles.

## [518] assistant

I found JSesh's actual cartouche implementation. Good news: JSesh draws everything procedurally too (Java2D paths), no SVG assets — so I can port the math directly. They have **4 enclosure types**, all driven by the same MdC `< >` / `h>` syntax mentioned in the standard:

- **Oval** — what we have. (`NormalCartoucheDrawer`)
- **Hwt** — flat rectangle with small square "tabs" at the corners. Used for sacred enclosures. (`HwtDrawer`)
- **Serekh** — palace-facade frame; one end is a fortified wall with vertical cornices and rectangular recesses. Used for early royal Horus names. (`SerekhDrawer`)
- **Enclosure** — full fortress wall with corner bastions and evenly-distributed intermediate bastions on every side. (`EnclosureDrawer`)

Let me port all three to our editor.

 I'm seeing that JSesh supports four different enclosure types for hieroglyphic cartouches. Beyond the cartouche style we've already implemented with its curved Bézier ends and optional knot detail, there's also the hwt variant which uses a flat-topped rectangle with small square tabs protruding inward at each end.

The serekh is more ornate—a rectangle decorated with a palace-facade pattern of vertical cornices and horizontal recesses along the sides, typically used for Horus names. The enclosure type is the most elaborate, featuring a fortress-wall aesthetic with corner bastions and additional bastions distributed along the perimeter for sacred place names.

Since JSesh generates all of these procedurally rather than using pre-made SVG assets, I can replicate the same approach with math-driven paths. I'm planning to implement all three additional types—hwt, serekh, and enclosure—as procedural shapes, starting with the simpler hwt and working up to the more complex fortress-wall enclosure. /**

 *
 *   - "oval"      : classic Egyptian cartouche — flat sides, semi-circular

/**
 * The set of cartouche frame shapes the editor can render procedurally
 * (i.e. drawn with raw SVG paths from W/H, no external art assets).
 *
 * Mirrors JSesh's four enclosure types from the Manuel de Codage standard
 * (see the `cartouche/` package in JSesh). Each one is hand-drawn from
 * W/H ratios so they scale crisply at any size.
 *
 *   - "oval"      : classic Egyptian cartouche — flat sides, semi-circular
 *                    bezier loops at the ends, single perpendicular bar
 *                    at the terminating end (the "shen" / name terminator).
 *                    Used for royal names. Maps to MdC `< >`.
 *   - "hwt"       : flat-sided rectangle with two small inset squares at
 *                    each corner of the cap. The Hwt-sign denotes a
 *                    sacred enclosure (e.g. a temple). Maps to MdC `h>`.
 *   - "serekh"    : palace-facade frame — one end is a fortified wall
 *                    with vertical cornice lines and rectangular recesses.
 *                    Used for the early royal Horus name format.
 *   - "enclosure" : rectangle ringed with corner bastions and evenly
 *                    distributed intermediate bastions. Used for very
 *                    sacred place / monument names.
 */
export type CartoucheShape = "oval" | "hwt" | "serekh" | "enclosure";
```

```diff
- OLD ---
  // Read the cartouche shape persisted on a wrapper. Falls back to "oval"
  // for any wrapper created before the shape feature shipped (those have
  // no `data-cartouche-shape` attribute), so legacy documents continue to
  // re-render as the original Egyptian cartouche on resize / vertical-flip.
  const getCartoucheShape = (el: HTMLElement): CartoucheShape => {
    const raw = el.dataset.cartoucheShape;
    if (raw === "rectangle" || raw === "rounded" || raw === "oval") {
      return raw;
    }
    return "oval";
  };
- NEW ---
  // Read the cartouche shape persisted on a wrapper. Falls back to "oval"
  // for any wrapper created before the shape feature shipped (those have
  // no `data-cartouche-shape` attribute), so legacy documents continue to
  // re-render as the original Egyptian cartouche on resize / vertical-flip.
  const getCartoucheShape = (el: HTMLElement): CartoucheShape => {
    const raw = el.dataset.cartoucheShape;
    if (
      raw === "hwt" ||
      raw === "serekh" ||
      raw === "enclosure" ||
      raw === "oval"
    ) {
      return raw;
    }
    return "oval";
  };
```

      // with a small "knot" square inset at each cap corner, marking
      // the enclosure as a sacred / temple boundary. Geometry is
      // symmetric in both orientations: caps at the start and end,
      // body fills the middle.
      const refPerp = vertical ? W : H;
      const refLong = vertical ? H : W;
      const capLen = Math.max(2, refPerp * 0.12);
      // Inset square dimension (jsesh's `getHwtSquareSize`). Drawn at
      // the inside corners of each cap.
      const knot = Math.max(1.5, refPerp * 0.18);

      if (vertical) {
        const xLeft = 0.5;
        const xRight = W - 0.5;
        const yTop = 0.5;
        const yBot = H - 0.5;
        const yStartCap = capLen;
        const yEndCap = H - capLen;

        // Body: two long verticals running the inner length.
        const left = document.createElementNS(svgNS, "path");
        left.setAttribute(
          "d",
          `M ${xLeft} ${yStartCap} L ${xLeft} ${yEndCap}`,
        );
        left.setAttribute("style", strokeStyle);
        svg.appendChild(left);
        const right = document.createElementNS(svgNS, "path");
        right.setAttribute(
          "d",
          `M ${xRight} ${yStartCap} L ${xRight} ${yEndCap}`,
        );
        right.setAttribute("style", strokeStyle);
        svg.appendChild(right);

        // Start cap (top). Flat top + two side joins + a knot square
        // 

_... [truncated 19705 chars] ..._

## [529] assistant

Done. The picker now offers all four enclosure types JSesh ships, all ported from JSesh's actual Java geometry (no SVG assets needed — same procedural approach the original cartouche uses):

- **Oval** — classic Egyptian cartouche (royal name).
- **Hwt** — flat rectangular frame with small corner-knot squares; sacred / temple enclosure.
- **Serekh** — palace-facade pattern (cornice lines + recessed niches) on the start end, Hwt-style cap on the other; the Horus name format used before cartouches.
- **Enclosure** — rectangle ringed with filled corner bastions plus evenly-distributed intermediate bastions on every side; fortress-wall style enclosure for monuments / sacred place names.

Three things worth knowing:

1. **Both orientations work.** Each shape has its own horizontal and vertical implementation that mirrors JSesh's `drawHorizontal` / `drawVertical` logic, so flipping a frame to vertical mode rebuilds it correctly.

2. **The picker preview is the source of truth.** Every preview is drawn with the *exact* same math as the editor frame — if a shape looks weird in the preview, it'll look the same way in the editor, and fixing one fixes the other.

3. **Geometry constants** (`cap length`, `bastion depth`, `facade length`, etc.) are all ratios of the cartouche's perpendicular dimension, so frames scale crisply at any size and the inner content padding (`PAD_LONG` / `PAD_PERP` in `wrapInCartouche`) stays compatible across all four shapes.

If after testing something looks off — e.g. the Hwt knot squares sit on the wrong corner, the serekh facade is too wide, or the enclosure bastions are too thick — those are all single-line tweaks (just changing the `0.12` / `0.18` / `0.06` / `0.1` ratios near the top of each shape's branch in `buildCartoucheSvg`). Let me know what to adjust.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/CartouchePicker.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
/**
 * The set of cartouche frame shapes the editor can render procedurally
 * (i.e. drawn with raw SVG paths from W/H, no external art assets).
 *
 * Mirrors JSesh's four enclosure types from the Manuel de Codage standard
 * (see the `cartouche/` package in JSesh). Each one is hand-drawn from
 * W/H ratios so they scale crisply at any size.
 *
 *   - "oval"      : classic Egyptian cartouche — flat sides, semi-circular
 *                    bezier loops at the ends, single perpendicular bar
 *                    at the terminating end (the "shen" / name terminator).
 *                    Used for royal names. Maps to MdC `< >`.
 *   - "hwt"       : flat-sided rectangle with two small inset squares at
 *                    each corner of the cap. The Hwt-sign denotes a
 *                    sacred enclosure (e.g. a temple). Maps to MdC `h>`.
 *   - "serekh"    : palace-facade frame — one end is a fortified wall
 *                    with vertical cornice lines and rectangular recesses.
 *                    Used for the early royal Horus name format.
 *   - "enclosure" : rectangle ringed with corner bastions and evenly
 *                    distributed intermediate bastions. Used for very
 *                    sacred place / monument names.
 */
export type CartoucheShape = "oval" | "hwt" | "serekh" | "enclosure";
- NEW ---
/**
 * The set of cartouche frame shapes the editor can render procedurally
 * (i.e. drawn with raw SVG paths from W/H, no external art assets).
 *
 *   - "oval" : classic Egyptian cartouche — flat sides, semi-circular
 *               bezier loops at the ends, single perpendicular bar at
 *               the terminating end (the "shen" / name terminator).
 *
 * To add a new variant, extend this union and add a matching branch in
 * `buildCartoucheSvg` (MainContent.tsx) and a `ShapePreview` case here.
 */
export type CartoucheShape = "oval";
```

## [559] assistant

Now cap history. Let me find and update the commitHistory function:

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  const commitHistory = (mode: "push" | "replace" = "push") => {
    if (isApplyingHistoryRef.current) return;

    const entry = captureHistoryEntry();
    if (!entry) return;

    const current = historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current];
    if (current?.html === entry.html) {
      historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current] = entry;
      return;
    }

    if (mode === "replace" && historyIndexRef.current >= 0) {
      historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current] = entry;
      return;
    }

    const nextHistory = historyRef.current.slice(
      0,
      historyIndexRef.current + 1,
    );
    nextHistory.push(entry);
    historyRef.current = nextHistory;
    historyIndexRef.current = nextHistory.length - 1;
  };
- NEW ---
  // Cap the undo stack to bound memory. Each entry is the editor's full
  // sanitized innerHTML, which for a doc with many cartouches / merged
  // groups can be hundreds of KB (inline SVG paths). Without a cap, a
  // long editing session quietly accumulates tens of MB in this array
  // alone. 100 entries is more than enough headroom for normal Ctrl+Z
  // usage while keeping worst-case retention bounded.
  const MAX_HISTORY = 100;

  const commitHistory = (mode: "push" | "replace" = "push") => {
    if (isApplyingHistoryRef.current) return;

    const entry = captureHistoryEntry();
    if (!entry) return;

    const current = historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current];
    if (current?.html === entry.html) {
      historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current] = entry;
      return;
    }

    if (mode === "replace" && historyIndexRef.current >= 0) {
      historyRef.current[historyIndexRef.current] = entry;
      return;
    }

    const nextHistory = historyRef.current.slice(
      0,
      historyIndexRef.current + 1,
    );
    nextHistory.push(entry);

    // FIFO-evict the oldest entries when we exceed the cap. The user
    // loses the ability to undo past that horizon, which matches the
    // behaviour of every other text editor with a bounded undo stack.
    if (nextHistory.length > MAX_HISTORY) {
      nextHistory.splice(0, nextHistory.length - MAX_HISTORY);
    }

    historyRef.current = nextHistory;
    historyIndexRef.current = nextHistory.length - 1;
  };
```

- OLD ---
  const cachePngForSvgIcon = (wrapper: HTMLElement) => {
- NEW ---
  // Hard cap on cached PNGs. Each entry is a base64 data-URL that can be
  // 30-150 KB depending on glyph complexity (and several hundred KB for
  // cartouches with many inner glyphs). The cache is keyed by data-id and
  // pruned in `flushPngCacheQueue` whenever the editor changes, but on
  // workflows where the user mainly *reads* / selects (no editor mutation)
  // the prune never fires and the cache grows unbounded as undo/redo and
  // relayout cycles produce fresh ids. The cap is enforced FIFO via Map
  // insertion order.
  const MAX_PNG_CACHE = 200;

  const setPngCacheEntry = (id: string, dataUrl: string) => {
    const cache = pngCacheRef.current;
    if (cache.has(id)) {
      cache.delete(id); // bump to most-recent slot
    } else if (cache.size >= MAX_PNG_CACHE) {
      const oldest = cache.keys().next().value;
      if (oldest !== undefined) cache.delete(oldest);
    }
    cache.set(id, dataUrl);
  };

  const cachePngForSvgIcon = (wrapper: HTMLElement) => {

The selection logic like justStartedDrag detection has to stay synchronous though, since it affects state directly — I'll only throttle the expensive DOM operations that follow.

  const handleEditorMouseMove = (e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLDivElement>) => {
    const start = pointerDownPointRef.current;

    }

    if (!isPointerDraggingRef.current) return;
    if (e.buttons !== 1) return;

    // ----- Glyph-aware drag selection -----
    //
    // The browser's native drag-selection treats each glyph as an atom
    // and only flips focus to the far side of the atom once the cursor
    // crosses its *midpoint*. With 30-40px glyphs that's perceptibly
    // laggy, and in the gap between two glyphs the focus can even flip
    // back, dropping the previously-selected glyph until the cursor
    // reaches the next one.
    //

    //

  // The drag-selection step iterates every top-level glyph and calls
  // `getBoundingClientRect()` on each. With many glyphs and a fast

  // of forced layouts per second plus a `querySelectorAll` allocation

  const dragSelectRafRef = useRef<number | null>(null);
  const dragSelectLatestRef = useRef<{
    cursorX: number;
    cursorY: number;
  } | null>(null);

  const runDragSelectStep = useCallback(() => {
    dragSelectRafRef.current = null;
    const start = pointerDownPointRef.current;
    if (!start) return;
    if (!isPointerDraggingRef.current) return;
    const latest = dragSelectLatestRef.current;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return;

    const sel = window.getSelection();

## [584] assistant

Clean. Here's what I changed and why:

The custom editor was bleeding memory in a few places that compound during normal editing:

**1. The `selectionchange` handler was the worst offender.** Every caret blink, every keypress, every mouse step during a drag fired this handler and called `range.cloneContents()` — which deep-clones the entire current selection (every glyph's inline SVG path data) into a fresh `DocumentFragment`. With a Ctrl+A over a doc full of cartouches that's several MB allocated and thrown at the GC dozens of times per second. Now it:
- Bails immediately for a collapsed range (the common case — caret is just moving),
- Replaces `cloneContents()` with `range.intersectsNode()` against the live `.svg-icon` nodes (no allocation, returns the real elements directly), and
- Coalesces rapid events through one `requestAnimationFrame` tick.

**2. `handleEditorMouseMove` did per-event DOM thrashing.** It ran `querySelectorAll(".svg-icon")` and called `getBoundingClientRect()` on every glyph for each `mousemove` (100-200 events/sec on a fast drag → hundreds of forced layouts/sec). Now coalesced through `requestAnimationFrame` so the work runs at most once per paint, and the cleanup cancels the pending tick on `mouseup`.

**3. `historyRef.current` was unbounded.** Each entry is the editor's full sanitized `innerHTML` (hundreds of KB once cartouches/merged groups are involved). A long session quietly accumulates tens of MB just in undo entries. Now capped at 100 entries with FIFO eviction.

**4. `pngCacheRef` was unbounded.** It's keyed by `data-id` and pruned in the flush queue, but only when the queue fires. In sessions with lots of relayout/merge churn (each generates fresh ids), stale entries piled up. Now capped at 200 entries with FIFO eviction via a `setPngCacheEntry` helper.

Files touched: `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`. `tsc -b` and `eslint` both clean (one pre-existing warning).

Try it in the same scenario — adding glyphs, rotating, grouping. The peak should land much closer to the original baseline.

## [593] assistant

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff

          onMouseDown={handleEditorMouseDown}

- NEW ---
          onMouseDown={handleEditorMouseDown}
          onMouseMove={handleEditorMouseMove}
          onMouseUp={handleEditorMouseUp}
          onClick={handleEditorClick}
          onContextMenu={(e) => {
            // Glyphs are atomic inline-block wrappers; right-clicking one
            // pops the browser's native menu (Copy / Save Image / Inspect)
            // which is noise — we have our own toolbar for everything the
            // user actually needs to do with a glyph. Suppress the native
            // menu only when the click is on or inside a glyph atom; plain
            // text right-click keeps the normal menu so spell-check, copy,
            // paste etc. still work.
            const target = e.target as HTMLElement | null;
            if (target?.closest(".svg-icon")) {
              e.preventDefault();
            }
          }}
```

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/menu/PalleteLoader.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
        onDragEnd={handlePaletteDragEnd}
        onMouseDown={() => startLongPress()}

        onTouchEnd={() => cancelLongPress()}
        onTouchMove={() => cancelLongPress()}
- NEW ---
        onDragEnd={handlePaletteDragEnd}
        onMouseDown={(e) => {
          // Long-press is a left-button gesture. Right-click would
          // otherwise schedule the transform popup *and* let Chrome
          // pop its native context menu — both fighting for the same
          // tile. Bail for any non-primary button.
          if (e.button !== 0) return;
          startLongPress();
        }}
        onMouseUp={() => cancelLongPress()}
        onTouchStart={() => startLongPress()}
        onTouchEnd={() => cancelLongPress()}
        onTouchMove={() => cancelLongPress()}
        onContextMenu={(e) => {
          // Suppress the browser's native context menu on palette
          // tiles. The tile is interactive (drag, click, long-press
          // for the transform popup), and the native menu's options
          // (Save Image / Inspect / etc.) are noise the user never
          // needs here.
          e.preventDefault();
        }}
```

## [661] assistant

 There's a critical bug where the editor becomes unresponsive after all content is deleted. The root cause appears to be a conflict between Chrome's placeholder `<br>` behavior and our normalization logic. When the editor empties completely, Chrome inserts a `<br>` to give the contentEditable something to render the caret in, but our normalize function strips it out because it doesn't have the `data-editor-line-break="true"` attribute. This leaves the editor truly empty with no way to place the caret, blocking all input. I need to either preserve Chrome's placeholder `<br>` when the editor is genuinely empty, or insert our own line break marker to maintain editability.

  const normalizeEditorArtifacts = useCallback((editor: HTMLElement) => {
    // Strip browser-inserted placeholder <br> elements. Our editor only
    // ever creates `<br data-editor-line-break="true">`; any plain <br>
    // came from Chrome's contentEditable auto-fill — most often after a
    // Backspace empties a leading text node, Chrome drops a placeholder
    // <br> in to maintain the line. That ghost <br> renders as a visible
    // empty line above the glyphs that none of our key handlers
    // recognise as a line break, so the user can't delete it.
    //
    // BUT — when the editor is *genuinely* empty (e.g. user selected all
    // and deleted, or just cleared the doc), Chrome NEEDS that
    // placeholder <br> to render a caret and accept input. Stripping it
    // here would leave a node-less contentEditable that swallows clicks
    // and key events until reload. So we only strip stray <br>s when the
    // editor still has meaningful content surrounding them; if removing
    // it would empty the editor, we keep one to keep the caret alive.
    const hasMeaningfulContent = (): boolean => {
      const walker = document.createTreeWalker(
        editor,
        NodeFilter.SHOW_ELEMENT | NodeFilter.SHOW_TEXT,
      );
      let node: Node | null = walker.nextNode();
      while (node) {
        if (node.nodeType === Node.ELEMENT_NODE) {
          const el = node as HTMLElement;
          if (el.tagName === "BR") {
            node = walker.nextNode();
      

_... [truncated 846 chars] ..._

## [668] assistant

Fixed in `normalizeEditorArtifacts`. The bug was the unconditional strip of placeholder `<br>` elements:

When you deleted everything, Chrome inserted a placeholder `<br>` so its `contentEditable` could render a caret and accept input. My normalize pass was tagging that as a "ghost from auto-fill" and removing it on every `onInput` — which left a node-less editor with nothing for the caret to anchor in, so clicks and keys became no-ops until reload.

The strip is now gated on `hasMeaningfulContent()` (anything beyond `<br>`s, the leading selection-boundary, and line-spacers). When the editor still has real content, we strip stray `<br>`s as before; when the editor is genuinely empty, we leave Chrome's placeholder alone so the caret keeps working.

    clone: HTMLElement,
    slotW: number,
    slotH: number,

## [694] assistant

Nesting is now supported. Three changes in `MainContent.tsx`:

1. **`readAspectRatios`** — when computing slot widths inside `computeMergedLayout`, recognize `.svg-icon.merged` and cartouche atoms and use their wrapper-level `origWidth/origHeight` (or explicit `style.width/height`) for AR. Previously it grabbed the first inner `<svg>`'s viewBox, which for a nested group was just one slot child — making the parent allocate a tiny slot for the entire nested group.

2. **`fitCloneIntoSlot`** — extended the cartouche transform-scale path to also cover merged groups. Both have absolutely-positioned children sized in pixels relative to the wrapper's natural size, so we keep the wrapper at its natural size and apply `transform: scale()` to scale the whole composite uniformly into the slot.

3. **`createMergedIcon` / `relayoutMergedIcon` / merged-resize path** — persist `dataset.origWidth` / `dataset.origHeight` on every merged wrapper whenever its layout is computed, so future fits (when this group becomes a slot in a parent group, or gets resized) can find its canonical natural dimensions.

You can now group a group with another glyph, two groups together, or any combination including cartouches inside — the inner composite scales as one unit into its slot in the new parent group.

## [743] assistant

Done. Here's what changed and why:

**New classification** (`MainContent.tsx`):
- `isSmallIcon(icon)`: a glyph is "small" iff `picture_size < 95` **and** its intrinsic aspect ratio sits in `[0.7, 1.4]`. The squareness gate is the key — it excludes horizontals (AR ≈ 2) and vertical staffs (AR ≈ 0.25) which often also carry `ps < 100` to mean "low/narrow", but those should still fill their slot in a group (the behavior you just confirmed works great).
- `getSmallIntrinsicSize(icon, baseSize)`: returns `(picture_size/100) × baseSize` on the dominant axis (AR-adjusted on the other), capped at `baseSize`.

**Vertical merge** (horizontal lines, glyphs stacked):
- All-same group → equal slot heights (`baseSize / n`). Two smalls each get a 50% slot; inside, the artwork stays at intrinsic size and is centered.
- Mixed group → smalls get a slot exactly as tall as their intrinsic height; the remaining height is split equally among non-smalls. So `small + horizontal` gives the small its natural size and lets the horizontal grow to absorb the rest of the quadrat.

**Horizontal merge** (vertical lines, glyphs side by side):
- Mirror of the above, on the width axis: smalls keep their intrinsic width; non-smalls share remaining width proportionally to their AR.

**`fitCloneIntoSlot`**:
- New early-return branch when `isSmallIcon(clone)` is true: positions the clone at its intrinsic `(w, h)` centered inside the slot instead of stretching to fill.

This propagates automatically to creation (`createMergedIcon`), in-place orientation flip (`relayoutMergedIcon`), and resize (`applyIconSizeToElement`) because they all already funnel through `computeMergedLayout` + `fitCloneIntoSlot`.

If after testing the threshold feels off (e.g. some signs you consider small slip through, or full signs get misclassified), tell me roughly what `picture_size` values your backend uses for each category and I'll tighten `SMALL_PS_MAX` / the AR window.

## [747] assistant

Clean. Here's what changed and why this should work where the previous attempt didn't:

**Old detector** rejected your small icon because it required the *intrinsic AR* to sit in `[0.7, 1.4]`. A small mark with visible 50% × 30% has artwork AR ≈ 1.67 — too wide for that gate, so it was treated as if it were a "horizontal low" sign and stretched to fill the slot.

**New detector** computes the **visible artwork box** instead:

1. Run the icon through `getIconLayoutDimensions(ps, baseSize, arW, arH, false)` to get the wrapper's CSS box (same path that produces the in-line rendering).
2. Apply `preserveAspectRatio: meet` math against the artwork's tight-viewBox AR to find how much of that wrapper the actual ink occupies.
3. If **both** axes of that visible box land below `baseSize * 0.85`, it's small.

That separates the categories cleanly:

| Sign type | ps | AR | Visible artwork | Small? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Full | 100 | 1 | 100% × 100% | no |
| Horizontal low | 100 | 2 | 100% × 50% | no (width full) |
| Vertical / staff | 100 | 0.25 | 25% × 100% | no (height full) |
| Small (your case) | 50 | 1.67 | 50% × 30% | **yes** |

And `getSmallIntrinsicSize` now returns that same visible box, so a small icon inside a merged group renders at **exactly** the dimensions it has in regular text — no more "fills the width of the bigger neighbor".

Worth re-testing the four cases together to be sure:
- single small inserted in a line (should look unchanged)
- small + full grouped (small stays tiny, full takes the rest)
- two smalls grouped (each gets 50% slot, small artwork centered inside)
- two horizontals grouped, two verticals grouped, full + horizontal grouped (should all behave exactly like before — none of these qualify as small)

If anything in the second list looks wrong, that means a non-small category accidentally falls below the 85% threshold on both axes, and I'll tune the threshold or add a per-category guard.

- NEW ---
  // Flatten a merged wrapper into its constituent leaf glyphs so that
  // re-grouping an existing group with other items produces ONE flat
  // group instead of a group-of-groups. Cartouches are treated as
  // atomic (a cartouche with inner glyphs is still a single sign).
  //
  // A merged wrapper's children are each a positioning <span>; that
  // span's firstElementChild is the cloned source glyph (which may
  // itself be a nested .merged wrapper, hence the recursion).
  const expandMergedToLeaves = (icon: Element): Element[] => {
    const el = icon as HTMLElement;
    if (!el.classList.contains("merged")) return [el];
    if (el.dataset.cartouche === "true") return [el];
    const out: Element[] = [];
    Array.from(el.children).forEach((slot) => {
      const inner = (slot as HTMLElement).firstElementChild;
      if (inner) out.push(...expandMergedToLeaves(inner));
    });
    return out.length > 0 ? out : [el];
  };

  const mergeGroup = () => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

## [754] assistant

Now wire the flattener into `mergeGroup` right before `createMergedIcon` is called:

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
    iconsToMerge.sort((a, b) =>
      a.compareDocumentPosition(b) & Node.DOCUMENT_POSITION_FOLLOWING ? -1 : 1,
    );

    const mergedWrapper = createMergedIcon(iconsToMerge);
    if (!mergedWrapper) return;

- NEW ---
    iconsToMerge.sort((a, b) =>
      a.compareDocumentPosition(b) & Node.DOCUMENT_POSITION_FOLLOWING ? -1 : 1,
    );

    // Flatten so re-grouping a group never produces a group-of-groups —
    // the source list still drives DOM removal, but the new merged
    // wrapper is built from the union of leaf glyphs across every
    // selected item (single glyphs pass through unchanged, cartouches
    // stay atomic).
    const flatLeaves = iconsToMerge.flatMap((ic) =>
      expandMergedToLeaves(ic),
    );
    if (flatLeaves.length < 2) return;

    const mergedWrapper = createMergedIcon(flatLeaves);
    if (!mergedWrapper) return;

    const first = iconsToMerge[0] as HTMLElement;
    first.parentNode?.insertBefore(mergedWrapper, first);
    iconsToMerge.forEach((ic) => (ic as HTMLElement).remove());
```

For the group button: any `.merged` in the selection is now expanded into its leaf glyphs (recursively, in case of nested groups) before the new wrapper is built. Cartouches stay atomic — they're a single sign even when they contain inner glyphs — and lone glyphs pass through unchanged. So:

- group + glyph → one flat group with all leaves
- group + group → one flat group with the union of both
- group + cartouche + glyph → flat group containing the leaves of the first group, the cartouche as an atom, and the glyph
- nested merged groups (if any sneak in) get fully flattened

You mentioned "2 more fixes" — what's the second one?
