- OLD ---
  const toggleColumnMode = () => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    const sel = window.getSelection();
    const hasSelection =
      !!editor &&
      !!sel &&
      sel.rangeCount > 0 &&
      !sel.getRangeAt(0).collapsed &&
      editor.contains(sel.getRangeAt(0).commonAncestorContainer);

    if (hasSelection && sel) {
      const range = sel.getRangeAt(0);
      const changed = toggleVerticalForSelection(range);
      if (changed) {
        // Merged groups inside the new (or removed) vertical-run need to
        // flip orientation so they stay perpendicular to the line.
        relayoutAllMergedIcons();
        resetTypingHistorySession();
        commitHistory("push");
      }
      editor?.focus();
      return;
    }

    const nextColumnMode = !columnMode;
    if (nextColumnMode) {
      const lastWrapper = wrapAllHieroglyphRuns();
      if (lastWrapper) placeCaretAfter(lastWrapper);
    } else {
      unwrapAllAutoHilo();
    }
    relayoutAllMergedIcons();
    setColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    commitHistory("push");
    editor?.focus();
  };
- NEW ---
  // Document-wide vertical-glyph switch. The button is a pure toggle:
  // pressing it wraps EVERY hieroglyph run in the document in a
  // `vertical-run.auto-hilo` span (so all glyphs render top-to-bottom),
  // while leaving plain text alone. Pressing again unwraps them.
  //
  // Selection is intentionally ignored — earlier we made this act per-
  // selection like Bold / Italic, but the client wanted a global
  // switch instead. Any helpers for selection-based wrapping
  // (`toggleVerticalForSelection` etc.) are kept in the file for
  // potential future use but no longer wired to this button.
  const toggleColumnMode = () => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    const nextColumnMode = !columnMode;
    if (nextColumnMode) {
      const lastWrapper = wrapAllHieroglyphRuns();
      if (lastWrapper) placeCaretAfter(lastWrapper);
    } else {
      unwrapAllAutoHilo();
    }
    // Merged groups need to flip orientation so they stay perpendicular
    // to the new line direction (horizontal stacks in vertical text,
    // vertical stacks in horizontal text).
    relayoutAllMergedIcons();
    setColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    commitHistory("push");
    editor?.focus();
  };
```

  // Wrap the current selection in a `.vertical-run`. If the selection is
  // already entirely contained in a vertical-run, unwrap that run instead
  // (toggle off). The selection is preserved across the toggle so the
  // user can re-click the button to reverse the change.
  const toggleVerticalForSelection = (range: Range): boolean => {
    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return false;

    const enclosingStart = findEnclosingVerticalRun(
      range.startContainer,
      editor,
    );
    const enclosingEnd = findEnclosingVerticalRun(range.endContainer, editor);

    if (enclosingStart && enclosingStart === enclosingEnd) {
      const target = enclosingStart;
      const firstChild = target.firstChild;
      const lastChild = target.lastChild;
      unwrapElement(target);
      if (firstChild && lastChild) {
        selectRangeOfSiblings(firstChild, lastChild);
      }
      return true;
    }

    try {
      const fragment = range.extractContents();
      // Strip nested vertical-run wrappers from the fragment so we don't
      // produce nested vertical sections that flip orientation back.
      fragment
        .querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>("span.vertical-run")
        .forEach((nested) => unwrapElement(nested));

      if (!fragment.firstChild) return false;

      const wrapper = document.createElement("span");
      wrapper.className = "vertical-run";
      wrapper.setAttribute("style", VERTICAL_RUN_CSS);
      wrapper.appendChild(fragment);

  // Set the current selection to span all contents of `el` (inclusive of
  // the first child's start through the last child's end). Used after
  // wrap/unwrap so the toggle is reversible without re-selecting.
  const selectContentsOf = (el: HTMLElement) => {
    const sel = window.getSelection();
    if (!sel) return;
    const first = el.firstChild;
    const last = el.lastChild;
    if (!first || !last) return;
    const newRange = document.createRange();
    newRange.setStartBefore(first);
    newRange.setEndAfter(last);
    sel.removeAllRanges();
    sel.addRange(newRange);
    savedRangeRef.current = newRange.cloneRange();
  };

  // Restore the selection to span the run of nodes from `from` (inclusive)
  // through `to` (inclusive), which must share the same parent. Used after
  // unwrapping a vertical-run so the previously-wrapped content stays
  // selected.
  const selectRangeOfSiblings = (from: Node, to: Node) => {
    const sel = window.getSelection();
    if (!sel) return;
    const newRange = document.createRange();
    newRange.setStartBefore(from);
    newRange.setEndAfter(to);
    sel.removeAllRanges();
    sel.addRange(newRange);
    savedRangeRef.current = newRange.cloneRange();
  };

## [784] assistant

Let me look at the horizontal-merge code and any column-mode-specific size handling:

 I'm looking at a rendering issue in vertical mode where grouped horizontal glyphs are appearing too short—they're taking up the full height slot but the actual artwork is compressed to the bottom with empty space above. The problem seems to be how the group's dimensions are being calculated when glyphs that are normally horizontal get repositioned side-by-side in a vertical layout.

When I trace through the math: two horizontal glyphs (each with aspect ratio 2) placed side-by-side in a 39-pixel wrapper get scaled down to about 8 pixels tall to fit the combined width, leaving roughly 30 pixels of empty space above due to bottom-anchoring. This creates the visual effect the user described—the group occupies a tall slot but only fills the bottom 20% with content.

The issue is that bottom-anchoring works fine for baseline alignment in horizontal layouts, but in vertical mode it's creating unwanted padding above the group. So for vertical lines with horizontal merges, the wrapper should be constrained to baseSize width but let the height be content-driven—that way a group with two side-by-side horizontals stays compact at around 8.2 px tall instead of padding out to 39 px. But this creates an asymmetry with horizontal lines where I forced the wrapper to fill the perpendicular dimension, so I need to clarify what "perpendicular" really means in each orientation and whether merges should always fill that space or adapt to content. I need to adjust the horizontal merge sizing logic: the wrapper should be full width (baseSize) but only as tall as the actual content (finalH), not forced to baseSize. Looking at the edge cases—two horizontals shrink to fit side by side and end up much shorter, two fulls would look cramped, and two verticals stay tall—it seems the content-height approach makes sense, though I'm not sure if the user tested all these combinations. The fix is to remove the forced baseSize height constraint and let the wrapper size to its content, positioning everything at the top with no bottom-anchoring. I need to reconsider how the wrapper dimensions work in vertical writing mode. When the merged group is inside a `.vertical-run` with `writing-mode: vertical-rl`, the CSS height and width properties map differently to the visual layout — height becomes the cross-axis measurement while width controls the main-axis extent along the line. Let me trace through exactly how the inline element's dimensions are interpreted in this context.

## [787] assistant

Now the fix — in `horizontal` branch, shrink `wrapperH` to the actual content height instead of forcing it to `targetH`, and drop the bottom-anchoring offset:

 Adjusting the wrapper dimensions so it hugs the actual content rather than the target size — tightening the horizontal padding while using the final height instead of the target height, and removing the bottom offset. But I need to reconsider how this works in vertical mode, where the wrapper width and height have different meanings.

Looking at the actual layout: if I keep the wrapper at full column width (baseSize) but shrink the height to just the content (finalH), that should address the user's complaint about the 80% empty space on top. The column width should stay consistent so the merged group aligns properly with other glyphs in the column, even if they have different natural widths. So the fix is to set wrapperW to the full column width and wrapperH to the actual content height.

I also need to adjust slotTop since I'm removing the vertical offset, and this change applies whether the merge is in vertical or horizontal mode since relayoutMergedIcon regenerates the layout either way.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
    // Critical invariant (matches JSesh's quadrat model): every merged
    // group occupies a FULL line-height block, regardless of how many
    // or what shape of glyphs it contains. Two horizontal "low" glyphs
    // grouped side-by-side still produce a baseSize-tall wrapper —
    // the glyphs themselves scale and center inside it. Without this,
    // grouped horizontals shrank to a sliver while neighbouring
    // un-grouped glyphs kept their full line height, so lines became
    // visually staggered.
    if (horizontal) {
      const targetW = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      const targetH = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      columnMergeTargetW = targetW;
      columnMergeTargetH = targetH;

      const aspectRatios = readAspectRatios();
      const heightCap = Math.min(90, baseSize);
      const desiredH = Math.min(targetH, heightCap);
- NEW ---
    // Horizontal merge (used when the line itself is vertical — group
    // lays its glyphs side-by-side perpendicular to the column).
    //
    // In a vertical column, `baseSize` constrains the column WIDTH
    // (cross-axis), not the inline extent along the column. So the
    // wrapper is sized as `baseSize × content_height` — the cross-axis
    // is the full column width, the inline-axis only takes whatever
    // height the side-by-side content actually needs.
    //
    // Earlier this branch forced `wrapperH = baseSize` (a baseSize×
    // baseSize square wrapper) and bottom-anchored the content. For
    // two low glyphs grouped together, that left ~80% empty space
    // above the artwork — visible as huge padding on top of the
    // merged group inside the vertical column. Letting wrapperH track
    // the content height removes that gap while still keeping the
    // group aligned to the column on the cross-axis.
    if (horizontal) {
      const targetW = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      const targetH = Math.round(Math.min(90, Math.max(12, baseSize)));
      columnMergeTargetW = targetW;
      columnMergeTargetH = targetH;

      const aspectRatios = readAspectRatios();
      const heightCap = Math.min(90, baseSize);
      const desiredH = Math.min(targetH, heightCap);
```

## [788] assistant

```diff
- OLD ---
      const contentW =
        slotWidths.reduce((s, w) => s + w, 0) + totalGapsW;
      const offsetX = (targetW - contentW) / 2;
      // Anchor the scaled content to the *bottom* of the quadrat so
      // horizontal "low" glyphs sit on the baseline (where they'd sit
      // un-grouped), instead of floating in the middle. Tall glyphs
      // that already fill the quadrat are unaffected (finalH == targetH).
      const offsetY = Math.max(0, targetH - finalH);

      wrapperW = targetW;
      // Always full line height — content occupies (offsetY..targetH).
      wrapperH = targetH;

      let cursor = offsetX;
      slotDims = slotWidths.map((sw, i) => {
        // Small slots keep their intrinsic height too (the artwork is
        // centered vertically inside via fitCloneIntoSlot).
        const slotH = smallFlags[i]
          ? Math.min(
              finalH,
              (smallIntrinsics[i] as { w: number; h: number }).h,
            )
          : finalH;
        const slotTop = offsetY + (finalH - slotH);
        const dim = { w: sw, h: slotH, left: cursor, top: slotTop };

- NEW ---
      const contentW =
        slotWidths.reduce((s, w) => s + w, 0) + totalGapsW;
      const offsetX = (targetW - contentW) / 2;

      wrapperW = targetW;
      // Wrapper height tracks the actual content — no forced
      // baseSize square, no bottom-anchored gap above the artwork.
      // Capped at the safety ceiling that already applied to merged
      // groups so a pathological combination can't blow out the line.
      wrapperH = Math.max(1, Math.round(Math.min(heightCap, finalH)));

      let cursor = offsetX;
      slotDims = slotWidths.map((sw, i) => {
        // Small slots keep their intrinsic height inside the (now
        // tight) wrapper. The artwork is centered vertically inside
        // its slot by fitCloneIntoSlot.
        const slotH = smallFlags[i]
          ? Math.min(
              finalH,
              (smallIntrinsics[i] as { w: number; h: number }).h,
            )
          : finalH;
        // Slot sits at the top of the wrapper (wrapperH now equals
        // finalH, so this is effectively the only valid Y for a
        // non-small slot; smalls are just shorter than the wrapper
        // and align to the bottom for parity with un-grouped lows).
        const slotTop = Math.max(0, wrapperH - slotH);
        const dim = { w: sw, h: slotH, left: cursor, top: slotTop };
        cursor += sw + INTERNAL_GAP;

## [795] assistant

Clean. Vertical merge now uses a "space-between" rule:

- **All-small group**: each slot takes its glyph's intrinsic height. Slot 0 starts at `y=0`, slot N-1 ends at `y=baseSize`, the leftover space is spread between them as wider gaps. So the top dot hugs the top of the line, the bottom dot hugs the bottom.
- **Mixed (small + others)**: smalls get their intrinsic slot, others share the remainder — slot sum already equals `baseSize`, so the same positioning code lays them edge-to-edge with the standard gap. First glyph at top, last at bottom, no empty space.
- **All non-small**: equal slot heights (`baseSize/n`), positioned with the standard gap — already touches both bounds, nothing changes.

Two more details:

1. The last slot's `top` is computed as `wrapperH - slotH` directly (instead of accumulating the cursor) so fractional rounding can't leave it a pixel shy of the bottom edge.
2. The "centered inside the slot" behaviour for small glyphs is now a no-op because the slot equals the intrinsic size — so the artwork ends up flush with whichever line edge its slot is sitting on.

If you also want the horizontal merge (used when the line is vertical) to follow the same "leftmost-touches-left, rightmost-touches-right" rule, say the word and I'll mirror it.

      const innerSmall = clone.querySelector("svg") as SVGElement | null;
      if (innerSmall) {
        innerSmall.removeAttribute("width");
        innerSmall.removeAttribute("height");
        (innerSmall.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).display = "block";
        (innerSmall.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).width = `${w}px`;
        (innerSmall.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).height = `${h}px`;
        innerSmall.setAttribute("preserveAspectRatio", "xMidYMid meet");
      }
      return;
    }

    clone.style.cssText = `
      position: absolute;
      top: 0;
      left: 0;
      width: ${slotW}px;
      height: ${slotH}px;
      line-height: 0;
    `;
    const inner = clone.querySelector("svg") as SVGElement | null;
    if (inner) {
      inner.removeAttribute("width");
      inner.removeAttribute("height");
      // `display: block` removes the inline-baseline gap that
      // appears under inline SVGs — without it, a horizontal glyph
      // that exactly matches its slot AR still ends up with ~1–2px
      // of empty space at the slot bottom because the inline box
      // reserves room for descenders.
      (inner.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).display = "block";
      (inner.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).width = `${slotW}px`;
      (inner.style as CSSStyleDeclaration).height = `${slotH}px`;
      inner.setAttribute("preserveAspectRatio", "xMidYMid meet");
    }
  };

## [804] assistant

Clean. Two fixes for the residual top/bottom padding on grouped horizontals:

1. **AR-aware slot height cap** in `computeMergedLayout`. For non-small slots, when a wide glyph's natural width (`shareH × AR`) would exceed `baseSize`, the slot height is shrunk to `baseSize / AR` so the slot's AR matches the artwork's. Otherwise `preserveAspectRatio: meet` letterboxes the SVG inside the slot and that's exactly the "small padding top/bottom" you saw. Any height the slot gives back this way is absorbed by the space-between distribution (i.e. it becomes a slightly wider middle gap), so the top glyph still hugs `y=0` and the bottom glyph still hugs `y=baseSize`.

2. **`display: block` on the inner `<svg>`** in `fitCloneIntoSlot`. Inline SVGs reserve a couple of pixels of baseline space below themselves (for descenders) even inside an absolutely positioned wrapper — those couple of pixels were leaking through as a thin gap at the slot bottom even when the AR was a perfect match. Setting the SVG to `display: block` (and `line-height: 0` on its wrapper) removes that. Applied to both regular and small icon branches.

Net effect: 2 grouped horizontals now have artwork flush with the wrapper top and bottom, regardless of whether their AR is exactly 2 or something a little wider.

  // Walk every merged group in the editor and flip its orientation to
  // match its current surrounding context (.vertical-run → horizontal,
  // otherwise → vertical). Called after any vertical-mode toggle so
  // existing groups stay perpendicular to the line.
  const relayoutAllMergedIcons = () => {
    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return;
    const merged = editor.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(".svg-icon.merged");
    merged.forEach((m) => {
      // Skip nested merged groups (a merged inside another merged, if that
      // ever happens) — they get reflowed via their parent's layout.
      if (m.parentElement?.closest(".svg-icon.merged")) return;
      const horizontal = shouldMergedGroupBeHorizontal(m);
      const currentLayout = m.dataset.layout;
      if (
        (horizontal && currentLayout === "horizontal") ||
        (!horizontal && currentLayout === "vertical")
      ) {
        return;
      }
      relayoutMergedIcon(m, horizontal);

## [821] assistant

Clean. Behavior now matches JSesh:

- **`createMergedIcon`**: groups always lay out as a vertical stack of their constituent glyphs (`horizontal = false` by default). The `options.horizontal` override is kept only for explicit callers like MagicBox that customize slot positions.
- **`toggleColumnMode`**: no longer reflows merged groups when the global vertical switch flips. A group made in horizontal mode stays a top-to-bottom stack when you switch to vertical, and stays the same on the way back.
- Dead helpers `relayoutAllMergedIcons`, `relayoutMergedIcon`, and `shouldMergedGroupBeHorizontal` are removed — orientation is determined once at creation time and never recomputed.

So in a horizontal line, a group of 2 still stacks top-over-bottom in a single quadrat. In a vertical column, that same group is one entry in the column where the two glyphs are also top-over-bottom inside. Toggling the document direction never touches existing groups' internal layout.

    wrapperW: number;

    columnMergeTargetW: number | null;

  // Three merged-group layout modes:
  //   • "quadrat"       — JSesh's horizontal-line behaviour: glyphs are
  //                       packed into a single line-height block, smalls
  //                       keep natural size, non-smalls stretch to share
  //                       the height, first/last touch the line bounds.
  //   • "side-by-side"  — Glyphs laid out horizontally next to each
  //                       other. Used by MagicBox; never picked
  //                       automatically.
  //   • "natural-stack" — Vertical-column behaviour: each glyph keeps
  //                       its natural rendered size, stacked top-to-
  //                       bottom with NO internal gap. The group looks
  //                       like the same glyphs would if they weren't
  //                       grouped at all; a slightly wider wrapper
  //                       margin gives 1–2px of visual separation
  //                       between adjacent groups.
  type MergedLayoutMode = "quadrat" | "side-by-side" | "natural-stack";

        // Composites carry their canonical box on the wrapper
        // (origWidth / origHeight). Falling back to inner content

          const oh = Number(el.dataset.origHeight) || baseSize;

        let arH = Number(el.dataset.arH);

          !Number.isFinite(arW) ||
          !Number.isFinite(arH) ||

    // Orientation: JSesh-style — every group is laid out as a
    // vertical stack of its constituent glyphs regardless of the

    // also stacks top-to-bottom (it just lives in a column of glyphs

    // Pick the layout mode at creation time and lock it in. The mode
    // is captured on the wrapper's dataset so toggling the global
    // vertical switch later doesn't re-flow this group — JSesh-style,
    // groups keep whatever layout they had when they were made.
    //   • MagicBox forces "side-by-side" via options.horizontal.
    //   • Otherwise: vertical line (columnMode on) → natural-stack;
    //     horizontal line → quadrat.
    const layoutMode: MergedLayoutMode =
      options?.horizontal === true
        ? "side-by-side"
        : columnMode
          ? "natural-stack"
          : "quadrat";

    const layout = computeMergedLayout(icons, layoutMode, iconSize);

    // Natural-stack groups in a vertical column get a slightly bigger
    // top/bottom margin than the standard 4px so the group reads as a
    // single unit — adjacent groups end up ~10px apart vs the regular
    // 8px between ungrouped glyphs (≈2px additional, per the client).
    // The cross-axis (3px) stays the same so the column itself isn't

    const wrapperMargin =
      layoutMode === "natural-stack" ? "5px 3px" : "4px 3px";
    // Vertical-stack natural mode doesn't have a fixed cap — the
    // wrapper is content-sized. Keep the safety cap for quadrat /
    // side-by-side layouts so a weird combination can't blow out
    // the line.
    const mergedBoxCap =
      layoutMode === "natural-stack"
        ? ""
        : `max-width: ${maxComboW}px; max-height: 90px;`;
    mergedWrapper.style.cssText = `
        display: inline-block;
        cursor: text;
        margin: ${wrapperMargin};
        vertical-align: middle;
        position: relative;

        height: ${wrapperH}px;
        ${mergedBoxCap}

## [841] assistant

All clean. Summary of what changed in `src/components/writing/content/MainContent.tsx`:

- Added a third merged-group layout mode `"natural-stack"` alongside the existing `"quadrat"` and `"side-by-side"` modes.
- `createMergedIcon` now picks `"natural-stack"` automatically when the user is in vertical mode (`columnMode === true`), and stamps the chosen mode on `dataset.layoutMode` so it sticks across resizes / toggles.
- New `naturalSize()` helper inside `computeMergedLayout` computes each glyph's natural rendered size (column-mode dimensions for normals, `origWidth/origHeight` for composites, visible-artwork box for small icons).
- In natural-stack mode, slots stack top-to-bottom with **zero internal gap** — the glyphs touch directly, exactly like ungrouped glyphs would in a column. Each slot is horizontally centered against the widest slot so different-width glyphs line up.
- The wrapper grows to fit its content (no `max-width`/`max-height` cap) and uses `margin: 5px 3px` instead of `4px 3px`, giving the requested ~2px of additional breathing room **between** groups while inside the group, glyphs sit flush.
- `applyIconSizeToElement` (resize path) now reads `dataset.layoutMode` so resizing a natural-stack group keeps natural-stack behaviour, with no caps and no INTERNAL_GAP collapsing.

What you'll see now in vertical mode:
- A grouped pair/trio reads as just stacked icons — same look as if they weren't grouped.
- No gap between glyphs inside a group (they touch).
- ~2px extra space between one group and the next item in the column.
- Items are not shrunk; each glyph keeps the size it would have in the column on its own.
- Pre-existing groups created in horizontal mode keep their quadrat layout (per your earlier "stays the same on toggle" rule).

- OLD ---
  const toggleColumnMode = () => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    const nextColumnMode = !columnMode;
    if (nextColumnMode) {
      const lastWrapper = wrapAllHieroglyphRuns();
      if (lastWrapper) placeCaretAfter(lastWrapper);
    } else {
      unwrapAllAutoHilo();
    }
    // Note: existing merged groups intentionally keep their original
    // (vertical-stack) layout — JSesh doesn't flip group orientation
    // when the surrounding line direction changes, and the client
    // wants the same behaviour.
    setColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    commitHistory("push");
    editor?.focus();
  };
- NEW ---
  const toggleColumnMode = () => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    const nextColumnMode = !columnMode;
    if (nextColumnMode) {
      const lastWrapper = wrapAllHieroglyphRuns();
      if (lastWrapper) placeCaretAfter(lastWrapper);
    } else {
      unwrapAllAutoHilo();
    }
    // Merged groups carry two different layouts:
    //   • horizontal line → "quadrat" (every glyph crammed into one
    //     baseSize-tall block, top/bottom flush, shared height).
    //   • vertical line   → "natural-stack" (each glyph at its natural
    //     column-mode size, no internal gap, no line-height shrinking).
    // Flip all existing groups so the toggle applies retroactively —
    // a group created in horizontal mode shouldn't keep its quadrat
    // shrinking after the user enters vertical mode.
    relayoutAllMergedGroupsForColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    setColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    commitHistory("push");
    editor?.focus();
  };
```

## [850] assistant

Everything is wired up. Here's the diff in behaviour:

- `relayoutMergedIcon(wrapper, mode)` rebuilds an existing merged group's wrapper styles + slot positions in-place using `computeMergedLayout(mode)`. It also re-runs `fitCloneIntoSlot` on each clone so cartouches and other composites scale correctly.
- `relayoutAllMergedGroupsForColumnMode(vertical)` walks every `.svg-icon.merged` in the editor and converts it: vertical line → `natural-stack`, horizontal line → `quadrat`. Side-by-side groups (MagicBox) are left alone.
- `toggleColumnMode` now calls that helper, so every existing group gets re-flowed when the user flips the global switch — not just newly created ones.

Net result for your test:
- Group 5 glyphs in horizontal mode → quadrat (each in `baseSize/5` slot, shared height).
- Hit the vertical button → every glyph in the group expands back to its natural column-mode size, stacked top-to-bottom with **0 internal gap**, no line-height shrinking. The wrapper grows tall to fit.
- Hit the vertical button again → back to quadrat (line-height-fit).
- Adjacent groups in the column still get ~2px extra breathing room from the bumped wrapper margin.

Type/lint clean (only the pre-existing unrelated `initializeHistory` warning).

```diff
- OLD ---
      const slotSizes = icons.map(naturalSize);
      wrapperW = Math.max(...slotSizes.map((s) => s.w));
      let cursor = 0;
      slotDims = slotSizes.map((s) => {
        const dim = {
          w: s.w,
          h: s.h,
          // Center each slot on the wrapper's cross-axis so glyphs
          // of different widths align like they would in a regular
          // ungrouped column.
          left: (wrapperW - s.w) / 2,
          top: cursor,
        };

        return dim;
      });

- NEW ---
      // Hair-line separator inside the stack so neighbouring glyphs
      // aren't visually fused, while still reading as one tight group.
      // The wrapper margin (set in createMergedIcon / relayoutMergedIcon)
      // is meaningfully larger than this so the outer gap between two
      // groups remains noticeably wider than the inner one.
      const NATURAL_INTERNAL_GAP = 2;
      const slotSizes = icons.map(naturalSize);
      wrapperW = Math.max(...slotSizes.map((s) => s.w));
      let cursor = 0;
      slotDims = slotSizes.map((s, i) => {
        const dim = {
          w: s.w,
          h: s.h,
          // Center each slot on the wrapper's cross-axis so glyphs
          // of different widths align like they would in a regular
          // ungrouped column.
          left: (wrapperW - s.w) / 2,
          top: cursor,
        };
        cursor += s.h;
        if (i < slotSizes.length - 1) cursor += NATURAL_INTERNAL_GAP;
        return dim;
      });
      wrapperH = Math.max(1, Math.round(cursor));
      wrapperW = Math.max(1, Math.round(wrapperW));
```

  const unwrapAllAutoHilo = () => {
- NEW ---
  // Make sure `glyph` lives inside a `.vertical-run.auto-hilo` wrapper.
  // Used when columnMode is on and a glyph was just inserted at the
  // caret (which has no idea about the global vertical lock). If the
  // glyph's previous sibling is already an auto-hilo run we extend it;
  // otherwise we create a fresh run wrapping just this glyph. This is
  // how the vertical button keeps acting as a "lock" — every new glyph
  // gets wrapped, regardless of whether the editor was empty, the
  // caret is post-Enter on a fresh line, etc.
  const ensureGlyphInVerticalRun = (glyph: HTMLElement): HTMLElement => {
    const existing = glyph.closest(".vertical-run") as HTMLElement | null;
    if (existing) return existing;

    const prev = glyph.previousSibling;
    if (
      prev instanceof HTMLElement &&
      prev.classList.contains("vertical-run") &&
      prev.classList.contains("auto-hilo")
    ) {
      prev.appendChild(glyph);
      return prev;
    }

    const next = glyph.nextSibling;
    if (
      next instanceof HTMLElement &&
      next.classList.contains("vertical-run") &&
      next.classList.contains("auto-hilo")
    ) {
      next.insertBefore(glyph, next.firstChild);
      return next;
    }

    const parent = glyph.parentNode;
    if (!parent) return glyph;
    const run = document.createElement("span");
    run.className = "vertical-run auto-hilo";
    run.setAttribute("style", VERTICAL_RUN_CSS);
    parent.insertBefore(run, glyph);
    run.appendChild(glyph)

_... [truncated 59 chars] ..._
```

Now wire it into `insertSvgAtCursor`:

```diff
- OLD ---
  const insertSvgAtCursor = (svgString: string, pictureSize?: number) => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return;
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    restoreSavedRangeIfNeeded();
    const wrapper = createSvgWrapper(svgString, pictureSize);
    const sel = window.getSelection();

    if (sel && sel.rangeCount > 0) {
      const range = sel.getRangeAt(0);
      if (editor.contains(range.commonAncestorContainer)) {
        range.deleteContents();
        range.insertNode(wrapper);

        range.setStartAfter(wrapper);
        range.collapse(true);
        sel.removeAllRanges();
        sel.addRange(range);
        savedRangeRef.current = range.cloneRange();
        scrollElementIntoView(wrapper);
      } else {
        editor.appendChild(wrapper);
        scrollElementIntoView(wrapper);
      }
    } else {
      editor.appendChild(wrapper);
      scrollElementIntoView(wrapper);
    }

    editor.focus();
    commitHistory("push");
  };
- NEW ---
  const insertSvgAtCursor = (svgString: string, pictureSize?: number) => {
    if (!editorCanEdit) return;

    const editor = editorRef.current;
    if (!editor) return;
    resetTypingHistorySession();
    restoreSavedRangeIfNeeded();
    const wrapper = createSvgWrapper(svgString, pictureSize);
    const sel = window.getSelection();

    let inserted = false;
    if (sel && sel.rangeCount > 0) {
      const range = sel.getRangeAt(0);
      if (editor.contains(range.commonAncestorContainer)) {
        range.deleteContents();
        range.insertNode(wrapper);
        inserted = true;
      }
    }
    if (!inserted) {
      editor.appendChild(wrapper);
    }

    // Global vertical lock: when columnMode is on, any glyph inserted
    // anywhere in the doc gets adopted by a `.vertical-run` so it
    // renders in a column. Without this, glyphs added into an empty
    // editor, or onto a fresh line created by Enter (which deliberately
    // exits any enclosing vertical-run), would land as horizontal text
    // and the toggle would feel like it forgot the user's choice.
    if (columnMode) {
      ensureGlyphInVerticalRun(wrapper);
    }

    if (sel) {
      const newRange = document.createRange();
      newRange.setStartAfter(wrapper);
      newRange.collapse(true);
      sel.removeAllRanges();
      sel.addRange(newRange);
      savedRangeRef.current = newRange.cloneRange();
    }
    scrollElementIntoView(wrapper);

    editor.focus();
    commitHistory("push");
  };
```

        ensureGlyphInVerticalRun(draggedElement);

      range.collapse(true);
      scrollElementIntoView(draggedElement);

## [897] assistant

All wired up. Summary of the vertical-lock fix:

**New helper `ensureGlyphInVerticalRun(glyph)`** — guarantees a glyph lives inside a `.vertical-run.auto-hilo`:
- If already inside one → no-op.
- If the previous sibling is an existing auto-hilo run → append to it (same line, same column).
- If the next sibling is one → prepend to it.
- Otherwise → create a fresh run and move the glyph in.

**Wired into every glyph-insertion path** (gated on `columnMode === true`):
- `insertSvgAtCursor` (palette click / menu) — restructured so the caret is re-anchored after the wrapper in its final position.
- The drop handler (palette → editor and editor → editor drags).
- The paste handler (when pasted nodes contain `.svg-icon` elements).

**Behaviour now:**
- Vertical mode on + empty editor → adding any glyph spawns a `.vertical-run` around it.
- Vertical mode on + Enter (which still jumps out of the surrounding run, to give the user a clean new line) → the next glyph added on the new line spawns a fresh `.vertical-run`.
- Vertical mode on + click anywhere + add glyph → glyph either joins the adjacent run or starts a new one. Always vertical.
- Vertical mode off → no wrapping happens; glyphs flow horizontally as before.

Net effect: the vertical button is now a true sticky lock, and a document can no longer end up mixing vertical and horizontal glyphs through the normal insertion paths.

 Thinking through the vertical mode scenario where the user starts with columnMode enabled and adds glyphs sequentially. When the first glyph is inserted, setStartAfter positions the caret after the wrapper within its parent run, so subsequent additions should merge into that same run rather than creating new ones.

Now I'm tracing through what happens when the user clicks outside the run and adds another glyph—the wrapper gets inserted as a sibling after the run, but ensureGlyphInVerticalRun detects the run as previousSibling and moves the wrapper back inside it. This means the new glyph appends to the existing column, which might not be the user's intent if they wanted to start a separate column.

The workaround is that pressing Enter first exits the run and inserts a break, so the next glyph would create a fresh run instead.

There's one more edge case with typing spans—when the wrapper lands inside a typing span, closest() will still find the vertical-run ancestor if one exists above the typing span. But if the typing span is at the editor root with no run ancestor, we'd create a new run inside the typing span itself, which is structurally awkward even if it renders fine visually.

I think this is reasonable enough to commit and let the user test the behavior.

  // Mirror of columnMode for callbacks that might capture a stale value
  // (palette click handlers pass through a few prop hops, and React batches
  // state updates, so the function reference handed to a child can lag

    // Global vertical lock: when columnMode is on, any glyph inserted

    // Read from the ref so a freshly-flipped toggle takes effect on

    if (columnModeRef.current) {

    // Flip all existing groups so the toggle applies retroactively —
    // a group created in horizontal mode shouldn't keep its quadrat
    // shrinking after the user enters vertical mode.
    relayoutAllMergedGroupsForColumnMode(nextColumnMode);
    // Update the ref synchronously so insertion handlers triggered

    columnModeRef.current = nextColumnMode;

For horizontal groups in a horizontal line, I'd bump the left/right margins similarly, maybe to 11-12px each side to create roughly 22-24px gaps between adjacent horizontal groups, applying this extra spacing consistently across all grouped elements.

```
import React, { useEffect, useRef, useState } from "react";

/**
 * Group orientation modes, mirroring JSesh's two grouping commands.
 *
 *   - "vertical"   : stack glyphs on top of each other (quadrat in
 *                    horizontal lines, natural-stack in vertical lines).
 *   - "horizontal" : pack glyphs side-by-side regardless of the
 *                    surrounding line direction.
 */
export type GroupOrientation = "vertical" | "horizontal";

interface IProps {
  selectedIconCount: number;
  /**
   * Group the current selection with the chosen orientation. Called once
   * per picker click — the picker itself stays stateless about which
   * orientation was used last.
   */
  onGroup: (orientation: GroupOrientation) => void;
}

interface OrientationOption {
  id: GroupOrientation;
  label: string;
  description: string;
}

const OPTIONS: OrientationOption[] = [
  {
    id: "vertical",
    label: "Vertical",
    description:
      "Stack glyphs vertically. In horizontal lines this packs them into one quadrat; in vertical lines they sit directly below each other.",
  },
  {
    id: "horizontal",
    label: "Horizontal",
    description:
      "Pack glyphs side-by-side. Always reads left-to-right regardless of the surrounding line direction.",
  },
];

/**
 * Tiny SVG preview of the orientation: two stacked or side-by-side
 * placeholder rectangles. Keeps the picker self-contained — no need
 * to render real glyph artwork.
 */

  orientation: GroupOrientation;
  size?: number;
}> = ({ orientation, size = 44 }) => {
  const W = size;
  const H = size;
  const stroke = "currentColor";
  const slotProps = {
    fill: "currentColor",
    fillOpacity: 0.25,
    stroke,
    strokeWidth: 1,
  } as const;

  if (orientation === "vertical") {
    const slotH = (H - 6) / 2;
    return (
      <svg width={W} height={H} viewBox={`0 0 ${W} ${H}`}>
        <rect x={4} y={2} width={W - 8} height={slotH} rx={3} {...slotProps} />
        <rect
          x={4}
          y={H - 2 - slotH}
          width={W - 8}
          height={slotH}
          rx={3}
          {...slotProps}
        />
      </svg>
    );
  }

  const slotW = (W - 6) / 2;
  return (
    <svg width={W} height={H} viewBox={`0 0 ${W} ${H}`}>
      <rect x={2} y={4} width={slotW} height={H - 8} rx={3} {...slotProps} />
      <rect
        x={W - 2 - slotW}
        y={4}
        width={slotW}
        height={H - 8}
        rx={3}
        {...slotProps}
      />
    </

## [947] assistant

Here's what changed:

**New `GroupPicker` component** (`src/components/writing/content/GroupPicker.tsx`)
- Click the Group button → popover with two options: **Vertical** and **Horizontal**.
- Same open / outside-close / Esc / auto-close-on-deselect behaviour as `CartouchePicker` and `RotateGlyph`.
- Small SVG previews so the orientation is unambiguous.

**`mergeGroup(orientation)` in `MainContent.tsx`**
- New signature: `mergeGroup(orientation: "vertical" | "horizontal" = "vertical")`.
- `"vertical"` (default) → `createMergedIcon` picks `quadrat` in horizontal lines, `natural-stack` in vertical lines (existing behaviour you already approved).
- `"horizontal"` → `createMergedIcon({ horizontal: true })` → `side-by-side` layout, used regardless of the line's direction.

**Layout invariants on toggle**
- `relayoutAllMergedGroupsForColumnMode` already skips groups whose `dataset.layoutMode === "side-by-side"`. So horizontal groups stay horizontal when the user toggles the vertical document switch. Vertical groups still flip between quadrat ↔ natural-stack based on the document direction.

**Outer spacing for side-by-side groups**
- Wrapper margin for `side-by-side` is `4px 10px` (vs `4px 3px` for solitary glyphs) — adjacent horizontal groups get ~20px breathing room horizontally, mirroring what we did for vertical groups on the top/bottom axis.
- `relayoutMergedIcon` honours the same margin map, so a re-flow keeps the spacing correct.

**`AssistantBar`**
- Old inline Group button replaced with `<GroupPicker selectedIconCount onGroup={mergeGroup} />`.
- Prop type updated to `mergeGroup: (orientation?: GroupOrientation) => void`.

Resulting behaviour matrix:

| Doc direction | Group orientation | Layout                                                          |
|---------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| Horizontal    | Vertical          | Quadrat (glyphs stacked into one baseSize square)               |
| Horizontal    | Horizontal        | Side-by-side packed into baseSize wide; extra L/R outer margin  |
| Vertical      | Vertical          | Natural-stack (full-size, stacked, top/bottom gaps as before)   |
| Vertical      | Horizontal        | Side-by-side packed into baseSize wide; extra L/R outer margin  |

Type-clean. Let me know how it feels in the editor and I'll fine-tune spacing / sizing if anything looks off.

## [955] assistant

Done. The side-by-side layout now uses a space-between rule:

- First slot pinned to `left = 0`.
- Last slot pinned to `left = wrapperW - slotWidth` (explicit, to dodge rounding errors).
- Leftover space distributed equally as gaps between intermediate slots.
- If the glyphs (with minimum `INTERNAL_GAP` between them) are already wider than the wrapper, falls back to the previous centered+minimum-gap layout so things don't overlap.

So two grouped sticks in a vertical document now sit flush with the line's left and right edges, with the empty width pushed out as one big middle gap — same "touch the bounds" rule we already have for vertically-grouped low glyphs in horizontal docs.

## [956] user

<timestamp>Tuesday, May 12, 2026, 5:17 PM (UTC+3)</timestamp>
<user_query>
can you give me some of the issues and quirks exist the editor made custom? i wanna tell the client some reasons why we should switch to a reliable rich text editor
</user_query>

---

## [957] assistant

Sure. Here's an honest rundown — organized so you can drop sections straight into a client doc.

---

## 1. Foundation is `contentEditable` with custom DOM manipulation

The editor is a single `<div contenteditable="true">` with `~8,800 lines` of bespoke logic in `MainContent.tsx` directly mutating the DOM via `range.insertNode`, `appendChild`, custom selection math, and dataset-driven layout. Every behaviour a normal editor gets for free (text input, selection, caret, undo, paste, IME, drag/drop) is hand-rolled.

**Why this matters:** `contentEditable` is notoriously inconsistent across browsers — Chrome, Safari, and Firefox each have different rules for what survives input events, how selections are normalised, where `<br>` vs `<div>` get inserted, etc. Every fix is a per-browser whack-a-mole.

## 2. Concrete bugs we've already hit (and others like them will keep appearing)

These are real issues we've personally fixed over the past few weeks. Each one represents a behaviour that a proper editor framework treats as a primitive:

- **Caret invisible between glyphs / random caret placement.** Required custom hit-testing (`placeCaretAtPoint`) because the browser's `caretRangeFromPoint` returns the wrong node for inline-block SVG icons. Even now, the caret behaviour depends on the browser.
- **Text selection broke after we patched glyph selection.** The two interact through the same global `mouseDown` handler.
- **Laggy backwards selection on glyphs.** Required `requestAnimationFrame` throttling and a hand-written edge-snap algorithm.
- **Ctrl+A missed the first glyph.** Browser's `selectAll` skips leading inline-block atoms; we had to intercept the keystroke.
- **Backspace at line start deleted the first glyph.** Browser merges across element boundaries unless you reimplement deletion semantics.
- **Pressing Enter inside a vertical hieroglyph run wiped the whole editor.** Native `deleteContents()` on a range that overlaps multiple `contentEditable=false` glyphs has unpredictable behaviour.
- **Typing one char and deleting it left a permanent empty extra line above the glyphs.** Browser inserted phantom `<br>` and stray text nodes that we have to clean up in a custom `normalizeEditorArtifacts` pass.
- **Clicking far-left of a glyph made the caret invisible and typing did nothing.** Needed a special "selection boundary" span and a custom anchor strategy.
- **First-character typed at line start got reordered ("hello" → "elloh").** Browser placed the caret after the boundary span instead of inside it.
- **Cartouche shrank and jumped to the top-left when its parent group was resized.** Resizing the wrapper didn't reflow its absolutely-positioned children.
- **Cartouche padding disappeared when nested in a merged group.** Required a custom `fitCloneIntoSlot` scaling shim.
- **Vertical-mode button's active state was stale.** We rely on selection-change events that don't fire when the user just clicks a toolbar button.
- **Grouped glyphs in vertical mode kept the wrong layout.** We had to add a full `relayoutMergedIcon` pass that re-runs the layout math for every existing group on toggle.
- **Adding a glyph after toggling vertical mode lost the vertical lock** until React re-rendered. Required a `useRef` mirror because the click handler captured the stale `columnMode`.
- **Grouping a group of glyphs created nested groups.** Browser doesn't know what a "group" is — we wrote `expandMergedToLeaves` to flatten manually.
- **Native right-click context menu opened the browser's image menu on every glyph.** Disabling it took adding a custom listener and was reverted because it broke other things.

**Each of these took multiple iterations to fix.** None of them would have happened with a structured editor (Lexical, Slate, ProseMirror, TipTap) because their internal model is a tree of typed nodes, not a raw DOM.

## 3. Performance issues are structural, not just optimisable

- The app was at **500 MB RAM for basic operations** (rotating, grouping, a few glyphs). The original was 100 MB. We brought it back down, but the work involved:
  - Capping the undo history (it was unbounded).
  - Adding an idle-time PNG cache + LRU pruning (each glyph is rasterised to PNG for clipboard at 4× scale).
  - `requestAnimationFrame` throttling on `mousemove`.
  - Coalescing `selectionchange` events.
  - Avoiding `cloneNode(true)` in hot paths.
- Every action commits a `innerHTML` snapshot to history. With a structured editor, undo is a doc-tree delta, which is orders of magnitude cheaper.
- Every selection update walks the entire DOM to figure out which glyphs are inside the range. A model-based editor knows this in O(1).
- Adding ~50 glyphs and a few groups already produces an HTML payload of tens of KB of inline styles, dataset attrs, absolutely-positioned wrappers, and rasterised PNGs in the clipboard cache.

## 4. State and serialization are fragile

- Source of truth is the literal `innerHTML` string — every glyph, group, cartouche, rotation, layout mode, size cap, and merge-column-target is encoded as inline `style.cssText` and `dataset-*` attributes (`data-orig-width`, `data-base-size`, `data-cartouche`, `data-layout-mode`, `data-ar-w`, `data-merge-col-target-w`, etc.).
- Round-tripping through copy/paste requires a custom base64-encoded payload because the browser strips/normalises HTML.
- Saving the document = saving raw HTML. Schema migrations (e.g. "rename `data-layout` to `data-layout-mode`") have to be handled by ad-hoc backfill code. We already had to add legacy-fallback logic when introducing the natural-stack layout.
- There's no formal document model. Two different DOM structures can represent the same semantic content; the editor can't tell them apart.

## 5. Layout & rendering is reinvented from scratch

- Inline SVGs in a `contenteditable` flow do not play well with selection, baseline, line-height, or `writing-mode`. We've had to patch baseline gaps with `display: block; line-height: 0`, fight `preserveAspectRatio: meet` letterboxing, manually compute slot sizes, etc.
- Vertical mode uses `writing-mode: vertical-lr` on wrappers, which interacts oddly with absolute-positioned children. We had to special-case `wrapperH` and `wrapperW` semantics depending on context.
- Grouped glyphs have **three** internal layout modes (`quadrat`, `side-by-side`, `natural-stack`), each with its own math, slot positioning, gap rules, and outer-margin tweaks. All of this is encoded as inline pixel values that need re-running on every resize and toggle.

## 6. Maintainability is poor

- `MainContent.tsx` is **8,800+ lines**, single component, ~30 `useRef`s, multi-purpose effect hooks.
- Tight coupling: changing one feature (say, the Enter-key handler) has caused regressions in unrelated ones (vertical mode, line spacers, caret visibility).
- No tests. There's no realistic way to write tests for the current setup because behaviour depends on browser-specific DOM responses.
- Onboarding a new dev would require weeks just to map out the invariants ("you can't put a `<br>` after a `.svg-icon` without a spacer span, but only if the spacer isn't inside a vertical-run, but…").

## 7. Things we still don't have that a real editor would give us free

- **Reliable undo/redo** at semantic granularity (typing chars → one undo, not one per keystroke).
- **IME / non-Latin keyboard support.** Untested. `contentEditable` IME is famously broken without a framework that intercepts `compositionstart/end`.
- **Collaborative editing / OT/CRDT.** Trivial with ProseMirror or Lexical+Yjs. Essentially impossible with our setup.
- **Accessibility.** Screen readers cannot parse a tree of `<span contentEditable="false">` SVG icons. A structured editor exposes a proper accessibility tree.
- **Mobile / touch.** Touch selection is even worse than desktop in `contentEditable`. We haven't tested it.
- **Spell check, drag-and-drop tables, find & replace, comments, suggestions, etc.** All available as plugins in TipTa

_... [truncated 2669 chars] ..._

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Global reset + base typography for the Lotus prototype.
 *  Hieroglyphs use Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs (Google Fonts, loaded in
 *  index.html). Latin text uses Inter. The two are deliberately separated
 *  so that a `<span class="hg">𓀀</span>` renders with the hieroglyph face
 *  while surrounding prose stays in the UI font.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

*,
*::before,
*::after {
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

html,
body,
#root {
  height: 100%;
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
}

body {
  font-family: "Inter", system-ui, -apple-system, "Segoe UI", Roboto, sans-serif;
  font-size: 14px;
  color: #1f2937;
  background: #f7f4ee;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
  -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
}

button {
  font-family: inherit;
}

/* Stable hieroglyph face used everywhere — palette previews, editor
 * content, and inside custom NodeViews. Keeping the rule on a single
 * class means we never have to chase font-family down a tree of
 * wrapper spans. */
.hg {
  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs", "Segoe UI Historic",
    "Aegyptus", serif;
  font-feature-settings: normal;
  font-variant-ligatures: none;
  line-height: 1;
}
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Sample glyph catalogue.
 *
 *  In production this list will come from the admin dashboard — each row
 *  carries a Gardiner-style code, the Unicode character, and a "type"
 *  classification that mirrors JSesh's four shape categories. The editor
 *  uses `type` only for layout hints (e.g. "small" glyphs keep their
 *  natural size inside merged groups instead of stretching to fill a
 *  slot).
 *
 *  Each entry is one row in the side palette. Clicking inserts the
 *  unicode character at the caret — selection, deletion, cursor
 *  movement, and copy/paste all use the native text engine because the
 *  glyph is just a normal text codepoint with a hieroglyph font face.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

export type GlyphType = "full" | "horizontal" | "vertical" | "small";

export interface Glyph {
  /** Gardiner sign-list code (or a stand-in for prototypes). */
  code: string;
  /** Unicode character — a single codepoint in the U+13000..U+1342F block. */
  char: string;
  /** Layout class. Drives merge-slot sizing later on. */
  type: GlyphType;
  /** Human-friendly label for the palette tooltip. */
  label: string;
}

export const GLYPHS: Glyph[] = [
  // Full / square — humans and large animals
  { code: "A1", char: "𓀀", type: "full", label: "Seated man" },
  { code: "G1", char: "𓄿", type: "full", label: "Egyptian vulture" },
  { code: "G17", char: "𓅓", type: "full", label: "Owl" },

  // Horizontal — wider than tall
  { code: "I9", char: "𓆑", type: "horizontal", label: "Horned viper" },
  { code: "N35", char: "𓈖", type: "horizontal", label: "Water ripple" },
  { code: "V30", char: "𓎟", type: "horizontal", label: "Basket" },

  // Vertical — taller than wide
  { code: "M17", char: "𓇋", type: "vertical", label: "Reed leaf" },
  { code: "Z1", char: "𓏤", type: "vertical", label: "Single stroke" },

  // Small — compact in both dimensions
  { code: "D21", char: "𓂋", type: "small", label: "Mouth" },
  { code: "X1", char: "𓏏", type: "small", label: "Bread loaf" },
];

/** Lookup table by code for fast inserts from external sources. */
export const GLYPHS_BY_CODE: Record<string, Glyph> = Object.fromEntries(
  GLYPHS.map((g) => [g.code, g]),
);
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Editor-wide types shared between extensions, NodeViews, and the
 *  toolbar. Kept tiny on purpose — these are the only domain concepts
 *  the editor as a whole needs to know about.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import type { GlyphType } from "../data/glyphs";

/**
 * How a merged glyph-group lays its children out.
 *
 *  - "vertical"   : stack on top of each other (JSesh quadrat in
 *                   horizontal lines; natural-stack in vertical lines).
 *  - "horizontal" : pack side-by-side regardless of line direction.
 */
export type GroupOrientation = "vertical" | "horizontal";

/**
 * Cartouche frame shape. Today only the classic oval; the picker is
 * future-proofed so admins can plug new shapes in without touching
 * the editor.
 */
export type CartoucheShape = "oval";

/** Re-export so the editor doesn't import from the data layer directly. */
export type { GlyphType };
```

For the schema, cartouches should allow mixed inline content like text and glyph-groups, while groups themselves only contain text to avoid nesting. VerticalRun nodes will handle the vertical layout mode for proper hieroglyph rendering.

This keeps English text horizontal while hieroglyphs display vertically.

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/extensions/hieroglyphRanges.ts`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Tiny helpers for reasoning about hieroglyph characters in plain text.
 *
 *  These are deliberately small and stateless — every consumer (auto-wrap
 *  plugin, group/cartouche commands, NodeView layout) needs to ask the
 *  same question: "is this character a hieroglyph?". Centralising the
 *  Unicode range here means changing the supported set (e.g. adding the
 *  Demotic block later) is a one-line edit.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { GLYPHS } from "../../data/glyphs";
import type { GlyphType } from "../types";

/** Egyptian Hieroglyphs block (Unicode 5.2+). */
const HIEROGLYPH_RANGES: Array<[number, number]> = [
  [0x13000, 0x1342f],
];

/** Returns true if `codePoint` is in any supported hieroglyph block. */
export const isHieroglyphCodePoint = (codePoint: number): boolean => {
  for (const [lo, hi] of HIEROGLYPH_RANGES) {
    if (codePoint >= lo && codePoint <= hi) return true;
  }
  return false;
};

/** Returns true if every character of `s` is a hieroglyph (or the
 *  string is empty). Surrogate-pair safe — uses string iteration. */
export const isAllHieroglyphs = (s: string): boolean => {
  for (const ch of s) {
    const cp = ch.codePointAt(0);
    if (cp === undefined || !isHieroglyphCodePoint(cp)) return false;
  }
  return true;
};

/** Map from hieroglyph character → declared `GlyphType` (full / vertical
 *  / horizontal / small). Built once from the data catalogue. Characters
 *  not in the catalogue default to "full" — a safe choice that keeps
 *  them un-shrunk inside merged groups. */
const TYPE_BY_CHAR: Map<string, GlyphType> = new Map(
  GLYPHS.map((g) => [g.char, g.type]),
);

export const getGlyphType = (char: string): GlyphType => {
  return TYPE_BY_CHAR.get(char) ?? "full";
};

/** Iterate the hieroglyph characters in `s` one codepoint at a time
 *  (handling surrogate pairs). Returns an array of single-codepoint
 *  strings so callers can iterate with .map. */
export const splitGlyphs = (s: string): string[] => {
  return Array.from(s);
};
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Cartouche node.
 *
 *  Wraps an inline run (typically a sequence of hieroglyphs, with or

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  GlyphGroup node.
 *
 *  Represents JSesh's "merged group" — two or more glyphs welded into a
 *  single quadrat-style unit. The schema is dead simple: an inline node

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  VerticalRun node + global vertical-mode plumbing.
 *
 *  The doc-wide "Vertical Mode" toggle behaves like the old editor:
 *    - Hieroglyph runs become column-oriented (writing-mode: vertical-lr).
 *    - English / Latin text stays horizontal.
 *  We model this as an inline `verticalRun` node that wraps a run of
 *  hieroglyph content (text + glyph groups + cartouches). A small
 *  appendTransaction plugin keeps the structure in sync as the user
 *  types / inserts / deletes content.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { mergeAttributes, Node } from "@tiptap/core";
import { ReactNodeViewRenderer } from "@tiptap/react";
import { Plugin, PluginKey } from "@tiptap/pm/state";
import type { Node as PMNode } from "@tiptap/pm/model";
import { VerticalRunView } from "../views/VerticalRunView";
import { isAllHieroglyphs } from "./hieroglyphRanges";

declare module "@tiptap/core" {
  interface Commands<ReturnType> {
    verticalRun: {
      /** Flip the document-wide vertical lock on/off. When on, all
       *  hieroglyph runs in the doc render as vertical columns. */
      toggleVerticalMode: () => ReturnType;
      /** Set the lock explicitly. Useful for restoring state. */
      setVerticalMode: (on: boolean) => ReturnType;
    };
  }
}

const verticalModeKey = new PluginKey<{ on: boolean }>("verticalMode");

/** Read the current vertical-mode flag from the editor state. */
export const isVerticalModeOn = (state: {
  plugins: Plugin[];
  // accept any state shape with .plugins
} & Record<string, unknown>): boolean => {
  for (const plugin of state.plugins) {
    if (plugin.spec.key === verticalModeKey) {
      const data = (plugin as Plugin<{ on: boolean }>).getState(state as never);
      return !!data?.on;
    }
  }
  return false;
};

/**
 * Decide whether a node should be considered "hieroglyph content" for
 * the auto-wrap pass. Hieroglyph text, glyph groups, and cartouches all
 * qualify; everything else (English text, paragraph breaks, etc.)
 * does not.
 */
const isHieroglyphNode = (node: PMNode): boolean => {
  if (node.type.name === "glyphGroup") return true;
  if (node.type.name === "cartouche") return true;
  if (node.isText && node.text) return isAllHieroglyphs(node.text);
  return false;
};

export const VerticalRun = Node.create({
  name: "verticalRun",
  group: "inline",
  inline: true,

## [983] assistant

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/extensions/rotation.ts`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Rotation mark.
 *
 *  Applies a CSS `transform: rotate(Ndeg)` to its target inline content.
 *  Modelled as a mark (not a node) so it can decorate arbitrary inline
 *  ranges — a single glyph, a group, a cartouche, or a stretch of mixed
 *  content — without altering the document structure.
 *
 *  Inline elements can't be transformed in CSS, so the rendered span
 *  flips to `display: inline-block`. That introduces a small box around
 *  the rotated content but keeps the flow inline.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { Mark, mergeAttributes } from "@tiptap/core";

declare module "@tiptap/core" {
  interface Commands<ReturnType> {
    rotation: {
      /** Apply a rotation to the current selection. `angle` in degrees,
       *  0..359. Pass 0 to clear an existing rotation. */
      setRotation: (angle: number) => ReturnType;
      unsetRotation: () => ReturnType;
    };
  }
}

const normaliseAngle = (a: number): number => {
  if (!Number.isFinite(a)) return 0;
  const wrapped = ((a % 360) + 360) % 360;
  return Math.round(wrapped);
};

export const Rotation = Mark.create({
  name: "rotation",

  addAttributes() {
    return {
      angle: {
        default: 0,
        parseHTML: (el) => Number(el.getAttribute("data-angle") || 0),
        renderHTML: (attrs) => {
          const angle = normaliseAngle(attrs.angle);
          if (!angle) return {};
          return {
            "data-angle": String(angle),
            style: `display: inline-block; transform: rotate(${angle}deg); transform-origin: center center;`,
          };
        },
      },
    };
  },

  parseHTML() {
    return [{ tag: "span[data-angle]" }];
  },

  renderHTML({ HTMLAttributes }) {
    return [
      "span",
      mergeAttributes(HTMLAttributes, { class: "hg-rotated" }),
      0,
    ];
  },

  addCommands() {
    return {
      setRotation:
        (angle) =>
        ({ commands }) => {
          const a = normaliseAngle(angle);
          if (a === 0) return commands.unsetMark(this.name);
          return commands.setMark(this.name, { angle: a });
        },
      unsetRotation:
        () =>
        ({ commands }) =>
          commands.unsetMark(this.name),
    };
  },
});
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  GlyphGroupView — renders the contents of a `glyphGroup` node.
 *
 *  Three layouts, all chosen at render time from the node's orientation
 *  attribute + the document's vertical-mode flag (the same matrix as
 *  the old editor):
 *
 *    | doc orientation | group attr   | layout            |
 *    |-----------------|--------------|-------------------|
 *    | horizontal      | vertical     | quadrat           |
 *    | horizontal      | horizontal   | side-by-side      |
 *    | vertical        | vertical     | natural-stack     |
 *    | vertical        | horizontal   | side-by-side      |
 *
 *  Layout maths is driven by glyph TYPE (full / horizontal / vertical /
 *  small) read from the catalogue. Plain CSS grid handles the actual
 *  positioning — no manual pixel math — which is the whole point of
 *  moving to fonts: the font metrics give us correct sizes for free.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { NodeViewWrapper } from "@tiptap/react";

} from "../extensions/hieroglyphRanges";

import type { GroupOrientation, GlyphType } from "../types";
import "./GlyphGroupView.css";

type LayoutMode = "quadrat" | "side-by-side" | "natural-stack";

const computeLayoutMode = (
  orientation: GroupOrientation,
  verticalMode: boolean,
): LayoutMode => {
  if (orientation === "horizontal") return "side-by-side";
  return verticalMode ? "natural-stack" : "quadrat";
};

 * decimals in here. */
const QUADRAT_WEIGHT: Record<GlyphType, number> = {
  full: 1,
  horizontal: 1,
  vertical: 1,
  small: 0,
};

interface SlotProps {
  char: string;
  type: GlyphType;
}

const slotKey = (char: string, i: number): string => `${i}-${char}`;

export const GlyphGroupView = (props: NodeViewProps) => {

## [987] assistant

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/views/GlyphGroupView.css`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  GlyphGroup layout.
 *
 *  Three modes, each driven entirely by flexbox:
 *
 *   - quadrat        : vertical stack inside a square-ish line-height
 *                      block; small glyphs keep their height, big ones
 *                      flex-grow to share the rest. Lives in a
 *                      horizontal line.
 *   - natural-stack  : vertical stack at natural sizes (no shrinking).
 *                      Used when the surrounding line is vertical.
 *                      Outer wrapper margin separates adjacent groups.
 *   - side-by-side   : horizontal pack. Smalls keep their width, others
 *                      flex-grow. justify-content: space-between pins
 *                      first/last slots to the line bounds.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

.glyph-group {
  display: inline-flex;
  vertical-align: baseline;
  user-select: none;
  line-height: 1;
}

/* ---- Quadrat ---- */
.glyph-group--quadrat {
  flex-direction: column;
  align-items: stretch;
  justify-content: space-between;
  /* Footprint of one line — narrow on the inline axis so the group
     reads as one "letter". The exact size is set via the editor's
     base font-size; we use 1em on the cross axis so a font-size
     bump scales the whole quadrat. */
  width: 1em;
  height: 1em;
  /* Light separator from the surrounding glyphs so the unit is
     visible, no border. */
  margin: 0 0.05em;
  gap: 0.04em;
}
.glyph-group--quadrat .glyph-group__slot {
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  font-size: 0.5em;
  min-height: 0;
  text-align: center;
}

/* ---- Natural-stack ---- */
.glyph-group--natural-stack {
  flex-direction: column;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: flex-start;
  /* Gap BETWEEN glyphs in the stack — same as the visual gap between
     ungrouped glyphs in a horizontal line. */
  gap: 0.18em;
  /* Outer breathing room so two adjacent groups in a vertical column
     read as distinct units (~1.5x the inter-glyph gap). */
  margin: 0.4em 0;
}
.glyph-group--natural-stack .glyph-group__slot {
  display: inline-block;
  font-size: 1em;
  line-height: 1;
}

/* ---- Side-by-side ---- */
.glyph-group--side-by-side {
  flex-direction: row;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: space-between;
  width: 1em;
  height: 1em;
  /* Bigger inline-axis margin so adjacent horizontal groups don't
     run into thei

_... [truncated 629 chars] ..._
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  CartoucheView — renders the cartouche frame around its content.
 *
 *  Frame is drawn with an SVG that sits absolutely positioned behind

 *  the geometry to whatever the parent's actual box ends up being, so
 *  the frame always hugs the content regardless of how many glyphs

## [989] assistant

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/views/CartoucheView.css`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Cartouche styling.
 *
 *  Outer .cartouche is positioned (so the SVG frame can absolutely
 *  position relative to it) and padded enough that the glyphs never
 *  touch the frame. Padding is in `em` so it scales with font-size.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

.cartouche {
  position: relative;
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: baseline;
  color: #5b4126;
  /* Generous horizontal padding leaves room for the SVG's
     semicircular end-caps; vertical padding keeps glyphs from
     touching the top/bottom edges. */
  padding: 0.15em 0.55em 0.15em 0.7em;
  margin: 0 0.2em;
  line-height: 1;
}

.cartouche__frame {
  position: absolute;
  inset: 0;
  width: 100%;
  height: 100%;
  pointer-events: none;
  /* Slightly translucent so the frame reads as a frame and not a hard
     box — matches the JSesh "ink-on-papyrus" feel. */
  color: #5b4126;
  opacity: 0.95;
}

.cartouche__content {
  display: inline-block;
  position: relative;
  z-index: 1;
}

.cartouche.ProseMirror-selectednode {
  background: rgba(216, 168, 101, 0.18);
  border-radius: 6px;
}
```

## [991] assistant

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/views/VerticalRunView.css`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Vertical-run wrapper. Sets writing-mode so glyphs read top-to-bottom
 *  while neighbouring Latin text stays horizontal. Glyphs themselves
 *  reset writing-mode so the hieroglyph face renders upright inside
 *  each cell.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

.vertical-run {
  writing-mode: vertical-lr;
  text-orientation: upright;
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: top;
  white-space: nowrap;
  line-height: 1.1;
}

.vertical-run__content {
  display: inline-block;
  writing-mode: vertical-lr;
}

/* Glyph nodes inside the run should stay rendered in their natural
   coordinate system — only the layout is vertical. */
.vertical-run .hg,
.vertical-run .glyph-group,
.vertical-run .cartouche {
  writing-mode: horizontal-tb;
  text-orientation: mixed;
}
```

## [992] assistant

Now the editor mount itself, and a small hook for components to read the vertical-mode flag:

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/useVerticalMode.ts`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Subscribe to the editor's vertical-mode plugin state.
 *
 *  TipTap exposes editor state via React-friendly hooks, but the plugin
 *  state lives in ProseMirror and only updates on transactions. This
 *  hook keeps a React `useState` value in sync with the plugin so the
 *  toolbar can highlight the button and child components can re-render
 *  when the lock flips.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { useEffect, useState } from "react";
import type { Editor } from "@tiptap/react";
import { isVerticalModeOn } from "./extensions/verticalRun";

export const useVerticalMode = (editor: Editor | null): boolean => {
  const [on, setOn] = useState(false);

  useEffect(() => {
    if (!editor) return;
    const update = () => setOn(isVerticalModeOn(editor.state));
    update();
    editor.on("transaction", update);
    return () => {
      editor.off("transaction", update);
    };
  }, [editor]);

  return on;
};
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Editor mount.
 *
 *  Composes TipTap's StarterKit with our domain-specific extensions
 *  (Cartouche, GlyphGroup, VerticalRun, Rotation) and exposes the
 *  editor instance to the parent so the toolbar / palette can issue
 *  commands. Visual styling (font, padding, caret, selection colour)
 *  lives in editor.css so this file stays focused on wiring.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { EditorContent, useEditor } from "@tiptap/react";
import StarterKit from "@tiptap/starter-kit";
import { useEffect } from "react";
import {
  Cartouche,
  GlyphGroup,
  Rotation,
  VerticalRun,
} from "./extensions";
import "./Editor.css";

const INITIAL_CONTENT = `
  <p>Type or drop hieroglyphs here. The catalogue lives on the left.</p>
  <p>𓀀 𓁹 𓅓 𓏏𓂋𓏤 — selection, undo, copy/paste all work natively.</p>
  <p></p>
`.trim();

interface IProps {
  /** Bubble the editor instance up so the toolbar can dispatch
   *  commands and react to selection changes. */
  onReady: (editor: ReturnType<typeof useEditor>) => void;
  /** Current document direction (LTR / RTL). */
  direction: "ltr" | "rtl";
  /** Current base font-size in pixels. Hieroglyphs inherit this. */
  fontSize: number;
}

export const Editor = ({ onReady, direction, fontSize }: IProps) => {
  const editor = useEditor({
    extensions: [
      StarterKit.configure({
        // Disable the parts of StarterKit we don't want: code blocks,
        // blockquotes, headings (the prototype is a flat doc), and
        // strike. Keep paragraphs, bold, italic, history, etc.
        heading: false,
        codeBlock: false,
        blockquote: false,
        horizontalRule: false,
        bulletList: false,
        orderedList: false,
        listItem: false,
        code: false,
        strike: false,
      }),
      Cartouche,
      GlyphGroup,
      Rotation,
      VerticalRun,
    ],
    content: INITIAL_CONTENT,
    editorProps: {
      attributes: {
        class: "lotus-editor",
        spellcheck: "false",
        // role + aria-label hint for screen readers.
        role: "textbox",
        "aria-multiline": "true",
        "aria-label": "Hieroglyph editor",
      },
    },
  });

  useEffect(() => {
    if (editor) onReady(editor);
  }, [editor, onReady]);

  return (
    <div
      className="lotus-editor-host"
      data-direction={direction}
      style={{ fontSize: `${fontSize}px` }

## [994] assistant

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/Editor.css`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Editor visual styling.
 *
 *  The editor is just a `<div contenteditable>` underneath; TipTap
 *  doesn't dictate any styling. We:
 *
 *    - Apply a font-family chain that picks Inter for Latin and falls
 *      back to Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs for codepoints Inter
 *      doesn't cover. CSS does the per-character matching for free —
 *      no wrapper spans needed.
 *    - Pad the editor and give it a paper-like background.
 *    - Style the cursor, selection, and placeholder so editing feels
 *      polished.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

.lotus-editor-host {
  position: relative;
  height: 100%;
  overflow: auto;
  background: #fffaf2;
  border-radius: 0 0 6px 6px;
}

.lotus-editor-host[data-direction="rtl"] .lotus-editor {
  direction: rtl;
}

.lotus-editor {
  /* Inter for Latin, Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs as fallback for
     codepoints Inter doesn't have. Result: prose looks normal, glyphs
     get the hieroglyph face automatically. */
  font-family: "Inter", "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs", system-ui, sans-serif;
  outline: none;
  padding: 32px 40px;
  min-height: 100%;
  color: #1f2937;
  caret-color: #d8a865;
  line-height: 1.6;
  white-space: pre-wrap;
  word-wrap: break-word;
}

.lotus-editor p {
  margin: 0 0 0.6em;
  /* Slightly taller line so vertical runs and tall glyphs don't crash
     into the next line. */
  min-height: 1.6em;
}

.lotus-editor p:last-child {
  margin-bottom: 0;
}

.lotus-editor ::selection {
  background: rgba(216, 168, 101, 0.35);
}

.lotus-editor strong {
  font-weight: 700;
}

/* The hieroglyph font is much wider than Inter at the same em-size;
   bumping the per-character font-size feature for hieroglyphs alone
   isn't supported, so instead we make the surrounding line-height
   generous enough that big glyphs fit. */

/* Empty-paragraph placeholder for the first paragraph when the doc is
   blank — common UX nicety on rich editors. */
.lotus-editor p.is-empty:first-child::before {
  content: "Start typing or click a glyph on the left.";
  color: #b08d5e;
  pointer-events: none;
  height: 0;
  float: left;
}
```

```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Glyph palette (left sidebar).
 *
 *  Renders the catalogue as a grid of clickable tiles. Clicking inserts
 *  the glyph at the current caret position via the TipTap command chain
 *  — no DOM manipulation, no manual selection juggling. The Unicode
 *  character is the entire payload; the receiving editor relies on its
 *  font-family fallback chain to render it.
 *
 *  Drag-and-drop uses the `text/plain` MIME type: dropping a glyph into
 *  the editor inserts the character at the drop point natively, no
 *  extra plumbing needed.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import type { Editor } from "@tiptap/react";
import { GLYPHS } from "../data/glyphs";
import type { Glyph, GlyphType } from "../data/glyphs";
import "./GlyphPalette.css";

interface IProps {
  editor: Editor | null;
}

const TYPE_LABEL: Record<GlyphType, string> = {
  full: "Full",
  horizontal: "Horizontal",
  vertical: "Vertical",
  small: "Small",
};

/** Group the glyph catalogue by type — keeps the palette scannable. */
const groupByType = (glyphs: Glyph[]): Record<GlyphType, Glyph[]> => {
  const groups: Record<GlyphType, Glyph[]> = {
    full: [],
    horizontal: [],
    vertical: [],
    small: [],
  };
  for (const g of glyphs) groups[g.type].push(g);
  return groups;
};

export const GlyphPalette = ({ editor }: IProps) => {
  const grouped = groupByType(GLYPHS);

  const insert = (glyph: Glyph) => {
    if (!editor) return;
    editor.chain().focus().insertContent(glyph.char).run();
  };

  return (
    <aside className="palette" aria-label="Glyph catalogue">
      <header className="palette__header">
        <h2 className="palette__title">Glyphs</h2>
        <p className="palette__hint">Click to insert</p>
      </header>

      <div className="palette__sections">
        {(Object.keys(grouped) as GlyphType[]).map((type) => {
          const list = grouped[type];
          if (!list.length) return null;
          return (
            <section className="palette__section" key={type}>
              <h3 className="palette__section-title">{TYPE_LABEL[type]}</h3>
              <div className="palette__grid">
                {list.map((g) => (
                  <button
                    key={g.code}
                    type="button"
                    className="palette__tile"
                    title={`${g.code} — ${g.label}`}
                    draggable
     

_... [truncated 850 chars] ..._
