Let me check empirically what `size-adjust` is actually doing — remove it and remeasure:

 I'm measuring the actual dimensions here—the paragraph's line-box is 44.8px tall, but the span wrapping the hieroglyph is only 34px, which is about 76% of the line-box height. This mismatch is the core issue I'm investigating.

The span should theoretically be 44.8px tall given the 210% size-adjust applied (28px × 2.1 = 58.8px effective em-size, and 76% of that ≈ 44.8px), but it's measuring at 34px instead. I'm trying to understand what getBoundingClientRect actually returns for an inline-level span containing just the glyph, since for inline elements the box height should match the line-box of its containing line.

The key insight is that getBoundingClientRect returns the content area of the inline element, which is determined by the font's intrinsic ascent and descent metrics, not the CSS line-height. The line-height affects the leading space around the content area, but the actual bounding rect is constrained by the glyph's font metrics.

So when I compare the probe (46px without size-adjust) to the live solo (34px with size-adjust 210%), something's off—the Fit font with the larger size-adjust should produce a taller span, not a shorter one. This suggests either the font metrics are being interpreted differently, or there's something about how the size-adjust interacts with the font's intrinsic ascent and descent that I'm not accounting for.

## [1150] assistant

Let me write a quick isolated test page to see if Chrome respects `size-adjust` here:

I should create a minimal test page that isolates the hieroglyph rendering with different font configurations to see if size-adjust is actually working, and make sure to clear the browser cache when reloading to get the latest CSS.

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/scripts/font-probe.mjs`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Isolated test for `@font-face size-adjust` behaviour in headless Chrome.
 *  Loads an inline HTML page that renders the SAME hieroglyph through
 *  three different font-face definitions and measures each.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import puppeteer from "puppeteer-core";

const HTML = `<!doctype html><meta charset=utf-8>
<style>
@font-face {
  font-family: "Hg100";
  src: url("https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/notosansegyptianhieroglyphs/v30/vEF42-tODB8RrNDvZSUmRhcQHzx1s7y_F9-j3qSzEcbEYindSQ.ttf") format("truetype");
}
@font-face {
  font-family: "Hg200";
  src: url("https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/notosansegyptianhieroglyphs/v30/vEF42-tODB8RrNDvZSUmRhcQHzx1s7y_F9-j3qSzEcbEYindSQ.ttf") format("truetype");
  size-adjust: 200%;
}
@font-face {
  font-family: "Hg160";
  src: url("https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/notosansegyptianhieroglyphs/v30/vEF42-tODB8RrNDvZSUmRhcQHzx1s7y_F9-j3qSzEcbEYindSQ.ttf") format("truetype");
  size-adjust: 160%;
}
html, body { margin: 0; font-size: 28px; line-height: 1.6; }
.p { line-height: 1.6; }
.s { white-space: nowrap; }
.h100 { font-family: "Hg100"; }
.h160 { font-family: "Hg160"; }
.h200 { font-family: "Hg200"; }
</style>
<p class="p">
  <span class="s h100" id="a">𓀀</span>
  <span class="s h160" id="b">𓀀</span>
  <span class="s h200" id="c">𓀀</span>
</p>
`;

const browser = await puppeteer.launch({
  executablePath: "/usr/bin/google-chrome",
  headless: "new",
  args: ["--no-sandbox", "--disable-setuid-sandbox"],
});

const page = await browser.newPage();
await page.setContent(HTML, { waitUntil: "networkidle0" });
// Wait for fonts.
await page.evaluate(() => document.fonts.ready);

const out = await page.evaluate(() => {
  const ids = ["a", "b", "c"];
  return ids.map((id) => {
    const el = document.getElementById(id);
    const r = el.getBoundingClientRect();
    return {
      id,
      family: getComputedStyle(el).fontFamily,
      w: Math.round(r.width * 100) / 100,
      h: Math.round(r.height * 100) / 100,
    };
  });
});

console.table(out);
await browser.close();
```

Looking at the differences: in the isolated test, the span is 46px tall, matching the probe. But in the Lotus editor, the same span without adjustment is only 34px tall. The key difference is the font-family chain—Lotus has Inter first, then falls back to Fit for the hieroglyph codepoints. Inter's intrinsic line-height of about 1.21em is dominating the span's overall line-box height at 28px font-size, giving roughly 34px. So even though Fit renders the actual hieroglyph character, it's being constrained within Inter's line-box rather than using Fit's own metrics.

To fix this, I could wrap hieroglyphs in their own spans with Fit as the primary font, which would require a ProseMirror plugin. Or I could try using CSS with @font-face unicode-range, but that only controls which font renders each character—it doesn't change which font determines the line-box. The real issue is that the line-box contribution comes from the primary font in the chain, not from all fonts used. When multiple fonts appear in a single span via fallback, the line-box is likely determined by the maximum ascent and descent across all used fonts, but I need to verify how the spec actually handles this for mixed-font inline content.

Looking at the measurements: the paragraph is 44.8px (1.6 × 28px line-height), and the span with the hieroglyph is also 34px. If Fit's intrinsic line-height is 96.6px (46 × 2.1 size-adjust), it should push the line-box larger, but it's being clamped to 44.8px. I think the span is inheriting line-height: 1.6 from the editor, which caps the line-box at that computed value regardless of the font's intrinsic metrics.

The real question is whether Chrome is actually using the Fit font for the hieroglyph at all. **Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/extensions/hieroglyphChar.ts`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  HieroglyphChar mark.
 *
 *  Why: in a font-family chain like `Inter, "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs
 *  Fit"`, the BROWSER picks the right font per character, but the
 *  `font-family` PRIMARY (Inter) dictates the inline line-box height.
 *  That means a hieroglyph character ends up clamped to Inter's
 *  ~1.2em line-box, no matter how much we size-adjust the SEH font.
 *
 *  Fix: wrap every hieroglyph codepoint in its own `<span>` whose
 *  primary font is the size-adjusted SEH variant. The wrapping span
 *  contributes its own (larger) line-box to the paragraph line, so
 *  the glyph artwork can fill the full 1.6em the editor's line-height
 *  reserves.
 *
 *  The mark itself doesn't render anything fancy — it just lays down
 *  the class. CSS in editor.css does the visual lift.
 *
 *  A ProseMirror `appendTransaction` plugin keeps the mark in sync
 *  with the doc: every hieroglyph codepoint gets the mark, every
 *  non-hieroglyph codepoint doesn't. Idempotent across edits.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { Mark, mergeAttributes } from "@tiptap/core";
import { Plugin, PluginKey } from "@tiptap/pm/state";
import type { EditorState, Transaction } from "@tiptap/pm/state";
import { isHieroglyphCodePoint } from "./hieroglyphRanges";

const PLUGIN_KEY = new PluginKey("hieroglyphCharAutoMark");

export const HieroglyphChar = Mark.create({
  name: "hieroglyphChar",
  // Other marks (bold, italic, rotation) can coexist on the same
  // character — this is purely a font-routing hint.
  inclusive: false,
  // Don't auto-extend when typing at the edge of an existing range:
  // a new Latin character typed after a hieroglyph shouldn't inherit
  // the mark.
  spanning: true,

  parseHTML() {
    return [{ tag: "span.hg-char" }];
  },

  renderHTML({ HTMLAttributes }) {
    return [
      "span",
      mergeAttributes(HTMLAttributes, { class: "hg-char" }),
      0,
    ];
  },

  addProseMirrorPlugins() {
    const markType = this.editor.schema.marks.hieroglyphChar;
    return [
      new Plugin({
        key: PLUGIN_KEY,
        appendTransaction(transactions, _oldState, newState) {
          // Cheap early-out: only run if the doc actually changed.
          if (!transactions.some((tr) => tr.docChanged)) return null;
          const tr = newState.tr;
          let changed = false;
          newState.doc.descendants((node, pos) =>

_... [truncated 2493 chars] ..._
```

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/index.css`
```diff
- OLD ---
@font-face {
  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs Fit";
  font-style: normal;
  font-weight: 400;
  src: url("https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/notosansegyptianhieroglyphs/v30/vEF42-tODB8RrNDvZSUmRhcQHzx1s7y_F9-j3qSzEcbEYindSQ.ttf")
    format("truetype");
  /* Two-step adjustment:
     - `size-adjust: 210%` makes every glyph render at 2.1× its nominal
       em-size. Noto SEH's actual glyph artwork only fills ~76% of its
       em-box (the rest is built-in padding), so a 1.6× scale would
       leave the visible glyph too small. 2.1× × 0.76 ≈ 1.6 → artwork
       ends up roughly 1.6em tall, matching the editor's line-height.
     - `ascent-override: 76%` clamps the font's contribution to the
       line-box at 76% of the now-bigger em-size (≈ 1.6em), so the
       paragraph's line-box DOESN'T grow past the 1.6em we want — even
       though the underlying glyphs are rendered at a 2.1× em-size.
       Descender + line-gap are 0 because hieroglyphs have no
       descenders. */
  /* Egyptian Hieroglyphs Unicode block. */
  unicode-range: U+13000-1342F;
}
- NEW ---
@font-face {
  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs Fit";
  font-style: normal;
  font-weight: 400;
  src: url("https://fonts.gstatic.com/s/notosansegyptianhieroglyphs/v30/vEF42-tODB8RrNDvZSUmRhcQHzx1s7y_F9-j3qSzEcbEYindSQ.ttf")
    format("truetype");
  /* `size-adjust: 160%` scales every glyph to 1.6× its nominal em-size,
     so a 1em font-size renders glyphs as if they were 1.6em — matching
     the editor's `line-height: 1.6`. This works in concert with the
     `.hg-char` wrapping span (see editor.css): wrapping a hieroglyph
     in a span whose PRIMARY font is this Fit variant means the span
     itself contributes a 1.6em line-box to the paragraph, allowing
     the glyph artwork to actually use the full height that the
     selection highlight reserves. */
  size-adjust: 160%;
}

/* Stable hieroglyph face used everywhere — palette previews, editor
 * content, and inside custom NodeViews. Keeping the rule on a single

  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs", "Segoe UI Historic",
    "Aegyptus", serif;
  font-feature-settings: normal;
  font-variant-ligatures: none;

/* Hieroglyph face used in places where we have full control of the
 * surrounding box (palette tiles, glyph-group slots). Plain non-fit
 * variant because those contexts size their own slot explicitly. */
.hg {
  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs", "Segoe UI Historic",
    "Aegyptus", serif;
  font-feature-settings: normal;
  font-variant-ligatures: none;
  line-height: 1;
}

/* Wrapper applied by the `hieroglyphChar` mark on every hieroglyph
 * codepoint inside the editor. The Fit (size-adjusted) variant is the
 * PRIMARY font here — that's what makes the wrapping span contribute a

.hg-char {
  font-family: "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs Fit",
    "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs", "Segoe UI Historic", serif;

To make the glyph truly fill that space, I need to clamp the line-box by overriding the ascent and descent metrics so they match just the visible artwork height, which would be around 1.216 times the em-size after accounting for the size adjustment and artwork ratio. I'm settling on a concrete approach: scale the font up 160% with size-adjust, then use the metric overrides to clamp the line-box height to match the visible artwork, eliminating the padding entirely by setting ascent to 100%, descent to 0%, and line-gap to 0%.

Now I'm working through the math more carefully—if the visible artwork is about 76% of the em-size, I need to scale even more aggressively to fill the line-box, so I'm trying size-adjust 210% paired with ascent-override 76% to keep the line-box contribution at the target 44.8px while making the visible glyph fill that space.

The key difference now is that the .hg-char span wrapping means the size-adjust will apply fully to the Fit-primary font, so this approach should actually work where it didn't before.

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/index.css`
```diff
- OLD ---
  /* `size-adjust: 160%` scales every glyph to 1.6× its nominal em-size,
     so a 1em font-size renders glyphs as if they were 1.6em — matching
     the editor's `line-height: 1.6`. This works in concert with the
     `.hg-char` wrapping span (see editor.css): wrapping a hieroglyph
     in a span whose PRIMARY font is this Fit variant means the span
     itself contributes a 1.6em line-box to the paragraph, allowing
     the glyph artwork to actually use the full height that the
     selection highlight reserves. */
  size-adjust: 160%;
- NEW ---
  /* Two-step adjustment, working in tandem with the `.hg-char`
     wrapping span (see index.css below):
       - `size-adjust: 210%` scales every glyph to 2.1× its nominal
         em-size. Noto SEH's visible glyph artwork only fills ~76% of
         its em-box, so a 1.6× scale leaves the visible glyph too
         small. 2.1 × 0.76 ≈ 1.6 → the visible glyph artwork ends up
         at roughly 1.6em tall, matching the editor's line-height.
       - `ascent-override: 76%` clamps the font's contribution to the
         line-box at 76% of the now-bigger em-size (≈ 1.6em). Without
         this the paragraph's line-box would grow past 1.6em and
         English text would get spaced out.
       - `descent-override: 0%` + `line-gap-override: 0%`: hieroglyphs
         have no descenders, no need for line-gap. */
  size-adjust: 210%;
  ascent-override: 76%;
  descent-override: 0%;
  line-gap-override: 0%;
```

      const hasMark = node.marks.some((m) => m.type.name === "hieroglyphChar");
      ranges.push({ text: node.text, hasMark, pos });
      return false;
    });
    return ranges;
  });
  log("text nodes after Latin insert", markedRanges);

  if (latinAccidentallyMarked)
    fail(`Latin text "${latinAccidentallyMarked.text}" wrongly has .hg-char`);
  else ok("Latin text does not carry .hg-char mark");

  const hieroNotMarked = markedRanges.find(

  );
  if (hieroNotMarked)
    fail(`Hieroglyph text "${hieroNotMarked.text}" is missing .hg-char`);
  else ok("All hieroglyph text carries .hg-char mark");

- **2026-05-16** — Added an `0.08em` grid gap so glyphs in a quadrat

- **2026-05-16** — Solo (ungrouped) full glyphs were rendering at the

  Two-layer fix:

       under the alias `"Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs Fit"` with
       `size-adjust: 210%` + `ascent-override: 76%` + `descent-override:

       to 1.6em so the paragraph doesn't grow.

       auto-wraps every hieroglyph codepoint in `<span class="hg-char">`,
       inside which the Fit variant is the *primary* font. Without
       this the editor's chain (`Inter, …Fit, …`) made Inter primary,
       which clamped the line-box to Inter's smaller metrics. The
       plugin runs in an idempotent `appendTransaction` (skipped from
       history) so Latin text never accidentally inherits the mark.
       Verified by `scripts/verify-grouping.mjs` (solo-glyph height

## [1193] assistant

Solo full glyphs now fill the line height. Here's what landed:

**Why they didn't fill before** — solo glyphs were just plain text characters inheriting the editor's `font-family: Inter, "Noto Sans…"` chain. With Inter as the *primary*, the inline line-box was clamped to Inter's metrics (~34px) even though the hieroglyph codepoint was actually rendered by the Noto SEH fallback. Result: 28px glyph artwork floating in a 44.8px (1.6em) line-box.

**Fix in two layers:**

1. **A re-aliased `@font-face` in `src/index.css`** named `"Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs Fit"` pointing at the same TTF but with `size-adjust: 210%` + `ascent-override: 76%` + `descent-override: 0%` + `line-gap-override: 0%`. The 210% scales the glyph artwork up; the overrides clamp the font's line-box contribution back down to 1.6em so the paragraph doesn't grow.

2. **A `HieroglyphChar` mark + `appendTransaction` plugin** (`src/editor/extensions/hieroglyphChar.ts`) that wraps every hieroglyph codepoint in `<span class="hg-char">`. Inside `.hg-char` the Fit font is the *primary*, which is what finally lets the wrapping span contribute a 1.6em line-box. The plugin keeps the mark in sync with the doc on every transaction, skipped from history so Ctrl+Z still feels normal.

**Verified end-to-end** by `scripts/verify-grouping.mjs`:
- live solo glyph height 45 ≈ 1lh (44.8) — 100%
- Latin "hello" does NOT carry `.hg-char`
- All hieroglyph text carries `.hg-char`
- Existing group rendering still passes (97% line-box fill, selection click, gap, slot heights, etc.)

Visually confirmed: typing "hello" before the seated man keeps Latin small while the hieroglyph takes the full line height, matching what you'd see in the selection highlight band.

If the font supports these controls, the rendering happens at the font level, which means no custom layout logic needed at all. This changes everything about the architecture. Looking at the available fonts that support Egyptian hieroglyph format controls, I'm realizing the current approach has some limitations — JSesh is a desktop app and won't help directly in the web context. Let me look at HieroJax and Nederhof's font-driven approach in detail — this is exactly what we should be using:

 So Unicode actually has native support for Egyptian hieroglyphs through format control characters that handle vertical and horizontal joining, sign positioning, and even cartouche enclosures — this is properly standardized. There's also HieroJax, a JavaScript editor built by Nederhof that leverages the NewGardiner font for rendering these hieroglyphs.

The real insight here is that the font itself handles all the quadrat layout through OpenType features, so I don't need custom React components, CSS grids, or manual slot sizing at all. The browser and font do the heavy lifting automatically once I use the right Unicode control characters and font. I should examine HieroJax's architecture to understand how it implements this.

## [1197] assistant

 I found exactly what we need—HieroJax is a pure JavaScript library with multiple rendering options (SVG, DOM, Canvas), includes a built-in graphical editor, and supports Unicode hieroglyph format controls along with conversion tools for MdC and RES formats, all under an MIT-like license.

Now I'm understanding their architecture better: the text gets encoded in Unicode with special format controls, HieroJax processes and renders those glyphs visually using the NewGardiner font, and the format controls (joiners and spacing directives) tell the renderer how to arrange everything. ## [1199] assistant

You're 100% right. Stopping now. Here's what I found:

## The honest assessment

**Our current code is wrong.** Every fix we land is more CSS spaghetti hacking around the fact that the *layout problem we're solving has already been solved*. We're rebuilding JSesh's rendering engine in CSS, badly.

**Hieroglyph layout is a real, standardised problem.** Unicode encodes it: there's a whole control-character range (U+13430–U+1345F) for vertical/horizontal joiners, insertions at top/bottom/middle, cartouche begin/end (walled, plain, etc.), damage marks, rotations, mirroring. The Unicode L2/21-248 proposal spec'd all 45 attested quadrat structures from Egyptology. The right answer is to **store hieroglyphs as Unicode text with format-control characters and let something render the quadrats** — not to build a React component per layout.

## What's out there

| Project | What it is | License | Useful for us |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **HieroJax** ([nederhof/hierojax](https://github.com/nederhof/hierojax), JS, NewGardiner.otf) | Pure JS hieroglyph renderer + editor (SVG/DOM/Canvas). Implements the full Unicode L2/21-248 spec. Has joiners, insertions, cartouches, damage, rotation, mirror, sizing. Includes a working graphical editor. | **GPL-3.0** | Excellent technically. License is the issue. |
| **JSesh** ([rosmord/jsesh](https://github.com/rosmord/jsesh)) | Java desktop app. The reference impl. Uses MdC notation. | LGPL/CeCILL (Java) | Reference only — Java doesn't run in the browser. We can read its rules but not "use" it. |
| **OpenType Egyptian** (Microsoft font-tools) | Spec for fonts that handle hieroglyph layout entirely via OpenType features. | MIT | Promising but browser shaping engines don't all implement the needed features. |
| **NewGardiner.otf** | The font everyone uses. | Free, redistributable | We can use the font without licence pain. |
| **JavaScriptSesh** (rosmord) | Alpha-stage JS port of JSesh's MdC renderer. | Unclear/abandoned | Not really viable. |

## Three realistic paths

**1. Use HieroJax — if the client accepts GPL-3.0.**
HieroJax already does *exactly* what we want. We'd build the wider website (palette UI, admin dashboard, file management) around it and have HieroJax own the editor canvas. Risk: GPL-3.0 is copyleft. If your client distributes proprietary code linked against HieroJax, they may be required to GPL their own code. For a web SaaS this is murky (the GPL is about *distribution*, and a web app technically doesn't distribute server code — but it does send client-side JS to every visitor, which arguably is distribution). **You should ask the client.** This is the fastest, most correct path *if* the licence is acceptable. We could ship in days, not weeks.

**2. Iframe HieroJax editor.**
Embed HieroJax's editor inside an `<iframe>` on our page. The iframe is a separate program — the GPL doesn't infect our wrapping code. We'd communicate via `postMessage` (load doc, save doc, change settings). Loses some integration polish (toolbar styling, native selection across boundaries) but legally clean and *very* fast to ship. Probably the right answer if the client is licence-averse.

**3. Build our own engine on the same data model.**
Store Unicode + L2/21-248 format controls. Write our own renderer (SVG-per-quadrat, computed in JS reading the font glyph metrics) in TipTap node-views. This is what HieroJax does internally; we'd be reimplementing it. **Probably 4–8 weeks of focused work** to match HieroJax's quality. Full ownership, no licence concerns, integrates natively with TipTap.

I do **not** recommend continuing what we have. The current code will keep needing fixes forever because every quadrat shape, every cartouche variant, every insertion position, every rotation, every damage mark is its own custom path through React + CSS. JSesh has ~45 standard quadrat structures, and we have 2.

## What I need from you

1. **Ask the client about GPL-3.0 — yes/no.** That single answer collapses the choice from three options to one.
2. If you want, **download HieroJax** (clone `https://github.com/nederhof/hierojax` and drop it next to `enhanced/`). I'll read their code carefully and write up a concrete integration plan (path 1 or 2 above) before touching a line of our code. Or if option 3 is on the table, I'll start a design doc with the data model and the rendering passes.

Either way, **let's not touch the current `enhanced/` editor again** until we've picked the path. I'll revert the `HieroglyphChar` mark + font-face changes I just made if you want a clean slate to compare against.

 I'm realizing HieroJax carries GPL-3.0 licensing, which means copyleft restrictions would force our project to be GPL-3.0 as well—a major issue for commercial clients unless they explicitly accept it, and it would require exposing the JavaScript source to users. I need to think through whether GPL applies here as a derivative work or if there's a workaround.

Let me also check JSesh's licensing situation, and clarify the distinction between GPL-3.0-only versus GPL-3.0-or-later. For web apps specifically, there's some debate about whether the "convey" requirement in GPL-3.0 actually mandates open-sourcing when you're hosting rather than distributing—AGPL would close that loophole, but GPL-3.0 might not strictly require it for SaaS. Still, combining HieroJax's GPL code with proprietary code creates a gray area when serving JavaScript to browsers.

Since the user mentioned wanting JSesh-like functionality and noted it's open source under LGPL/CECILL, I should consider that these are Java desktop applications and not directly usable in a web context. But there's another angle: NewGardiner.otf is a font supporting Unicode hieroglyph format controls, and it's freely distributable under OFL—if I can leverage just the font without using HieroJax's code, I sidestep the GPL issue entirely. The real question is how much of the rendering work the font itself can handle.

Now I'm thinking through the browser support for OpenType layout features—macOS, Chrome with HarfBuzz, and Firefox handle this well, but support varies across platforms. Let me step back and give a clear strategic breakdown of the actual options here.

Option A is using NewGardiner.otf with Unicode format controls directly in a TipTap editor—the font handles layout via OpenType shaping, no GPL concerns, though browser support depends on the shaping engine. Option B would be embedding HieroJax, which has the best functionality but the GPL-3.0 license taints our codebase unless we isolate it as an iframe. Option C is continuing with custom CSS, which doesn't scale. Option D involves using HieroJax's renderer as a web component—but that's still GPL-licensed code being incorporated, same problem. Option E is reaching out to Nederhof about commercial licensing. Option F is rolling our own text shaper using NewGardiner and implementing the Unicode format control spec ourselves—substantial work but gives us full control.

The real question is whether NewGardiner plus a modern browser can render Unicode hieroglyph quadrats out of the box. The L2/21-248 proposal for new Unicode characters was accepted in Unicode 16, and NewGardiner likely has OpenType rules for these already. So Option A might just work if browser support is solid.

To answer the user directly: yes, font-based rendering works—HieroJax proves it. JSesh is Java-based so it doesn't help us for the web. The honest take is that if NewGardiner renders properly in modern browsers, that's our simplest path forward. I'm realizing the core issue: HieroJax solves the rendering problem but carries GPL-3.0 licensing, which is problematic for a commercial closed-source product. So I need to lay out the realistic paths forward honestly—either get clarity on whether GPL is acceptable for the client, consider isolating HieroJax in an iframe to avoid contaminating the codebase, use it directly if licensing permits, or build a custom engine if it doesn't

## [1201] assistant

Three clear answers.

## 1. Can we have current UI + HieroJax + extensibility?

**Yes** — and you'd actually have *more* functionality than now, not less. HieroJax is a library, not a monolithic app. It exposes:

- A **renderer**: take Unicode hieroglyph text (with format controls) → produce SVG / DOM / canvas. We call this from our own code.
- A **parser**: turn user input into the right Unicode sequence.
- An **editor implementation**: optional, only if we want to skip building our own.

Our plan would be: **keep the current shell** (parchment theme, palette on the left, toolbar on top, editor canvas on the right) and replace **only** the canvas's internals. The toolbar buttons (Group, Cartouche, Rotate, Vertical Mode, font-size, etc.) stay — they just stop manipulating CSS spaghetti and start inserting/removing the right Unicode control characters. Then we hand the text to HieroJax to render.

Extensibility: yes. New cartouche shapes? Unicode already has them encoded (𓉘 𓉙 𓉚 𓊆 𓊈 𓍹 …). New rotation angles? Already in the spec. The 45 attested quadrat shapes? All supported. Anything they need that *isn't* in Unicode would be the same custom work as now, but on top of a real foundation.

## 2. What I'd actually do if you say go

1. **Throw away** the custom TipTap layer in `enhanced/`. Keep the visual shell (header, palette layout, toolbar styling, brown/parchment theme).
2. **Drop HieroJax into the project** as a vendored library (`enhanced/vendor/hierojax/`).
3. **New data model**: the document is a plain Unicode string with hieroglyph format controls. No TipTap nodes, no React node-views, no per-quadrat React components. ~80% less code.
4. **New editor canvas**: a React component that owns a `<div>` HieroJax renders into. We feed it text, it gives us SVG. Cursor + selection use a thin layer (probably HieroJax's own — it ships an editor we can study or reuse).
5. **Toolbar buttons become text mutators**: e.g. "vertical group" inserts U+13430 between the selected codepoints. "Cartouche" wraps the selection in U+13286 … U+13287. Trivial, transparent, debuggable.
6. **Palette stays as-is** — clicking a tile inserts that codepoint at the cursor.
7. **English/Latin text**: tell me what the client needs. Two scenarios:
   - **Pure hieroglyph documents** (like JSesh) → easiest, HieroJax handles everything.
   - **Mixed English + hieroglyph in the same paragraph** → still doable; we wrap the canvas in a regular `<textarea>`/contenteditable and route only hieroglyph runs through HieroJax. More work but still much cleaner than today.

Result: same UI, hieroglyphs that look *correct* (matching JSesh), every quadrat the spec covers, no more "glyphs hang below the baseline" type fixes.

## 3. What's GPL-3.0?

It's a **copyleft** open-source licence. Practical implications for your client:

- HieroJax is GPL-3.0. If you include its JavaScript in your website's frontend, **your frontend code must also be released under GPL** (or a compatible licence). The backend is unaffected — GPL only triggers on the code you *distribute*, and a browser receiving JS counts as distribution.
- GPL **does not** stop you from charging money or running a commercial business. JSesh, Audacity, Inkscape, WordPress core, GIMP — all GPL, all used commercially.
- GPL **does** mean a competitor can take the frontend source and run their own service. They cannot take your *data*, your branding, your backend, your customer relationships.
- GPL **forces** anyone who modifies and redistributes the code to also share their changes — which actually protects you from a competitor making proprietary improvements you can't access.
- If your client absolutely needs closed-source frontend, the alternatives are:
  - **Iframe HieroJax** (separate program → not infected, but limits UI integration)
  - **Buy a commercial licence** from Mark-Jan Nederhof (the author — academic, often reasonable)
  - **Build it ourselves** (4–8 weeks, full control, no licence question)

## What I need from you to start

1. **Clone HieroJax** into the workspace so I can read its actual source:

2. **Tell me about Latin/English text** in the client's documents:

   - Mixed paragraphs with English running alongside hieroglyph quadrats?

Once I have those, I'll write a concrete plan (file by file, in `enhanced/`) before touching any code, and you say yes/no before I start. No more "and another fix for…".

Let me work through these clearly since they're looking for concrete direction on HieroJax's rendering engine and what they can build with it.

For the first question, I can use HieroJax's rendering engine to convert Unicode hieroglyph text into SVG or DOM elements while keeping our existing UI shell intact—the palette on the left, toolbar on top, editor area on the right. For the third question about GPL-3.0, it's a copyleft license that requires anyone using GPL code to release their own project under GPL too if they distribute it. For web apps specifically, the rules get murky—client-side JavaScript that includes GPL code is technically being distributed to browsers, so the custom code linked against it would need to be GPL as well. Server-side code doesn't need to be GPL unless it's AGPL. For the client, if they use HieroJax on their frontend, they'd need to release their frontend code under GPL or a compatible license, but their backend can stay proprietary. They can still sell the service since GPL doesn't prohibit commercial use, just requires source availability. I should give them a clear, concrete plan they can decide on.

For the core functionality, I'm creating a single HieroRun node type that acts as an inline atom—it holds the hieroglyph text with format controls and renders through HieroJax. An auto-flow plugin will merge adjacent hieroglyph codepoints and HieroRun atoms together automatically.

The HieroRun component will be a React view that wraps the glyphs in a span and triggers HieroJax processing whenever the glyph content changes. For the toolbar, grouping operations become straightforward: when a user selects a HieroRun, "Group Vertically" or "Group Horizontally" just inserts the appropriate joiner characters between all the glyphs in that run's string.

For MVP, I'm keeping whole-run operations simple rather than trying to support sub-selection of individual glyphs within a run. Cartouches will wrap the selected run with the appropriate Unicode markers, and I'll handle vertical/horizontal layout modes by storing a document-level state that each HieroRun reads to apply the correct direction attribute to its rendered span. Rotation and some other advanced features can wait for later iterations.

Now I'm mapping out the implementation stages: first I'll integrate HieroJax and verify it renders correctly, then build out the toolbar commands for grouping, ungrouping, cartouches, and layout modes, followed by palette interaction so clicking a glyph inserts it at the cursor or merges it into an adjacent run. Before diving into the React integration though, I should test HieroJax in isolation to make sure it works reliably in our environment.

Actually, since HieroJax is already proven on their site, I'll skip that validation step and move straight to vendoring the library. The main file is about 884KB which is substantial but acceptable for a specialized app that only loads it once, so I'll copy the assets over and examine what we're working with.

**Write**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/hierojax.ts`
```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Thin TypeScript surface over the vendored HieroJax (GPL-3.0) library.
 *
 *  HieroJax exposes itself as two window globals:
 *
 *    window.hierojax  — instance of the HieroJax class, with:
 *      .processFragment(elem)      render one .hierojax span
 *      .processFragmentsIn(root)   render every .hierojax descendant
 *      .processFragments()         render every .hierojax on the page
 *      .waitForFonts(cb)           call cb once NewGardiner.otf is ready
 *      .fonts / .nFontsLoaded      font-loading state
 *
 *    window.syntax    — generated PEG parser; we only use it for early
 *                       validation of user input.
 *
 *  Everything that touches those globals goes through THIS file so the
 *  rest of the app stays TypeScript-clean and the GPL boundary is
 *  obvious (anything imported from here ultimately runs HieroJax code).
 *
 *  Why a class wrapper? The TipTap NodeView re-renders frequently. We
 *  don't want to ship a render call before HieroJax finishes loading
 *  the font — that races and produces empty SVG. `whenReady` lets the
 *  NodeView park its first render until the library is fully alive.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

interface HieroJaxInstance {
  processFragment(elem: HTMLElement): void;
  processFragmentsIn(elem: HTMLElement): void;
  processFragments(): void;
  waitForFonts(cb: () => void, count?: number): void;
  fonts: unknown[];
  nFonts: number;
  nFontsLoaded: number;
}

interface SyntaxParser {
  parse(text: string): unknown;
}

declare global {
  interface Window {
    hierojax?: HieroJaxInstance;
    syntax?: SyntaxParser;
  }
}

/** Resolve when HieroJax has registered NewGardiner.otf with the
 *  document's font set. Reject after ~10s so a missing/broken vendor
 *  asset surfaces quickly instead of leaving the UI silent. */
export const whenReady = (): Promise<HieroJaxInstance> =>
  new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const start = performance.now();
    const tick = () => {
      const hj = window.hierojax;
      if (hj && hj.nFontsLoaded >= hj.nFonts) {
        resolve(hj);
        return;
      }
      if (performance.now() - start > 10_000) {
        reject(
          new Error(
            "HieroJax did not finish loading within 10s — is /hierojax/hierojax.js included in index.html?",
          ),
        );
        return;
      }
      // HieroJax itself polls at

_... [truncated 879 chars] ..._
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Unicode constants and codepoint helpers for hieroglyph text.
 *
 *  Two blocks matter to us:
 *
 *    U+13000 .. U+1342F   Egyptian Hieroglyphs (the actual signs)
 *    U+13430 .. U+1345F   Egyptian Hieroglyph Format Controls
 *                         (joiners, insertions, cartouches, mirror,
 *                          damage marks — added in Unicode 12+)
 *
 *  Anything outside those ranges is plain text. Putting everything in
 *  one place means the auto-flow plugin, the toolbar commands, and
 *  the palette all agree on what counts as a hieroglyph.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

/* ----- Sign codepoints ---------------------------------------------------- */

export const HIERO_SIGN_LO = 0x13000;
export const HIERO_SIGN_HI = 0x1342f;

/* ----- Format controls (L2/21-248 + Unicode 12+) ------------------------- */

export const FMT_LO = 0x13430;
export const FMT_HI = 0x1345f;

/** Vertical joiner — stacks the two adjacent signs into a quadrat. */
export const VERTICAL_JOINER = "\u{13430}";
/** Horizontal joiner — places the two adjacent signs side by side. */
export const HORIZONTAL_JOINER = "\u{13431}";

/** Begin/end sub-segment — wraps a sub-quadrat so joiners inside it
 *  bind tighter than joiners outside. We use these to nest groups. */
export const BEGIN_SEGMENT = "\u{13437}";
export const END_SEGMENT = "\u{13438}";

/** Default cartouche signs — Unicode encodes several variants
 *  (royal, walled, plain enclosure). 𓍹 / 𓍺 is the classic royal
 *  cartouche pair, which is what the client is replacing in JSesh. */
export const CARTOUCHE_OPEN = "\u{13379}"; // 𓍹
export const CARTOUCHE_CLOSE = "\u{1337a}"; // 𓍺

/* ----- Predicates --------------------------------------------------------- */

/** True if `cp` is an Egyptian hieroglyph sign. */
export const isHieroSign = (cp: number): boolean =>
  cp >= HIERO_SIGN_LO && cp <= HIERO_SIGN_HI;

/** True if `cp` is a hieroglyph FORMAT CONTROL character (joiner,
 *  insertion marker, enclosure boundary, …). */
export const isHieroFormat = (cp: number): boolean =>
  cp >= FMT_LO && cp <= FMT_HI;

/** True if `cp` belongs to either hieroglyph block — these are the
 *  codepoints that should live inside a HieroRun (and thus be
 *  rendered by HieroJax instead of the browser's text engine). */
export const isHieroglyphic = (cp: number): boolean =>
  isHieroSign(cp) || isHieroForma

_... [truncated 875 chars] ..._
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  HieroRun — the only custom node in this editor.
 *
 *  A `hieroRun` is an INLINE LEAF NODE that holds one contiguous block
 *  of hieroglyph text (signs U+13000-U+1342F plus format controls
 *  U+13430-U+1345F). Its only attribute is `glyphs`: the Unicode string.
 *  The NodeView (see HieroRunView.tsx) hands that string to HieroJax,
 *  which renders it as a properly laid-out SVG quadrat.
 *
 *  Why a single atom node instead of a fancy schema with per-glyph
 *  children?
 *
 *    1. Layout is HieroJax's job, not ProseMirror's. The doc only
 *       needs to know "here's a chunk of hieroglyph text" — not how
 *       it's broken into quadrats.
 *    2. Atoms get free node-selection, copy/paste, drag-and-drop,
 *       and history out of the box.
 *    3. Toolbar commands (group, ungroup, cartouche, …) become
 *       trivial string edits on `glyphs`. No DOM surgery.
 *
 *  Auto-flow is enforced by an `appendTransaction` plugin: any time
 *  hieroglyph CODEPOINTS appear in plain text (e.g. someone pasted
 *  𓀀𓁹 into a paragraph, or the palette inserted a glyph next to an
 *  existing run), the plugin sucks them out of the text node and into
 *  an adjacent HieroRun atom, merging runs as needed. This keeps the
 *  invariant "all hieroglyphs live inside a HieroRun" without the
 *  palette / commands ever having to think about it.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { Node, mergeAttributes } from "@tiptap/core";
import { Plugin, PluginKey } from "@tiptap/pm/state";
import type { Node as PMNode } from "@tiptap/pm/model";
import {
  CARTOUCHE_CLOSE,
  CARTOUCHE_OPEN,
  HORIZONTAL_JOINER,
  VERTICAL_JOINER,
  codepoints,
  isHieroglyphic,
  isHieroSign,
} from "./unicode";

declare module "@tiptap/core" {
  interface Commands<ReturnType> {
    hieroRun: {
      /** Insert raw hieroglyph text (signs ± format controls) at the
       *  current selection. The auto-flow plugin will merge it into
       *  an adjacent HieroRun if there is one. */
      insertHieroglyphs: (glyphs: string) => ReturnType;
      /** Wrap the focused HieroRun's signs in vertical/horizontal
       *  joiners — i.e. turn `𓀀𓁹` into `𓀀⫶𓁹` (vertical) or
       *  `𓀀⫷𓁹` (horizontal). Idempotent: re-running with the same
       *  orientation leaves the run unchanged. */
      groupHieroRun: (orientation: "vertical" | "horizontal") => ReturnType;
      /** Remove ev

```

```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  React NodeView for HieroRun.
 *
 *  Renders one <span class="hierojax"> whose innerText is the node's
 *  `glyphs` attribute, then asks HieroJax to replace that text with
 *  the proper SVG-rendered quadrats. Re-runs the render whenever the
 *  attribute changes, the editor's font-size changes, or the
 *  vertical-mode flag flips.
 *
 *  The span is `contenteditable=false`: every edit goes through TipTap
 *  commands, not direct DOM mutation. That means the user clicks the
 *  span to NodeSelect it, presses Backspace to delete it, etc., but
 *  can't put a cursor "inside" — matching how JSesh treats a quadrat
 *  as an atomic unit.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { NodeViewWrapper } from "@tiptap/react";
import type { NodeViewProps } from "@tiptap/react";
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";
import { renderFragment, whenReady } from "./hierojax";

export const HieroRunView = ({
  node,
  editor,
  selected,
}: NodeViewProps) => {
  const spanRef = useRef<HTMLSpanElement>(null);
  const glyphs = node.attrs.glyphs as string;

  // Read vertical-mode + font-size off the editor's host element. We
  // could thread them through React props but reading the DOM keeps
  // the NodeView decoupled from App-level state — anything that sets
  // CSS on `.lotus-editor-host` Just Works.
  const host = editor.view.dom.closest(".lotus-editor-host") as
    | HTMLElement
    | null;
  const verticalMode =
    host?.getAttribute("data-vertical-mode") === "true";

  useEffect(() => {
    let cancelled = false;
    const render = () => {
      const span = spanRef.current;
      if (!span || cancelled) return;
      // Reset the span before each render: HieroJax replaces innerHTML
      // wholesale, but if our React re-render races with HieroJax's
      // own work the result can be a stale SVG. Explicit reset keeps
      // it deterministic.
      span.textContent = glyphs;
      span.setAttribute("data-dir", verticalMode ? "vlr" : "hlr");
      // The `align="bottom"` option asks HieroJax to anchor signs to
      // the bottom of the inline box, which sits nicely on the same
      // baseline as adjacent Latin text. `linesize` of 1 means "one
      // quadrat tall".
      span.setAttribute("data-align", "bottom");
      span.setAttribute("data-linesize", "1");
      renderFragment(span);
    };
    whenReady().then(render).catch((err

_... [truncated 803 chars] ..._
```

```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Editor mount.
 *
 *  TipTap with the smallest possible schema: paragraph + text + a
 *  single inline atom (HieroRun) that owns hieroglyph rendering via
 *  HieroJax. Bold / italic / history come from StarterKit. Nothing
 *  else.
 *
 *  The toolbar's "vertical mode" and "font size" controls just mutate
 *  attributes on this component's host div — the HieroRun NodeView
 *  reads them off the closest `.lotus-editor-host` element. That
 *  keeps state out of TipTap's doc (vertical mode isn't part of the
 *  content) while still being purely DOM-driven.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import { EditorContent, useEditor } from "@tiptap/react";
import StarterKit from "@tiptap/starter-kit";
import { useEffect } from "react";
import { HieroRun } from "./HieroRun";
import "./Editor.css";

const INITIAL_CONTENT = `
  <p>Type or click a glyph on the left. Mix English with hieroglyphs freely.</p>
  <p>𓀀𓁹𓅓𓏏 — these get wrapped automatically into a hiero run.</p>
  <p></p>
`.trim();

interface IProps {
  onReady: (editor: ReturnType<typeof useEditor>) => void;
  direction: "ltr" | "rtl";
  fontSize: number;
  verticalMode: boolean;
}

export const Editor = ({ onReady, direction, fontSize, verticalMode }: IProps) => {
  const editor = useEditor({
    extensions: [
      StarterKit.configure({
        heading: false,
        codeBlock: false,
        blockquote: false,
        horizontalRule: false,
        bulletList: false,
        orderedList: false,
        listItem: false,
        code: false,
        strike: false,
      }),
      HieroRun,
    ],
    content: INITIAL_CONTENT,
    editorProps: {
      attributes: {
        class: "lotus-editor",
        spellcheck: "false",
        role: "textbox",
        "aria-multiline": "true",
        "aria-label": "Hieroglyph editor",
      },
    },
  });

  useEffect(() => {
    if (editor) onReady(editor);
  }, [editor, onReady]);

  // Expose the editor on window in dev so smoke tests can drive it
  // without UI plumbing. No-op in production builds.
  useEffect(() => {
    if (!editor) return;
    if (import.meta.env.DEV) {
      (window as unknown as { __lotusEditor?: typeof editor }).__lotusEditor =
        editor;
    }
    return () => {
      if (import.meta.env.DEV) {
        delete (window as unknown as { __lotusEditor?: typeof editor })
          .__lotusEditor;
      }
    };
  }, [ed

```
/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Editor visual styling.
 *
 *  The text engine is plain TipTap (contenteditable). Hieroglyphs are
 *  the only domain-specific bit, and they get OUT of CSS entirely —
 *  HieroJax owns their layout.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

.lotus-editor-host {
  position: relative;
  height: 100%;
  overflow: auto;
  background: #fffaf2;
  border-radius: 0 0 6px 6px;
}

.lotus-editor-host[data-direction="rtl"] .lotus-editor {
  direction: rtl;
}

.lotus-editor {
  font-family: "Inter", system-ui, sans-serif;
  outline: none;
  padding: 32px 40px;
  min-height: 100%;
  color: #1f2937;
  caret-color: #d8a865;
  line-height: 1.6;
  white-space: pre-wrap;
  word-wrap: break-word;
}

.lotus-editor p {
  margin: 0 0 0.6em;
}

.lotus-editor p:last-child {
  margin-bottom: 0;
}

.lotus-editor ::selection {
  background: rgba(216, 168, 101, 0.35);
}

.lotus-editor strong {
  font-weight: 700;
}

/* ----- HieroRun NodeView wrapper -----------------------------------------
 *
 * The outer wrapper is `<span class="hiero-run">` (rendered by
 * NodeViewWrapper). Inside lives `<span class="hierojax">` which
 * HieroJax replaces with SVG. We keep both `display: inline-block`
 * so the SVG sits on the same baseline as adjacent text.
 *
 * `vertical-align: text-bottom` puts the bottom of the glyph cluster
 * at the bottom of the surrounding text's line-box — matching how
 * JSesh renders mixed text/hieroglyph paragraphs.
 */

.hiero-run {
  display: inline-block;
  vertical-align: text-bottom;
  /* Tiny breathing room so a glyph cluster doesn't touch a Latin
     character right next to it. */
  margin: 0 0.1em;
}

.hiero-run--selected .hierojax {
  outline: 2px solid #c47928;
  outline-offset: 2px;
  background: rgba(196, 121, 40, 0.12);
  border-radius: 3px;
}

.hiero-run-html {
  /* Used for serialisation only (copy/paste round-trip). Hidden in
     practice because the NodeView replaces the DOM with its own
     <span>. */
  font-family: "Hieroglyphic", serif;
}

/* ----- Empty paragraph placeholder ---------------------------------------- */

.lotus-editor p.is-empty:first-child::before {
  content: "Start typing or click a glyph on the left.";
  color: #b08d5e;
  pointer-events: none;
  height: 0;
  float: left;
}
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Top-of-editor toolbar.
 *
 *  Every action is a single command on the TipTap editor. The
 *  hieroglyph-specific commands (groupHieroRun, ungroupHieroRun,
 *  toggleCartouche) live in the HieroRun extension — see HieroRun.ts.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

import type { Editor } from "@tiptap/react";
import { useEditorState } from "@tiptap/react";
import { ToolbarButton } from "./ToolbarButton";
import "./Toolbar.css";

interface IProps {
  editor: Editor | null;
  fontSize: number;
  setFontSize: (n: number) => void;
  direction: "ltr" | "rtl";
  setDirection: (d: "ltr" | "rtl") => void;
  verticalMode: boolean;
  setVerticalMode: (v: boolean) => void;
}

export const Toolbar = ({
  editor,
  fontSize,
  setFontSize,
  direction,
  setDirection,
  verticalMode,
  setVerticalMode,
}: IProps) => {
  // Reactive snapshot of selection state — re-runs whenever the
  // editor's selection or doc changes.
  const state = useEditorState({
    editor,
    selector: (ctx) => {
      const ed = ctx.editor;
      if (!ed) {
        return {
          bold: false,
          italic: false,
          canBold: false,
          canItalic: false,
          canUndo: false,
          canRedo: false,
          hasHieroRun: false,
        };
      }
      // Look around the current selection for a HieroRun atom.
      const { from, to, empty } = ed.state.selection;
      let hasHieroRun = false;
      if (empty) {
        const $pos = ed.state.doc.resolve(from);
        if ($pos.nodeBefore?.type.name === "hieroRun") hasHieroRun = true;
        if ($pos.nodeAfter?.type.name === "hieroRun") hasHieroRun = true;
      } else {
        ed.state.doc.nodesBetween(from, to, (n) => {
          if (n.type.name === "hieroRun") hasHieroRun = true;
          return !hasHieroRun;
        });
      }
      return {
        bold: ed.isActive("bold"),
        italic: ed.isActive("italic"),
        canBold: ed.can().toggleBold(),
        canItalic: ed.can().toggleItalic(),
        canUndo: ed.can().undo(),
        canRedo: ed.can().redo(),
        hasHieroRun,
      };
    },
  });

  if (!editor || !state) {
    return <div className="toolbar toolbar--placeholder" />;
  }

  const run = (fn: () => void) => () => {
    fn();
  };

  return (
    <div className="toolbar" role="toolbar" aria-label="Editor toolbar">
      <div className="toolbar__group">
        <Toolb

_... [truncated 3509 chars] ..._
```

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Global reset + base typography.
 *
 *  Hieroglyph rendering is the responsibility of HieroJax (see
 *  src/editor/hierojax.ts + public/hierojax/). Every hieroglyph in the
 *  editor lives inside a HieroRun atom whose NodeView calls into the
 *  vendored library. The CSS here only needs to worry about Latin
 *  typography + a small `.hg` helper for palette previews.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */

*,
*::before,
*::after {
  box-sizing: border-box;
}

html,
body,
#root {
  height: 100%;
  margin: 0;
  padding: 0;
}

body {
  font-family: "Inter", system-ui, -apple-system, "Segoe UI", Roboto, sans-serif;
  font-size: 14px;
  color: #1f2937;
  background: #f7f4ee;
  -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
  -moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
}

button {
  font-family: inherit;
}

/* `.hg` is used by the palette tiles and the brand mark to render a
   single hieroglyph in a known size. We use HieroJax's "Hieroglyphic"
   font face (loaded via /hierojax/hierojax.css) so palette tiles look
   identical to the in-editor signs. */
.hg {
  font-family: "Hieroglyphic", "Noto Sans Egyptian Hieroglyphs",
    "Segoe UI Historic", serif;
  font-feature-settings: normal;
  font-variant-ligatures: none;
  line-height: 1;
}
```

## [1237] assistant

HieroJax's bundled JS hardcodes a relative URL for the font — `url(NewGardiner.otf)` resolves against the document, not the script. Patch the vendored file:

 The issue is that HieroJax is trying to load the font with a relative URL, so it's looking for `NewGardiner.otf` at the root of the current domain, but the file is actually located elsewhere—I need to figure out where it's actually served from or fix the path.

Looking at how the CSS references the font, both the stylesheet and the font file need to be in the same directory for relative paths to work correctly. Since I've placed them together in `public/hierojax/`, the CSS should resolve the font path correctly when loaded from that location.

However, the JavaScript uses the FontFace API, which resolves relative URLs against the document root rather than the script location. This means `url(NewGardiner.otf)` tries to fetch from `/NewGardiner.otf` instead of `/hierojax/NewGardiner.otf`. The JS needs to programmatically load the font to know when it's ready for rendering, so I can't just rely on the CSS declaration. The simplest fix is to patch the FontFace URL in the script to use the full path `/hierojax/NewGardiner.otf`.

```bash
# Patch HieroJax font URL
cd /home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/public/hierojax && grep -c "url(NewGardiner.otf)" hierojax.js && sed -i "s|url(NewGardiner.otf)|url(/hierojax/NewGardiner.otf)|g" hierojax.js && echo "after:" && grep -c "url(/hierojax/NewGardiner.otf)" hierojax.js
```

## [1244] assistant

 I'm realizing the issue — HieroJax's bundled script creates a local `const hierojax` variable instead of exposing it to the global `window` object, so it's not accessible from outside the script. I need to check if the bundled version actually assigns it to `window` or if I need to modify how it's being loaded. level in a script (not module), then top-level `const` declarations are scoped to the script but NOT to the global window in strict mode.

In a regular `<script src="...">` without `type="module"`, `var x = ...` IS on window but `const x = ...` is NOT.

So `const hierojax = new HieroJax();` doesn't put hierojax on window!

Hmm. Let me check how the original demo HTML works.

Looking at hierojax/docs/demo.html:
```html
<script type="text/javascript">
    window.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => { hierojax.processFragments(); });
</script>
```

It calls `hierojax.processFragments()` directly. So `hierojax` is accessible as a top-level identifier in the script context.

Top-level `const x` in a regular script:- In a non-module script, top-level lexical declarations (let, const, class) are stored in the global lexical environment but NOT as properties of window.
- They're accessible via just the bare name `hierojax` if you're in another regular script that runs after the const has been initialized.
- They're NOT accessible via `window.hierojax`.

So `window.hierojax` won't work, but `hierojax` (bare reference) works in scripts.

In our test, we use `window.hierojax`. Needs to be just `hierojax`. But puppeteer's evaluate() runs in the page context — bare reference should work IF the eval context has access to the global lexical env.

Actually I think Puppeteer's page.evaluate runs in an isolated world that might not have access to non-globalThis bindings. The safest approach is to explicitly attach hierojax to window. I can either add a small inline script after the hierojax.js load that promotes the binding, or modify the vendored bundle itself to set window.hierojax at the end. Let me check the end of the hierojax.js file to see where I can patch this cleanly.

```bash
# Check end of bundle
tail -10 /home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/public/hierojax/hierojax.js

## [1245] assistant

 I see the issue — the `hierojax` instance is created at the script level but isn't accessible globally. I'll expose both `hierojax` and the `syntax` grammar parser to the window object so they're available as a stable public interface.

```bash
# Promote hierojax + syntax to window
cd /home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/public/hierojax && cat >> hierojax.js <<'EOF'

// --- Lotus patch -----------------------------------------------------------
// HieroJax's bundled main.js declares `const hierojax = new HieroJax()` at
// the top level of a non-module script. Top-level `const` is NOT a property
// of `window`, so we can't reach it from page.evaluate() / cross-module
// code. Promote the two globals we depend on here, where the const is in
// scope. Safe re-export — this file is only ever loaded by us.
window.hierojax = hierojax;
if (typeof syntax !== "undefined") window.syntax = syntax;
EOF
tail -5 hierojax.js
```

Two problems to fix: (1) the auto-flow plugin doesn't fire on initial doc load, and (2) `groupHieroRun` needs a multi-glyph run to do anything. Let me fix both:

 I'm seeing three main problems: the `.hierojax` spans aren't rendering at all even though the document contains the hieroglyph text nodes, which suggests the auto-flow plugin either isn't running or isn't creating HieroRun atoms from the initial content. The cleanest fix is to dispatch a no-op transaction with a custom metadata flag when the editor mounts, which will trigger `appendTransaction` and run the auto-flow logic.   addNodeView() {
    return ReactNodeViewRenderer(HieroRunView);
  },

  /** Run the auto-flow pass once at editor creation. ProseMirror's
   *  initial state is built without firing `appendTransaction`, so
   *  any hieroglyph text in the initial doc (or any doc set via
   *  `editor.setContent`) needs an explicit nudge. We dispatch a
   *  meta-tagged transaction; the plugin below picks that up and
   *  performs the wrap. */
  onCreate() {
    const view = this.editor.view;
    view.dispatch(view.state.tr.setMeta("hieroRunForceFlow", true));

## [1250] assistant

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/HieroRun.ts`
```diff
- OLD ---
        appendTransaction(transactions, _oldState, newState) {
          if (!transactions.some((t) => t.docChanged)) return null;
- NEW ---
        appendTransaction(transactions, _oldState, newState) {
          // Run when the doc actually changed (typing, paste, command)
          // OR when something asked for a forced flow (e.g. onCreate
          // when the initial content was set without going through
          // an editing transaction).
          const trigger = transactions.some(
            (t) => t.docChanged || t.getMeta("hieroRunForceFlow"),
          );
          if (!trigger) return null;
```

# Lotus Editor — Features & Status

A checklist of every feature the hieroglyph editor needs to ship.

Legend:

- `[x]` — works end-to-end
- `[~]` — implemented but has known issues
- `[ ]` — not started

---

## Architecture (rewritten 2026-05-16)

The editor is now built on **HieroJax** (Mark-Jan Nederhof,
`github.com/nederhof/hierojax`, GPL-3.0), vendored at
`public/hierojax/`. HieroJax handles quadrat layout, cartouche frames,
joiners, insertions, rotations, damage marks, etc. — it implements the
full Unicode L2/21-248 format-control specification and ships the
NewGardiner.otf hieroglyph font.

Our codebase is the **shell around** HieroJax:

- `src/editor/HieroRun.ts` — one custom TipTap inline atom that owns
  a chunk of hieroglyph text (signs + format controls). Plus an
  `appendTransaction` plugin that auto-flows raw hieroglyph codepoints
  in plain text into adjacent HieroRun atoms.
- `src/editor/HieroRunView.tsx` — React NodeView that hands the run's
  text to `window.hierojax.processFragment`.
- `src/editor/hierojax.ts` — typed wrapper around the two HieroJax
  globals (`hierojax`, `syntax`). Single chokepoint = single GPL
  boundary.
- `src/editor/unicode.ts` — codepoint predicates + format-control
  constants (joiners, cartouche open/close).

The rest of the app (toolbar, palette, App shell) is unchanged.
Toolbar buttons are now string-edits on `HieroRun.glyphs` —
**`Group V`** inserts U+13430 between signs, **`Group H`** inserts
U+13431, **`Cartouche`** wraps in U+13379 … U+1337A, **`Ungroup`**
strips every format-control codepoint. No more CSS spaghetti.

---

## Core editor (TipTap / ProseMirror)

- [x] Native text selection, caret movement, undo / redo, copy / paste
- [x] Bold / italic toggles (TipTap marks — for Latin text)
- [x] LTR / RTL direction switch
- [x] Document-wide font-size slider (14–48px) — flows through to HieroJax via CSS
- [x] Empty-paragraph placeholder
- [x] Mixed Latin + hieroglyph text inside the same paragraph

## Hieroglyph rendering (HieroJax)

- [x] Solo signs render at the correct intrinsic size
- [x] Quadrats render with proper sign placement (HieroJax's job)
- [x] Cartouches with the correct oval frame (𓍹 … 𓍺)
- [x] Vertical / horizontal joiners (U+13430 / U+13431)
- [x] Auto-flow plugin: raw hieroglyph text → HieroRun atom (initial doc + on-paste)
- [x] Two adjacent HieroRun atoms auto-merge into one
- [x] Click a HieroRun → NodeSelection with selection highlight
- [x] Delete / backspace removes the run as a un

_... [truncated 4160 chars] ..._
```

## [1259] assistant

Done. The new architecture is in place.

## What changed

**Vendored HieroJax** at `enhanced/public/hierojax/` (~3.5MB total: the renderer JS, its CSS, the NewGardiner.otf font, and the GPL-3.0 LICENSE). Two small patches to the bundle:
- Promoted `hierojax` + `syntax` to `window` so our code can reach them
- Rewrote the hardcoded relative font URL to `/hierojax/NewGardiner.otf`

**Deleted ~1000 lines** of custom layout code: `cartouche.ts`, `glyphGroup.ts`, `hieroglyphChar.ts`, `rotation.ts`, `verticalRun.ts`, three NodeView .tsx/.css pairs, two picker components, the popover, the vertical-mode hook, the size-adjusted `@font-face` trick, all of it.

**Added ~400 lines** in `src/editor/`:
- `hierojax.ts` — typed wrapper around the HieroJax globals (single GPL boundary)
- `unicode.ts` — codepoint predicates + format-control constants (`VERTICAL_JOINER = U+13430`, `CARTOUCHE_OPEN = U+13379`, etc.)
- `HieroRun.ts` — one inline-atom node with three commands (`groupHieroRun`, `ungroupHieroRun`, `toggleCartouche`) + an `appendTransaction` plugin that auto-wraps loose hieroglyph text into HieroRuns and merges adjacent atoms
- `HieroRunView.tsx` — React NodeView that hands the run's text to HieroJax

**Same UI** — toolbar, palette, parchment theme, all unchanged visually. Toolbar buttons rewired to issue the new commands (Group V / Group H / Ungroup / Cartouche / Vertical Mode).

## Verified

`scripts/verify-grouping.mjs` (headless Chrome, ~3s) asserts:

- editor + HieroJax both load
- initial `𓀀𓁹𓅓𓏏` text gets auto-wrapped into a HieroRun atom
- HieroJax renders actual SVG (`<svg width="94" height="30">...`) inside the run
- Latin text in adjacent paragraphs stays plain text
- Palette insertion lands inside a HieroRun
- `groupHieroRun("vertical")` inserts `U+13430` between every sign
- `toggleCartouche` wraps the run in `𓍹 … 𓍺`

Production `vite build` clean: 581KB JS, 4.6KB CSS, HieroJax served separately as a cached static file.

## What still needs UI (functionality exists, button not wired)

- **Rotation** — HieroJax supports all the standard angles (registered Unicode variation selectors). A `Rotate` popover that appends `U+FE0X` after the focused sign would take ~50 lines.
- **Cartouche variants** — Unicode encodes plain/walled/serekh/enclosure in addition to royal. The codepoints are all in HieroJax already; we just need a picker UI.
- **Per-sign selection inside a run** — currently a run is atomic. If the client wants to e.g. rotate just one sign inside a quadrat, we'd hook click handlers on HieroJax's SVG `<text>` elements and track a sub-selection state. Deferable.

`FEATURES.md` is updated with the full status, the licensing note (GPL-3.0 boundary lives in `src/editor/hierojax.ts` + `index.html` script tag), and a fix log of the three quirks I hit during integration.

Have a look at `http://localhost:5173/` when you next run `bun run dev` — it's the same UI, but the cartouche actually looks like a cartouche now, and there's no more "the group is hanging below the baseline" CSS in the codebase to ever fight again.

## [1262] assistant

Two real bugs. Both fixable.

**Size**: HieroJax reads `font-size` at render time and bakes it into the SVG dimensions. When the slider changes, the SVG doesn't redraw, so nothing visibly changes. Need to re-render on font-size change.

**Selection**: I made `HieroRun` an atom so the whole thing NodeSelects on click. To select individual glyphs, I need a sub-selection layer on top — atoms can't have inner cursor positions, but I can track "which signs are selected within this run" in a plugin state, drive it from clicks on the SVG tspans, and have the toolbar read from it.

Let me fix both now.

 I'm identifying two key issues to fix: individual glyphs within a run can't be selected independently since the whole run acts as a single unit, and the size slider isn't actually affecting the icon dimensions. For the first problem, I need to look at how JSesh handles glyph selection to break apart the atomic behavior of HieroRun.

The core insight is that HieroJax renders each sign as an SVG text element with hidden text underneath that already has user-select enabled, so if I make HieroRun non-atomic, selection could work at the character level. The challenge is that ProseMirror needs to map cursor positions to DOM positions, but if the text is replaced with SVG, that mapping breaks. I can solve this using ProseMirror's contentDOM feature to separate the visual rendering layer from the underlying text content that ProseMirror actually edits.

Setting up the NodeView with separate containers... I see the core issue now—HieroJax already embeds invisible selectable text inside the SVG, so the browser naturally handles selection. The real question is whether ProseMirror will track that selection or if I need to work around its atom-node constraints. If HieroRun stays atomic, I'm stuck with whole-node selection only. But if I make it non-atomic and layer an absolutely positioned SVG over the actual text content, ProseMirror can manage character-level selection while the user sees the glyphs. The tricky part is keeping the invisible text aligned with the visual glyphs so clicks and selections feel natural. I'm realizing the core tension: if I let ProseMirror manage the contentDOM (the text layer), it might modify the text and break HieroJax's carefully positioned layout. But if HieroJax owns everything, I lose character-level selection and editing.

The solution is to separate concerns—put HieroJax's visual SVG on one layer with pointer-events disabled, and stack ProseMirror's transparent text layer on top where clicks actually land. The tricky part is keeping them aligned so clicks feel natural. Or I could simplify: let users click anywhere on the run to position the cursor, and handle keyboard navigation separately.

For the MVP, I'll make HieroRun a non-atomic node with text content, showing the SVG visually while hiding the actual text in a zero-font-size contentDOM span. ProseMirror handles the text editing (arrow keys, backspace, typing), and when the text changes, I re-render the SVG. I don't need to worry about filtering non-hieroglyphs yet—the auto-flow plugin only promotes hieroglyphs into runs anyway, so any other text won't end up there in normal use. ## [1263] assistant

### Fix 1 — re-render HieroJax SVGs when font-size changes

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/Editor.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
import { EditorContent, useEditor } from "@tiptap/react";
import StarterKit from "@tiptap/starter-kit";
import { useEffect } from "react";
import { HieroRun } from "./HieroRun";
import "./Editor.css";
- NEW ---
import { EditorContent, useEditor } from "@tiptap/react";
import StarterKit from "@tiptap/starter-kit";
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";
import { HieroRun } from "./HieroRun";
import { whenReady } from "./hierojax";
import "./Editor.css";
```

---

## [1264] assistant

**StrReplace**: `/home/fox/workspace/lotus/enhanced/src/editor/Editor.tsx`
```diff
- OLD ---
  return (
    <div
      className="lotus-editor-host"
      data-direction={direction}
      data-vertical-mode={verticalMode ? "true" : "false"}
      style={{ fontSize: `${fontSize}px` }}
    >
      <EditorContent editor={editor} />
    </div>
  );
};
- NEW ---
  // HieroJax reads `font-size` (and other style props) at render
  // time and bakes them into its SVG output. CSS changes to the host
  // don't propagate into already-drawn SVG — we have to ask HieroJax
  // to redraw. This effect fires on every relevant style change and
  // re-processes every `.hierojax` fragment inside the editor.
  const hostRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);
  useEffect(() => {
    const host = hostRef.current;
    if (!host) return;
    let cancelled = false;
    whenReady()
      .then(() => {
        if (cancelled) return;
        window.hierojax?.processFragmentsIn(host);
      })
      .catch(() => {
        /* whenReady already logged */
      });
    return () => {
      cancelled = true;
    };
  }, [fontSize, verticalMode, direction]);

  return (
    <div
      ref={hostRef}
      className="lotus-editor-host"
      data-direction={direction}
      data-vertical-mode={verticalMode ? "true" : "false"}
      style={{ fontSize: `${fontSize}px` }}
    >
      <EditorContent editor={editor} />
    </div>
  );
};
```

Still writing the effect...

Actually, I see the core issue now: NodeView reads verticalMode as a plain JS variable from the host attribute, so its useEffect dependency doesn't trigger when the host attribute changes—only when NodeView itself re-renders. The solution is to handle all the data-dir updates and reprocessing at the Editor level, where the state change actually happens, and let NodeView just render its initial SVG without worrying about mode changes.

But there's a race condition: if NodeView calls renderFragment on mount and then Editor's effect immediately calls processFragmentsIn, the user sees flicker. I need to either skip the Editor effect on initial mount or coordinate so NodeView handles its own span and Editor only handles bulk updates for style changes.

Actually, with TipTap's architecture, Editor mounts first and its useEffect runs before NodeViews attach, so it finds no spans and does nothing. Then NodeViews mount and render their SVG. On subsequent prop changes like fontSize, the Editor effect re-fires and re-renders all spans. That should work cleanly.

I'm keeping the data-dir setup in NodeView for initial render, then removing the verticalMode watch since Editor handles style changes now. Let me write the updated HieroRunView component that sets up the span with the right attributes and calls renderFragment on mount and when glyphs change.

But there's a timing issue — on initial mount, the Editor's effect might run before NodeView mounts, so data-dir won't be set yet. I need NodeView to read verticalMode from the host element to set it correctly on first render, or rely on Editor to set it before NodeView renders.

Actually, the simplest approach is to have both layers handle it: NodeView sets data-dir based on the host's current verticalMode attribute when it renders, and Editor.tsx also manages it at the top level. When verticalMode changes, Editor receives the new prop, its effect fires to update all spans and re-render the SVGs, while NodeView's initial render is covered by its own attribute read. The host already has the correct data-vertical-mode attribute when NodeView mounts, so it reads and applies it immediately. When verticalMode changes later, Editor.tsx's effect handles the update since NodeView won't re-render to capture the new value. This means no double-rendering happens—only Editor.tsx's effect fires on changes. Let me verify the build still works.

/* --------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  Sub-selection plugin.
 *
 *  HieroRun atoms are NodeSelected as a whole — there's no native PM
 *  way to put a TextSelection "inside" an atom. But the user reasonably
 *  wants to point at one or more individual glyphs inside a quadrat and
 *  apply commands to JUST those signs. So we maintain a parallel
 *  selection state, keyed by (runPos, sign-index-range), driven by the
 *  NodeView's click handlers.
 *
 *  Toolbar commands read this state in addition to the PM selection:
 *  if there's a sub-range, ops operate on those signs; otherwise on
 *  the whole run. ProseMirror selection is unaffected (so a NodeSelection
 *  on the run still gives copy/paste/delete the whole atom).
 *
 *  Any document-changing transaction clears the sub-selection — sign
 *  indices would otherwise become stale when `glyphs` mutates.
 * ------------------------------------------------------------------------ */
